topics.title: Introduction to Robotics - Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Robotics - Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Robotics – The Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Robot Institute of America, 1979):**
> A **robot** is a *reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator* designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks.

A **robotic system** is an integrated assembly of:
- **Mechanical Structure** (links, joints, end-effector)
- **Actuators** (servo motors, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders)
- **Sensors** (encoders, vision, force/torque)
- **Controller** (microcontroller/PLC/industrial PC)
- **Power Supply & Software Stack**

## 1.2 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (Foundational Ethics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. A robot may not injure a human being, or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
> 2. A robot must obey the orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
> 3. A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.
> *(Added later — Zeroth Law: A robot may not harm humanity as a whole.)*

## 1.3 What is a "Degree of Freedom" (DOF)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is an *independent parameter* (translation or rotation along/around a single axis) required to completely specify the configuration of a mechanical system in space.

A rigid body in **3D space** possesses a maximum of **6 DOF**:
- **3 Translational DOF** (movement along $x$, $y$, $z$ axes)
- **3 Rotational DOF** (rotation about $x$, $y$, $z$ axes — called *roll*, *pitch*, *yaw*)

**Mathematically stated:**

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{rigid body, 3D}} = 3 \text{ (translations)} + 3 \text{ (rotations)} = 6
$$

## 1.4 The Intuitive Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine you are sitting at a desk and you want to bring a coffee cup to your lips. Count the independent motions:

| # | Body Part | Independent Motion | Type |
|---|-----------|--------------------|------|
| 1 | Shoulder | Pitch up/down | Rotational |
| 2 | Shoulder | Yaw left/right | Rotational |
| 3 | Shoulder | Roll | Rotational |
| 4 | Elbow | Flex/extend | Rotational |
| 5 | Forearm (pronation) | Rotate wrist-in/out | Rotational |
| 6 | Wrist | Pitch up/down | Rotational |
| 7 | Wrist | Yaw left/right | Rotational |

> **Total human-arm DOF ≈ 7** (highly dexterous!).

A typical **industrial robot arm** has **6 DOF** — just enough to position *and* orient its end-effector anywhere in its workspace. The *minimum* DOF required to position a wrist at any point in 3D space is **3**; the *minimum* to also orient the gripper is **6**.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:**
> $\text{DOF}_{\min} = 3$ for *positioning only*; $\text{DOF}_{\min} = 6$ for *positioning + orientation*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gruebler–Kutzbach Criterion (Mobility Formula)

The most important equation in this module. It computes the **mobility (M)** — i.e., the DOF of a planar or spatial **mechanism / linkage**, NOT of a single rigid body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gruebler–Kutzbach Mobility Formula:**
>
> $$
> M = d\,(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}\,(d - f_i)
> $$
>
> where:
> - $M$ = mobility / number of independent DOF
> - $d$ = number of independent generalized coordinates *per link* ( $= 3$ planar, $= 6$ spatial)
> - $n$ = total number of **links** (including ground)
> - $j$ = total number of **joints**
> - $f_i$ = number of constrained DOF removed by joint $i$

**Equivalently expanded:**

$$
M = d\,(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}\,(d - f_i) = d\,(n - 1) - j\,d + \sum_{i=1}^{j}f_i
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M = d(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i\,}
$$

## 2.2 Standard Joint Library (Memorize This Table)

| Joint Symbol | Joint Name | $f_i$ (DOF) | Constrained DOF | Motion Description |
|--------------|------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **R** | Revolute (Hinge) | $1$ | $5$ | Rotation about 1 axis |
| **P** | Prismatic (Sliding) | $1$ | $5$ | Translation along 1 axis |
| **H** | Helical (Screw) | $1$ | $5$ | Rotation + coupled translation |
| **C** | Cylindrical | $2$ | $4$ | Rotation + translation (same axis) |
| **S** | Spherical (Ball) | $3$ | $3$ | Rotation about 3 axes (no translation) |
| **E** | Planar | $3$ | $3$ | Translation in 2 axes + 1 rotation (in plane) |
| **U** | Universal | $2$ | $4$ | Rotation about 2 intersecting axes |

> [!NOTE]
> In **planar** mechanisms, the number of unconstrained DOF per link is $d = 3$. In **spatial** mechanisms, $d = 6$.

## 2.3 DOF of Common Robot Configurations

| Robot Type | DOF | Typical Joint Sequence | Use Case |
|------------|-----|------------------------|----------|
| Cartesian (PPP) | $3$ | 3 Prismatic | Pick & place, CNC |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | $3$ | 1 Revolute + 2 Prismatic | Assembly, machine tending |
| Spherical (RRP) | $3$ | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic | Welding, painting |
| Articulated (RRR) | $3$ | 3 Revolute | General assembly |
| SCARA (RRP) | $3$ | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic | PCB assembly |
| **6-DOF Articulated** | $6$ | RRR + RRR (shoulder/elbow/wrist) | Welding, machining |

## 2.4 The "Wrist" Sub-Problem

Industrial robots usually separate:
- **First 3 joints (Arm):** Position the wrist center
- **Last 3 joints (Wrist):** Orient the end-effector

> [!TIP]
> **Spherical wrist** = 3 revolute axes intersecting at a single point ⇒ 3 rotational DOF, zero translation between axes. This decouples position from orientation, simplifying kinematics.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Workspace analysis** – Number of DOF sets the dimensionality of the robot's *configuration space*.
- **Inverse Kinematics feasibility** – At least 6 DOF are required to solve position + orientation simultaneously.
- **Redundancy** – DOF $> 6$ for the task ⇒ *kinematically redundant* robot (e.g., 7-DOF KUKA LBR iiwa), useful for obstacle avoidance.
- **Mechatronic design** – DOF dictates the count of motors, encoders, and controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gruebler–Kutzbach Formula

Let us derive the formula from first principles to ensure full understanding.

### Step 1 — Total DOF Available

Consider a mechanism with $n$ rigid links *floating* in 3D space. Each link, in isolation, has $d = 6$ independent DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations). One link is designated as the **ground (frame)** and is fixed, so it contributes $0$ DOF.

$$
\text{Total available DOF} = d \cdot (n - 1)
$$

**Reason:** We exclude the ground link because it is stationary.

### Step 2 — Constraints Introduced by Each Joint

A joint between two links *removes* a certain number of DOF. For joint $i$ with $f_i$ remaining DOF, the number of DOF *removed* is:

$$
c_i = d - f_i
$$

### Step 3 — Total Constraints

$$
\text{Total constraints} = \sum_{i=1}^{j}\,(d - f_i)
$$

### Step 4 — Net DOF (Mobility)

$$
M = d\,(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}\,(d - f_i)
$$

This is the **Gruebler–Kutzbach** result.

### Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification

$$
M = d\,(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}\,(d - f_i)
$$

Expand the summation:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{j}\,(d - f_i) = j\cdot d - \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

Substitute back:

$$
M = d\,(n - 1) - j\cdot d + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M = d\,(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i\,}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 4-Bar Planar Linkage

A classic four-bar linkage has:
- 4 links (1 ground, 3 moving) $\Rightarrow n = 4$
- 4 revolute joints $\Rightarrow j = 4$, each $f_i = 1$
- Planar $\Rightarrow d = 3$

Apply formula:

$$
M = 3\,(4 - 1 - 4) + \sum_{i=1}^{4} 1
$$

$$
M = 3\,(-1) + 4
$$

$$
M = -3 + 4 = 1
$$

> **Result:** $M = 1$ — one independent DOF. As expected, a four-bar has *one* input crank that drives the entire mechanism.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — 6-DOF Spatial Industrial Robot

Configuration: 6 revolute joints, 7 links (1 ground + 6 arm links).

Parameters:
- $d = 6$ (spatial)
- $n = 7$
- $j = 6$, each $f_i = 1$

Apply formula:

$$
M = 6\,(7 - 1 - 6) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} 1
$$

$$
M = 6\,(0) + 6
$$

$$
M = 6
$$

> **Result:** $M = 6$ — confirms the 6-DOF articulated robot.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — SCARA Robot

SCARA: 2 revolute + 1 prismatic + 1 revolote (last for end-effector rotation), 5 links total.

- $d = 6$ (spatial)
- $n = 5$
- Joints: 2 revolute (wrist) + 1 prismatic (Z) + 1 revolute (theta) $\Rightarrow j = 4$

$$
M = 6\,(5 - 1 - 4) + (1 + 1 + 1 + 1)
$$

$$
M = 6\,(0) + 4
$$

$$
M = 4
$$

(SCARA commonly presented as 4-DOF: X-translation, Y-translation, Z-translation, Z-rotation).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Failure Cases of Gruebler

> [!WARNING]
> The formula gives **necessary** but not always **sufficient** conditions. Special cases:

| Condition | Meaning | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| $M = 0$ | **Structure** (statically determinate, no motion) | A rigid triangle |
| $M < 0$ | **Pre-loaded structure** — over-constrained, only moves if links yield | Welded truss |
| $M \ge 1$ but mechanism cannot move | **Redundant constraints / special geometry** | Parallelogram 4-bar (Gruebler gives $M = 1$ but it actually has $2$ passive DOF and $1$ active, also called *special cases* like Grashof condition) |

> [!TIP]
> Always check the *Grashof condition* for planar four-bars as a follow-up step (not strictly required for KTU module 1, but excellent for full credit).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Robot Joint Taxonomy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Joints]:::root
    A --> B[Revolute R]:::rot
    A --> C[Prismatic P]:::trans
    A --> D[Cylindrical C]:::mixed
    A --> E[Spherical S]:::rot
    A --> F[Helical H]:::mixed
    A --> G[Universal U]:::rot
    A --> H[Planar E]:::mixed
    B --> B1[1 DOF Rotation]
    C --> C1[1 DOF Translation]
    D --> D1[2 DOF R plus T]
    E --> E1[3 DOF Rotation]
    F --> F1[1 DOF Screw Motion]
    G --> G1[2 DOF Rotation]
    H --> H1[3 DOF In-Plane]
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rot fill:#c5e0b4,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef trans fill:#fbe5d6,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef mixed fill:#fff2cc,color:#000,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a 6-DOF Articulated Robot

```mermaid
graph LR
    Base[Base Link]:::base
    J1[Shoulder Revolute 1]:::joint
    L1[Upper Arm Link]:::link
    J2[Shoulder Revolute 2]:::joint
    L2[Forearm Link]:::link
    J3[Elbow Revolute]:::joint
    L3[Wrist Link 1]:::link
    J4[Wrist Revolute 4]:::joint
    L4[Wrist Link 2]:::link
    J5[Wrist Revolute 5]:::joint
    L5[Wrist Link 3]:::link
    J6[Tool Revolute 6]:::joint
    Tool[End Effector Tool]:::tool
    Base --- J1 --- L1 --- J2 --- L2 --- J3 --- L3 --- J4 --- L4 --- J5 --- L5 --- J6 --- Tool
    classDef base fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef joint fill:#ed7d31,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef link fill:#a9d18e,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef tool fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    PS[Power Supply]:::pwr
    CTRL[Controller CPU]:::ctrl
    SEN[Sensors Encoders]:::sens
    ACT[Actuators Motors]:::act
    MECH[Mechanical Structure Links and Joints]:::mech
    TOOL[End Effector Tool]:::tool
    TSK[Task Execution]:::task
    PS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT
    SEN --> CTRL
    ACT --> MECH
    MECH --> TOOL
    TOOL --> TSK
    TSK -->|Feedback| SEN
    classDef pwr fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef sens fill:#bf9000,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef act fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef mech fill:#404040,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef tool fill:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef task fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — DOF Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Task requires orientation?}:::q
    Q1 -->|NO| A1[Use 3 DOF Positioning Arm]:::ans
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Obstacle Avoidance Needed?}:::q
    Q2 -->|NO| A2[Use 6 DOF Standard Arm]:::ans
    Q2 -->|YES| A3[Use 7 plus DOF Redundant Arm]:::ans
    classDef q fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ans fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Degree of Freedom of a robot. State the minimum number of DOF required to position and orient an object in 3D space.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is the number of independent parameters (translations or rotations) required to completely specify the configuration of a robot's end-effector in space. A rigid body in 3D space has a maximum of 6 DOF — 3 translations along $x$, $y$, $z$ and 3 rotations about $x$, $y$, $z$ axes.

- **Positioning only** (reaching a point) — minimum **3 DOF**
- **Positioning + Orientation** (placing a tool in a specific pose) — minimum **6 DOF**

> *(3 marks — full credit)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P) joints. State one example use of each in industrial robots.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Revolute (R) | Prismatic (P) |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Motion | Rotation about a fixed axis | Linear translation along an axis |
| DOF | 1 | 1 |
| Symbol | Circular hinge | Sliding block |
| Example | Shoulder/elbow joint of an articulated robot | Cartesian gantry's linear guide |

> *(3 marks — full credit)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer **ONE** of two alternatives. Each alternative is divided into (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the anatomy of a 6-DOF articulated robot. Label all joints and links. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **6-DOF articulated robot** resembles a human arm. Its structure (from base to tool) is:

$$
\text{Base} \rightarrow J_1 \rightarrow L_1 \rightarrow J_2 \rightarrow L_2 \rightarrow J_3 \rightarrow L_3 \rightarrow J_4 \rightarrow L_4 \rightarrow J_5 \rightarrow L_5 \rightarrow J_6 \rightarrow \text{End-Effector}
$$

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Base (Link 0)** | Fixed pedestal, mounted to floor. |
| **Link 1 / $J_1$** | Waist rotation — rotates the whole arm about a vertical axis. |
| **Link 2 / $J_2$** | Shoulder pitch — raises/lowers the upper arm. |
| **Link 3 / $J_3$** | Elbow pitch — folds/extends the forearm. |
| **Link 4 / $J_4$** | Wrist roll — rotates the wrist. |
| **Link 5 / $J_5$** | Wrist pitch — bends the wrist. |
| **Link 6 / $J_6$** | Tool roll — rotates the end-effector. |
| **End-Effector** | Gripper / welding torch / paint nozzle mounted on Link 6. |

The first 3 joints (J1, J2, J3) form the **arm** (positioning the wrist centre). The last 3 joints (J4, J5, J6) form the **spherical wrist** (orienting the tool). All 6 are revolute, giving 6 independent DOF.

*(Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.2 for a labeled diagram.)*

**(b)** Using the Gruebler–Kutzbach criterion, compute the mobility of a **5-bar planar mechanism** with all revolute joints, where link 1 is the ground. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- $n = 5$ links (including ground)
- $j = 5$ revolute joints, each $f_i = 1$
- Planar mechanism $\Rightarrow d = 3$

Apply formula:

$$
M = d\,(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

Substitute values:

$$
M = 3\,(5 - 1 - 5) + (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1)
$$

$$
M = 3\,(-1) + 5
$$

$$
M = -3 + 5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M = 2\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The mechanism has **2 independent inputs** (e.g., two driven cranks). It is a *2-DOF five-bar linkage* — the famous structure used in parallel manipulators and dynamic walking robots.

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define the following with a simple diagram: (i) Revolute joint, (ii) Prismatic joint, (iii) Spherical joint, (iv) Cylindrical joint. State the DOF of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Joint | Symbol | DOF | Diagram Description | Motion |
|-------|--------|-----|---------------------|--------|
| **Revolute (R)** | ⊙ | 1 | Two links connected by a pin | Rotation about a fixed axis |
| **Prismatic (P)** | ⊞ | 1 | A block sliding inside a channel | Linear translation along one axis |
| **Spherical (S)** | ⚽ | 3 | A ball inside a socket | Rotation about 3 axes intersecting at a point |
| **Cylindrical (C)** | ⬭ | 2 | A rod sliding + rotating in a sleeve | Translation along axis + rotation about same axis |

> **DOF summary:** R = 1, P = 1, S = 3, C = 2. *(+1 mark for the diagram, 6 marks for definitions and table.)*

**(b)** A **spatial robotic wrist** is constructed from 3 revolute joints whose axes intersect at a single point. The wrist is connected to a 3-revolute arm by a 1-DOF revolute coupling. Compute the total mobility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Given (treating the *arm* and *wrist* as one integrated linkage):

- Arm: 3 revolute joints + 3 moving links
- Wrist: 3 revolute joints + 3 moving links
- Total moving links: $n - 1 = 6 \Rightarrow n = 7$ (including ground)
- Total joints: $j = 6$ (all revolute, $f_i = 1$)
- Spatial mechanism: $d = 6$

Apply Gruebler–Kutzbach:

$$
M = d\,(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

$$
M = 6\,(7 - 1 - 6) + (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1)
$$

$$
M = 6\,(0) + 6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M = 6\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The robot is a standard **6-DOF articulated manipulator** — it can position the wrist centre anywhere in its 3D workspace and orient the end-effector with arbitrary roll, pitch, yaw.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link** — most students use $n$ as the *moving* links only, leading to $M = 0$. **Always count the ground as a link.**
> 2. **Confusing $d$** — for *planar* problems use $d = 3$; for *spatial* use $d = 6$. The question will explicitly say "spatial/3D" or "planar/2D".
> 3. **Joint symbol confusion** — Helical (H) and Revolute (R) both have $f_i = 1$ but are *not* the same; mixing them up will cost marks in description.
> 4. **Skipping units & drawing** — for 7-mark descriptive answers, a labeled sketch carries **at least 2 marks**; never omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Definition of DOF** — independent parameter (translation or rotation).
> - **Rigid body in 3D** — has **6 DOF** (3T + 3R).
> - **Minimum DOF** — 3 for position, 6 for position + orientation.
> - **Gruebler–Kutzbach formula:**
> $$M = d\,(n - 1 - j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i$$
> - **Joint DOF values:** R = 1, P = 1, C = 2, S = 3, U = 2, E = 3, H = 1.
> - **Planar** problems: $d = 3$. **Spatial** problems: $d = 6$.
> - **Count ground as a link** (this is the #1 mistake).
> - **6-DOF articulated robot** = 6 revolute joints = universal positioner.
> - **Redundant robot** = DOF $> 6$ (e.g., 7-DOF KUKA LBR).
> - **Spherical wrist** = 3 revolute axes intersecting at one point ⇒ decouples position from orientation.
> - **Cartesian / Cylindrical / Spherical / SCARA** = 3-DOF positioning arms.
> - Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** for any 7-mark descriptive sub-part.
> - State the **interpretation** of the final $M$ value (positioning, orientation, redundancy, structure).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Types - Manipulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Types - Manipulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Standard)

According to the **ISO 8373:2021** standard adopted in the **KTU 2024 Scheme Robotics syllabus**, an **industrial robot manipulator** is defined as:

> An **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator** programmable in three or more axes (degrees of freedom), which may be either fixed in place or mobile, intended for use in industrial automation applications.

A **manipulator** is the **mechanical arm subsystem** of a robot consisting of a series of rigid **links** interconnected by **joints**, whose primary function is to position and orient an **end-effector** (gripper, tool, or sensor) within a defined three-dimensional workspace.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine your own **human arm** reaching for a coffee cup on a table:

| Human Arm Component | Robot Manipulator Equivalent |
|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Shoulder (ball-and-socket) | Base joint (J1) — provides rotation/swivel |
| Upper arm (humerus bone) | Link 1 — rigid mechanical segment |
| Elbow (hinge) | Elbow joint (J2) — provides bending |
| Forearm (radius/ulna) | Link 2 |
| Wrist (complex joint) | Wrist assembly (J4, J5, J6) |
| Palm + Fingers | End-effector / Gripper |

Just as your arm has **7 degrees of freedom** (3 at shoulder, 1 at elbow, 3 at wrist), an industrial manipulator is engineered with a specific number of **DOFs (Degrees of Freedom)** to position and orient its tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every manipulator classification question is built around three foundational axes — (1) **Joint type** (P or R), (2) **Number of DOF**, and (3) **Workspace geometry**. Master this trio first.

### 1.3 Primary Joint Types (KTU Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **P-Joint (Prismatic):** Provides **linear sliding** translation along an axis. Example: a hydraulic piston.
> 
> **R-Joint (Revolute):** Provides **rotational** motion about an axis. Example: a motor shaft.

A manipulator's kinematic shorthand uses the letters **P** and **R** to describe the joint sequence from base to wrist. For instance, a **R-R-P** manipulator has two revolute joints followed by one prismatic joint.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Canonical Manipulator Configurations

KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **five fundamental manipulator geometries** — each defined by its joint arrangement and the shape of the workspace it can reach.

#### 2.1.1 Cartesian (Gantry) Manipulator — **P-P-P**

- **Joint sequence:** 3 prismatic joints along mutually perpendicular axes (X, Y, Z).
- **Workspace geometry:** A **rectangular cuboid** (box-shaped).
- **DOF at the main arm:** 3.
- **Real-world analogy:** A 3D printer bed or overhead crane.
- **Strengths:** Highest positional accuracy; simple kinematics; easy to visualize.
- **Weaknesses:** Poor reach into enclosed spaces; large footprint; expensive for large volumes.

#### 2.1.2 Cylindrical Manipulator — **R-P-P**

- **Joint sequence:** 1 revolute (base rotation about vertical Z-axis) + 2 prismatic (vertical lift + horizontal radial reach).
- **Workspace geometry:** A **hollow cylinder** (annular).
- **DOF at the main arm:** 3.
- **Real-world analogy:** A forklift's mast or a turret-mounted extendable arm.
- **Applications:** Assembly of rotational parts, machine tending.

#### 2.1.3 Spherical (Polar) Manipulator — **R-R-P**

- **Joint sequence:** 1 revolute (base) + 1 revolute (shoulder pitch) + 1 prismatic (radial extension).
- **Workspace geometry:** A portion of a **sphere** (spherical sector).
- **DOF at the main arm:** 3.
- **Real-world analogy:** A tank gun turret or a crane on a naval ship.
- **Applications:** Heavy material handling, forging, die casting.

#### 2.1.4 Articulated (Revolute / Anthropomorphic) Manipulator — **R-R-R**

- **Joint sequence:** 3 revolute joints — base rotation, shoulder pitch, elbow pitch.
- **Workspace geometry:** A **sphere-like** envelope (sometimes called "doughnut-shaped" with a hollow center near the base).
- **DOF at the main arm:** 3.
- **Real-world analogy:** A human shoulder–elbow–wrist reach.
- **Applications:** Welding, painting, sealing, assembly (most common in automotive industry). **Examples:** ABB IRB, KUKA KR series, FANUC R-2000.

#### 2.1.5 SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) — **R-R-P**

- **Joint sequence:** 2 revolute joints in the horizontal plane + 1 prismatic joint for vertical insertion.
- **Workspace geometry:** A **circular cylindrical** sector.
- **DOF at the main arm:** 3.
- **Distinctive feature:** **Compliant** in X-Y plane (good for peg-in-hole insertion) but **rigid** along Z (strong vertical pushing).
- **Applications:** Pick-and-place, PCB assembly, small-parts insertion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wrist Sub-assembly (J4, J5, J6):** Every industrial manipulator appends a **3-DOF spherical wrist** to the end of the main arm, giving **3 additional DOFs for orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw). Total DOF = 3 (position) + 3 (orientation) = **6 DOF**, the standard for general-purpose manipulation.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 1 manipulator problems. Memorize the column entries and the equations — they appear verbatim in KTU university papers.

| Parameter | Symbol | Equation / Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Degrees of Freedom | $n$ | $n = \sum f_i$ where $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$ | Industrial manipulators: **6 DOF** |
| Grübler–Kutzbach DOF (planar) | $n$ | $n = 3(L-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | — |
| Grübler–Kutzbach DOF (spatial) | $n$ | $n = 6(L-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$ | — |
| Link count | $L$ | Number of rigid bodies in mechanism | Manipulator: typically **6–7** |
| Revolute joint | R | 1 rotational DOF | Angle in **radians** or **degrees** |
| Prismatic joint | P | 1 translational DOF | Displacement in **mm** or **m** |
| Reach (cylindrical) | $R_{max}$ | $R_{max} = d_1 + d_2$ | sum of two prismatic extents |
| Reach (spherical) | $R_{max}$ | $R_{max} = l_1 + d_3$ | upper arm + radial extension |
| Workspace volume (cartesian) | $V$ | $V = a \times b \times c$ | $a,b,c$ are X, Y, Z travels (m³) |
| Workspace volume (cylindrical) | $V$ | $V = \pi (R_{out}^2 - R_{in}^2) h$ | $R_{in}$ = inner blind zone radius |
| Workspace volume (spherical) | $V$ | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3$ (sector) | full sphere if unrestricted |
| Position vector | $\mathbf{p}$ | $\mathbf{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ | Cartesian coordinates (m) |
| Orientation matrix | $\mathbf{R}$ | $3 \times 3$ SO(3) rotation matrix | Unitless |
| Homogeneous transform | $\mathbf{H}$ | $\mathbf{H} \in SE(3) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Escape Rule:** In KTU answer scripts, always write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. In our markdown tables, the same escape is used to keep the table parser stable.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why Manipulator Classification Matters

The **selection of manipulator type is a capital investment decision** in any manufacturing cell. The classification directly determines:

1. **Cycle time** — Cartesian and SCARA are fastest for pick-and-place; articulated is slower but more dexterous.
2. **Footprint** — Cartesian requires the largest ceiling/floor area; articulated needs the least.
3. **Accuracy** — Cartesian achieves $\pm 0.025$ mm; SCARA achieves $\pm 0.01$ mm; articulated typically $\pm 0.05$ mm.
4. **Reachability into cavities** — Articulated > Spherical > Cylindrical > Cartesian.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU viva questions, when asked *"Which manipulator would you choose for welding a car chassis?"*, the expected answer is the **Articulated (R-R-R) manipulator** because it offers maximum dexterity in tight body-cavity regions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a Standard 6-Axis Articulated Manipulator via Grübler–Kutzbach

The **Grübler–Kutzbach criterion** (also called the Kutzbach mobility formula) computes the number of degrees of freedom $n$ of a mechanism. For a **spatial closed-loop chain** it is:

$$
n = 6(L-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5
$$

For a **spatial open-chain** (which is what every serial manipulator is), the formula simplifies because there is **only one ground link** and no closed loops, so $J$ is the number of **single-DOF joints** (R or P).

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-chain simplification:** A serial manipulator has $L$ links (including the ground), $n$ single-DOF joints, and the chain is open. The total mobility equals the sum of joint freedoms.

Let us apply this to a **standard 6-DOF articulated manipulator** (R-R-R at the arm + R-R-R at the wrist).

**Step 1 — Count the links:**

$$
L = 1 \text{ (ground/base)} + 3 \text{ (arm links)} + 3 \text{ (wrist links)} = 7
$$

Each link is a single rigid body, so we have 7 links.

**Step 2 — Count the joints:**

The standard configuration has:

- 1 revolute joint at the base (waist rotation).
- 1 revolute joint at the shoulder.
- 1 revolute joint at the elbow.
- 3 revolute joints forming the spherical wrist (J4, J5, J6).

$$
J = J_1 = 6 \text{ revolute joints, all single-DOF}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the open-chain formula:**

For a serial open spatial chain, mobility $n$ = number of joint freedoms:

$$
n = \sum_{i=1}^{J} f_i = 6 \times 1 = 6
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the closed-loop form:**

Treat the open chain as a "mechanism" with 7 links. Apply the closed-loop formula with $J_2 = J_3 = J_4 = J_5 = 0$:

$$
n = 6(L-1) - 5J_1 = 6(7-1) - 5(6)
$$

$$
n = 6 \times 6 - 30 = 36 - 30
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 6 \text{ DOF}}
$$

The result matches: a standard 6-axis articulated manipulator has **6 degrees of freedom**, confirming the analytical rigour of the Grübler–Kutzbach criterion.

### 3.2 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar R-R Manipulator (Simplified)

The **forward kinematics** problem: given joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$, find the end-effector position $(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Assign the link parameters:**

Let the manipulator have two links of lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, with joint angles $\theta_1$ (base) and $\theta_2$ (elbow) measured from the horizontal.

**Step 2 — Write the position of joint 2 (elbow):**

$$
x_2 = l_1 \cos \theta_1
$$

$$
y_2 = l_1 \sin \theta_1
$$

**Step 3 — Write the position of the end-effector (tip):**

The end-effector is at distance $l_2$ from joint 2 along the direction $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$:

$$
x_{ee} = l_1 \cos \theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y_{ee} = l_1 \sin \theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the homogeneous transformation $SE(2)$ formulation:**

$$
\mathbf{H}_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \phantom{-}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the reach (radial distance from base):**

$$
r^2 = x_{ee}^2 + y_{ee}^2
$$

Expanding:

$$
r^2 = (l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2 + (l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2
$$

Using the identity $\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha = 1$ and the cosine subtraction rule $\cos(A-B)$:

$$
r^2 = l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 6 — Derive the workspace bounds:**

The maximum reach is when $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended):

$$
\boxed{r_{max} = l_1 + l_2}
$$

The minimum reach (the "dead-zone" or unreachable disk) is when $\theta_2 = 180^\circ$ (arm folded back on itself):

$$
\boxed{r_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert}
$$

This proves analytically that the **workspace of an R-R planar manipulator is an annulus** with inner radius $\vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$ and outer radius $l_1 + l_2$, with no reachable points in the central disk.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Manipulator Types

| Property | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) | SCARA (RRP) |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Main arm DOF | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| Joint sequence | P-P-P | R-P-P | R-R-P | R-R-R | R-R-P |
| Workspace shape | Rectangular cuboid | Hollow cylinder | Spherical sector | Spherical with cavity | Cylindrical sector |
| Reach formula | $V = abc$ | $V = \pi h(R_{out}^2 - R_{in}^2)$ | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3$ | $V \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 - V_{blind}$ | $V = \pi h R^2$ |
| Best for | Pick & place, CNC loading | Machine tending | Heavy payloads | Welding, painting | PCB assembly |
| Typical payload | Up to 250 kg | Up to 1000 kg | Up to 2000 kg | Up to 1000 kg | Up to 20 kg |
| Typical accuracy | $\pm 0.025$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.01$ mm |
| Relative cost | Medium–High | Medium | Medium–High | Medium | Low–Medium |


> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the architecture diagram:** The controller sends **trajectory commands** to each servo drive, which energise the **motors** mounted at each joint. **Optical encoders** at each motor close the **position feedback loop**, enabling precise joint-angle control. The **links** are sequential rigid bodies transmitting motion from the base to the **end-effector**. This entire assembly constitutes the **manipulator** as defined in Module 1 of PCADT502.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define the term "Degrees of Freedom (DOF)" as applied to a robot manipulator. Mention the standard DOF count of a general-purpose industrial manipulator.*

**Model Answer:**

Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a manipulator is the **number of independent parameters** required to **completely specify the configuration** (position and orientation) of the end-effector in three-dimensional space. Each joint contributes one DOF — a revolute (R) joint provides one rotational DOF, and a prismatic (P) joint provides one translational DOF.

A **standard general-purpose industrial manipulator** is designed with **6 DOF**: 3 DOF for **positioning** the end-effector at any point in 3D space (X, Y, Z) and 3 DOF for **orienting** the tool in roll, pitch, and yaw. This 6-DOF arrangement allows the end-effector to reach any pose within the workspace.

> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for R/P joint, 1 mark for 6 DOF breakdown)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between a Revolute (R) joint and a Prismatic (P) joint with one suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Revolute (R) Joint | Prismatic (P) Joint |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Motion type | Rotational about a fixed axis | Linear translation along an axis |
| Mathematical variable | Angle $\theta$ (radians or degrees) | Displacement $d$ (mm or m) |
| Variable range | Typically $0$ to $2\pi$ (or limited by limits) | Limited by physical stroke length |
| Drive example | Servo motor, stepper motor, harmonic drive | Lead screw, ball screw, linear actuator |
| Real-world example | Elbow joint of a human arm | Hydraulic ram of a backhoe |
| Robot example | Joint of an ABB IRB articulated arm | Linear track of a Cartesian gantry |

> *(3 marks: 1.5 marks for differences, 1.5 marks for examples)*
