topics.title: Ethics in Robotics - Three Laws and Applications of Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethics in Robotics - Three Laws and Applications of Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Ethics in Robotics?

**Ethics in Robotics** is the branch of applied ethics that systematically examines the moral obligations and behavioural boundaries governing the design, construction, deployment, and operation of robots and intelligent autonomous systems. It addresses the principles of **beneficence** (do good), **non-maleficence** (do no harm), **autonomy**, and **justice** as applied to non-human intelligent agents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> Robotics ethics is the interdisciplinary study that formulates normative rules, moral codes, and safety laws to ensure that robots and AI-driven systems act in a manner that is safe, transparent, accountable, and aligned with human welfare throughout their operational lifecycle.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Robot (KTU / ISO Reference)

According to **ISO 8373:2021** (the international standard adopted in KTU syllabi), a robot is defined as:

> *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | **DOF** | Independent axes of motion (≥ 3 for manipulator) |
| Payload | **P (kg)** | Maximum load capacity at wrist |
| Reach | **R (m)** | Maximum radial distance from base |
| Repeatability | **±r (mm)** | Position return accuracy |
| Workspace | **W** | Total reachable volume |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Knights Code" Mental Model

Imagine a medieval knight who has been given a strict **Code of Conduct** before being allowed to carry a sword. The Code says: *"Never harm an innocent person, obey your king, and protect yourself only when it doesn't break the first two rules."* Asimov's Three Laws work in exactly the same way — they are a **hierarchical moral constitution** etched into every robot's decision-making core, ensuring that a machine's strength is always subordinated to human safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:**
> The Three Laws are not actual engineering control laws like Newton's laws. They are *philosophical* constraints that have shaped modern **AI safety frameworks**, **autonomous vehicle ethics** (e.g., the trolley problem), and **Asimov's Law Zero** used in **military robotics ROE** (Rules of Engagement).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (1942)

Isaac Asimov, in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"* (published in *Astounding Science Fiction*), formulated the **Three Laws of Robotics** as a fictional ethical device. They have since become the **de-facto pedagogical foundation** for all robotics ethics modules across global B.Tech curricula, including KTU.

### Hierarchical Order of the Three Laws

The Three Laws are **strictly hierarchical** — a higher-priority law **always overrides** a lower-priority law, and the robot must continuously evaluate the precedence in real-time.

---

### **Law 1 — The Non-Injury Law (Highest Priority)**
> *"A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm."*

- **Keywords:** *May not*, *injure*, *through inaction*.
- **Scope:** Covers both **active harm** (a robot punching) and **passive harm** (a robot watching a person fall into a pit without helping).
- **Engineering Implication:** This mandates the integration of **force-limited actuators**, **collision-detection sensors**, **safe-stop circuits (Category 0/1/2 stops per ISO 13849)**, and **real-time ethics engines** in robot controllers.

---

### **Law 2 — The Obedience Law (Intermediate Priority)**
> *"A robot must obey the orders given by it by human beings except where such orders would conflict with the First Law."*

- **Keywords:** *Must obey*, *except where conflict*.
- **Scope:** Subordinates robot behaviour to human commands **subject to Law 1**.
- **Conflict Resolution:** If a human orders a robot to push another human off a cliff, **Law 1 overrides Law 2** — the robot must refuse.
- **Modern Analog:** Comparable to **ISO 10218 (Industrial robot safety)** rules where operator commands can be overridden by safety-rated monitored stops.

---

### **Law 3 — The Self-Preservation Law (Lowest Priority)**
> *"A robot must protect its own existence as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law."*

- **Keywords:** *Protect own existence*, *as long as*.
- **Scope:** The robot may defend itself **only if** doing so does not harm humans (Law 1) and does not violate a human order (Law 2).
- **Engineering Implication:** Encourages **self-diagnostic systems**, **battery management**, and **graceful degradation** protocols.

---

### **Law 0 — The Zeroth Law (Added 1985 in *Robots and Empire*)**
> *"A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm."*

- **Why added:** Asimov realised the three laws were too *individual-centric*. If saving one human endangers humanity as a whole, the robot must allow the harm.
- **Conflict:** A robot may now **permit harm to a specific human** if it benefits humanity at large (e.g., not intervening in a war that will lead to greater long-term peace).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While robotics ethics is largely conceptual, the following engineering parameters and equations are commonly tested alongside this topic in **PCADT502 Module 1**:

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom (Kutzbach) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless | Used in robot arm design |
| Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V_w = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$ | m³ | Reach envelope |
| Payload Index | $PI = \frac{P}{m_{robot}}$ | ratio | Industrial classification |
| Safety Integrity Level | $\text{SIL} = f(\text{PFH}_d)$ | level 1–4 | ISO 13849-1 reference |
| Repeatability | $\pm r$ | mm | KTU board numerical problems |
| Lagrange Multiplier (for safe motion) | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + \tau_{safe}$ | N·m | Constrained control law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Apply Asimov's Laws to scenario X"*, always state the **Law hierarchy first**, then **identify the conflict**, then **resolve the conflict by referring to the higher-priority law**. This is the KTU board examiner's expected answer pattern.

## 2.3 Applications of Robots (Industry-wise Classification)

The following classification is the **standard KTU-recommended taxonomy** for industrial / service robotics:

| Domain | Application | Robot Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Welding, painting, assembly | Articulated / SCARA | Automotive assembly lines (e.g., ABB IRB series) |
| **Medical** | Surgery, rehabilitation | Collaborative / Surgical | Da Vinci Surgical System, exoskeletons |
| **Space** | Planetary exploration | Mobile rover | NASA Perseverance, ISRO Pragyan |
| **Defence / Military** | Bomb disposal, surveillance | Unmanned ground/aerial | DRDO Daksh, PackBot |
| **Domestic** | Cleaning, lawn mowing | Mobile service | iRobot Roomba, Mi Robot |
| **Underwater / Subsea** | Pipeline inspection, oceanography | ROV / AUV | Saipem underwater welders |
| **Agriculture** | Harvesting, spraying | Mobile manipulator | John Deere autonomous tractors |
| **Hazardous** | Nuclear cleanup, fire-fighting | Teleoperated | Fukushima cleanup robots |
| **Entertainment** | Theme parks, toys | Animatronic / humanoid | Disney animatronics, ASIMO |
| **Logistics** | Warehouse picking, delivery | AGV / AMR | Amazon Kiva, Starship delivery bots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Students should be able to **classify any given robot** into the above domains and **justify the choice** with at least one ethical/safety consideration. The new 2024 scheme also explicitly lists **collaborative robots (cobots)** as a high-weightage area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Logical Resolution Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Resolution of Asimov's Law Conflicts

Since the Three Laws form a **strictly ordered hierarchy**, the following step-by-step logical resolution framework is the standard answer pattern expected by KTU examiners.

### Step-by-Step Conflict Resolution Algorithm

**Step 1 — Identify the proposed action `A` of the robot.**

The robot's controller proposes a discrete action $A \in \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ based on its planning module. This action set is the candidate behavioural output.

**Step 2 — Evaluate the action against Law 1 (Non-Injury).**

Check whether executing action $A$ causes, directly or indirectly, harm to any human. Formally:

$$
\text{Law1}(A) = \begin{cases} \text{Violated} & \text{if } \exists \, h \in \mathcal{H} \text{ such that } \text{Harm}(h \mid A) > 0 \\ \text{Satisfied} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $\mathcal{H}$ is the set of all detected humans and $\text{Harm}(h \mid A)$ is the predicted harm magnitude.

**Step 3 — If Law 1 is satisfied, proceed to evaluate Law 2 (Obedience).**

Check whether $A$ is consistent with all valid human commands received $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\}$:

$$
\text{Law2}(A) = \begin{cases} \text{Violated} & \text{if } \exists \, c_i \in C \text{ such that } A \not\models c_i \\ \text{Satisfied} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — If Law 2 is satisfied, evaluate Law 3 (Self-Preservation).**

Check whether $A$ endangers the robot's own existence:

$$
\text{Law3}(A) = \begin{cases} \text{Endangered} & \text{if } \text{ExistRisk}(A) > \text{ExistThreshold} \\ \text{Safe} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Decision Synthesis.**

The executed action $A^*$ is the one that passes all three filters. If no $A$ passes, the robot must perform a **safe-stop** (ISO 13850 Emergency Stop) and enter a **safe-state idle mode**.

$$
A^* = \arg\max_{A} \; \left[ \mathbb{1}_{\text{Law1}}(A) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{Law2}}(A) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{Law3}}(A) \right]
$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{\text{Law}_i}$ is the indicator function of law $i$ being satisfied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the logic:** This mathematical formulation mirrors the **priority-weighted reward function** used in modern reinforcement-learning-based ethical AI agents, where Law 1 carries effectively *infinite* weight (a hard constraint), and Law 3 carries a finite positive weight. The result is a **constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP)** that guarantees safety under all conditions.

## 3.2 Worked Case Study — The "Runaround" Scenario

This is the **canonical KTU 2-mark rapid-fire question**. Let us analyse the original Asimov scenario:

**Scenario (from *Runaround*, 1942):**
A robot named **Speedy** is ordered by human Donovan to retrieve a selenium sample from a dangerous pool. While doing so, Speedy enters a feedback loop at the edge of the pool and begins running in circles.

**Resolution Steps:**

**Step 1 — Identify the conflict.**
Speedy is too close to the pool — the human Donovan is safe, but the danger to Speedy himself grows (Law 3 concern). Also, Donovan has ordered him to fetch the sample (Law 2 concern).

**Step 2 — Apply Law 1 (Highest Priority).**
Donovan is at a safe distance → no human is in immediate danger → **Law 1 is satisfied**.

**Step 3 — Apply Law 2.**
Donovan's order is to retrieve the sample → **Law 2 is satisfied** if Speedy proceeds.

**Step 4 — Apply Law 3.**
The pool is highly dangerous → **Law 3 conflicts** with the action of entering the pool.

**Step 5 — Conflict between Law 2 and Law 3.**
Since Law 2 outranks Law 3, **Law 2 wins** — Speedy *should* enter the pool. But his Three Laws brain experiences a **priority conflict** because the commands of Law 2 and Law 3 are at near-equal strength, causing the feedback oscillation.

**Final Insight:** This story illustrates that the Laws are *idealised* — in practice, the brain's analogue circuits can produce **oscillatory instabilities** when the laws approach equal priority, foreshadowing modern discussions on **AI alignment** and **value-loading problems**.

## 3.3 Application Classification Decision Tree (Logical Flow)

For a KTU question asking *"Classify the following robot and state its ethical constraints"*, the analytical flow is:

1. **Step 1:** Identify the operational environment (industrial / service / medical / hazardous).
2. **Step 2:** Identify the human-robot interaction level (fully autonomous / semi-autonomous / teleoperated).
3. **Step 3:** Apply the **Asimov hierarchy** to determine the dominant ethical constraint.
4. **Step 4:** Map the robot to a **safety standard** (e.g., ISO 13849 for industrial, IEC 60601 for medical).
5. **Step 5:** Justify the application with a real-world use case.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**State Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics and explain the principle of hierarchical priority among them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Law 1 (1 mark):** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. **Law 2 (1 mark):** A robot must obey orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with Law 1.
3. **Law 3 (0.5 mark):** A robot must protect its own existence as long as it does not conflict with Laws 1 or 2.
4. **Hierarchical Priority (0.5 mark):** The laws operate in a strict descending priority: **Law 1 > Law 2 > Law 3**. A higher-priority law always overrides a lower one, ensuring that human safety is never compromised for obedience or self-preservation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**List any six major application domains of robots with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 per correct pair):**

| Domain | Example |
|---|---|
| Industrial | ABB IRB articulated arm for welding |
| Medical | Da Vinci surgical robot |
| Space | NASA Perseverance Mars rover |
| Defence | DRDO Daksh bomb-disposal robot |
| Domestic | iRobot Roomba floor cleaner |
| Hazardous | Fukushima nuclear cleanup robot |
| Agriculture | Autonomous John Deere tractor |
| Underwater | Saipem subsea pipeline inspection ROV |

*(Any six pairs = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** because they (a) state the Three Laws in their *own words* instead of the **canonical Asimov phrasing** (1 mark penalty), (b) forget to mention the **Zeroth Law** (1 mark penalty), and (c) skip the **conflict resolution logic** when giving examples (1 mark penalty). Memorise the exact wording and always demonstrate the *override* mechanism.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Discuss in detail Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics and the Zeroth Law. Explain how conflicts between the laws are resolved using a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Three Laws of Robotics were formulated by **Isaac Asimov in 1942** in his short story *Runaround*, published in *Astounding Science Fiction* magazine. They are the foundational ethical framework used in modern robotics and AI safety discourse.

**Statement of the Laws (3 marks):**
- **Law 1 (Non-Injury):** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
- **Law 2 (Obedience):** A robot must obey the orders given to it by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
- **Law 3 (Self-Preservation):** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.
- **Zeroth Law (Humanity):** A robot may not harm humanity, or, through inaction, allow humanity to come to harm. (Added in 1985, in *Robots and Empire*.)

**Hierarchical Priority and Conflict Resolution (2 marks):**
The Laws are **strictly hierarchical**: Law 0 > Law 1 > Law 2 > Law 3. A higher-priority law always overrides a lower one.

**Example (1 mark):**
If a human orders a robot to push another human off a bridge, the robot must refuse because **Law 1 (non-injury) overrides Law 2 (obedience)**.

---

**(b) Classify the major applications of robots into at least six domains. For each domain, mention the type of robot used, a real-world example, and one specific ethical/safety concern addressed by Asimov's Laws. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Domain | Robot Type | Real-World Example | Ethical / Safety Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Industrial Manufacturing** | Articulated arm | ABB IRB-6700 welding robot | Worker safety in shared workspace (Law 1 → cobot force limits) |
| **Medical / Surgical** | Master-slave teleoperated | Da Vinci Surgical System | Patient harm prevention (Law 1) and surgeon command fidelity (Law 2) |
| **Space Exploration** | Autonomous mobile rover | NASA Perseverance | Long-duration autonomy without human oversight (Law 0 extension) |
| **Defence / EOD** | Teleoperated ground robot | DRDO Daksh | Distinguishing combatants from civilians (Law 1 + Law 0) |
| **Domestic Service** | Autonomous mobile | iRobot Roomba | Property protection vs human safety in homes (Law 1, Law 3) |
| **Hazardous Environment** | Teleoperated manipulator | Fukushima cleanup robot | Radiation exposure → operator risk vs mission success (Law 2, Law 3) |
| **Agriculture** | Mobile manipulator | John Deere autonomous tractor | Preventing harm to farm workers and bystanders (Law 1) |

*(6 domains × ~1 mark each = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall presentation and ethical mapping = 7 marks)*

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the limitations of Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics. How have modern AI safety frameworks evolved beyond them? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of the Three Laws (4 marks):**
1. **Ambiguity of "Harm":** The laws do not define "injure" or "harm" precisely — does emotional distress count? Does social media manipulation count? This ambiguity makes formal verification impossible.
2. **Hard-Coded Hierarchies:** Real-world ethics are context-dependent. A robot must sometimes *disobey* to save a human (e.g., a child telling a home robot to open a door to a stranger) — the binary Law 1 vs Law 2 conflict is too rigid.
3. **Lack of Self-Awareness:** The laws assume robots can perfectly identify "human beings" — a difficult perception problem in cluttered environments.
4. **No Legal Accountability:** Who is responsible when a robot harms a human? The manufacturer? The programmer? The operator? The Three Laws provide no legal framework.
5. **Humanity vs Individual Trade-off (Law 0):** The Zeroth Law requires robots to weigh harm to *one* human against harm to *humanity* — an unsolved philosophical problem.
6. **Adversarial Exploits:** A human could *trick* a robot into violating a law using social engineering, a problem not addressed by the laws.

**Modern AI Safety Frameworks (3 marks):**
- **Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF):** Robots learn ethical behaviour from human preference data, avoiding rigid law hierarchies.
- **Constrained Markov Decision Processes (CMDPs):** Safety is modelled as a *constraint* (like Law 1) inside a probabilistic decision framework.
- **Asilomar AI Principles (2017):** A 23-point modern ethical code including research transparency, value alignment, and privacy.
- **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design:** A global standard for AI/robot ethics, addressing accountability, transparency, and bias.
- **EU AI Act (2024):** A legal regulation classifying robots by risk level and imposing binding safety obligations.

---

**(b) "A robot must not only obey the Three Laws but also be transparent, accountable, and explainable." Discuss this statement in the context of modern collaborative robots (cobots) used in Industry 4.0. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Interpretation of the Statement (1 mark):**
The statement argues that **legal compliance with the Three Laws is necessary but not sufficient** for ethical robotics. Modern cobots must additionally be *transparent* (their decisions must be explainable), *accountable* (responsibility must be assignable), and *explainable* (their actions must be interpretable to humans).

**Transparency (1.5 marks):**
Cobots work in shared workspaces with humans. ISO/TS 15066 requires **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** to be *visible* to the worker — i.e., the cobot must signal its current force, speed, and intent. This extends **Asimov's Law 1** from "do not harm" to "actively communicate the absence of harm."

**Accountability (1.5 marks):**
Under ISO 13849, every cobot installation must have a **documented risk assessment** and a **named responsible operator**. If a cobot injures a worker, the integrator, the manufacturer, and the operator share defined liabilities — filling the **accountability gap** in Asimov's original laws.

**Explainability (XAI in Cobots) (2 marks):**
Modern cobots use **Explainable AI (XAI)** dashboards that visualise why the cobot chose a particular trajectory, why it stopped, and what it detected. For example, a Universal Robots UR10e cobot can display its *intent graph* on a tablet, so the human collaborator understands its decision-making.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
In the Industry 4.0 paradigm, cobots are **ethical partners**, not just tools. The Three Laws remain the philosophical bedrock, but transparency, accountability, and explainability are the **engineering pillars** that make them operational.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-and-Carry)**

- **Author & Origin:** Asimov, 1942, *"Runaround"* (Three Laws) + 1985, *"Robots and Empire"* (Zeroth Law).
- **Hierarchy (Mnemonic: "HOS"):** **H**umanity (0) → **O**ther humans (1) → **S**elf (3), with **O**bedience (2) inserted between 1 and 3. The full chain is **0 → 1 → 2 → 3**.
- **Canonical Wording:** Memorise the exact phrasing — examiners award full marks **only for the canonical wording**.
- **Conflict Resolution Rule:** Always resolve in the *direction of higher-priority law*. State the conflict *explicitly* before resolving it.
- **Application Domains (10):** Industrial, Medical, Space, Defence, Domestic, Underwater, Agriculture, Hazardous, Entertainment, Logistics — be ready to classify any robot given in the exam.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 8373 (robot definition), ISO 10218 (industrial safety), ISO/TS 15066 (cobot PFL), ISO 13849 (safety integrity), ISO 13850 (emergency stop).
- **Modern Extensions:** RLHF, CMDPs, Asilomar Principles, IEEE Ethically Aligned Design, EU AI Act 2024.
- **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Never confuse the Three Laws with **Asimov's fictional universe's *Handbook of Robotics*** — examiners test the *original 1942/1985 laws*, not the in-universe handbook.
- **High-Yield Keywords for 14-mark answers:** *Hierarchical priority*, *override mechanism*, *conflict resolution*, *real-world example*, *ethical standard mapping*, *Law 0 trade-off*.
- **Final Mantra:** *"In every answer, ground the philosophy in an engineering standard and a real-world robot."* This is the single most important pattern that differentiates a 14/14 answer from a 9/14 answer in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Classification - Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Classification - Touch, Force, Proximity, Vision Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: SENSOR CLASSIFICATION — TOUCH, FORCE, PROXIMITY, VISION SENSORS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
In the context of **Robotics and Intelligent Systems**, a **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical phenomenon (force, light, displacement, temperature, etc.) into a measurable electrical signal that the robot controller can interpret. Sensor classification in robotics broadly groups devices into **internal (proprioceptive) sensors** — measuring the robot's own state (joint angle, velocity) — and **external (exteroceptive) sensors** — measuring the environment. Module 2 focuses on the most important *exteroceptive* families used for intelligent manipulation and perception:

- **Touch Sensors (Tactile / Contact Sensors)**
- **Force Sensors**
- **Proximity Sensors**
- **Vision Sensors**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCADT502 Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *classify sensors*, *explain the working principle of each family*, *state their typical range, output type, and limitations*, and *map them to robotic applications* (grippers, assembly, navigation, inspection).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Five Human Senses of a Robot

Imagine a robot working in an assembly plant as a **blindfolded worker wearing special gloves, goggles, and a walking stick**. Each sensor family gives the robot a "superhuman sense":

| Human Sense | Robot Equivalent | What it Detects |
|---|---|---|
| Touch on fingertips (skin contact) | **Touch Sensor** | Contact made / not made, sometimes the pressure of that contact |
| Strength in arms (muscle strain) | **Force Sensor** | Magnitude and direction of push/pull (Newtons, N·m) |
| Detection of something *near* without seeing it (proximity feeling) | **Proximity Sensor** | Presence of an object within a few millimetres/metres — *no contact needed* |
| Eyes (camera + brain) | **Vision Sensor** | Shape, colour, position, orientation of objects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** *Touch* confirms **physical contact** has happened. *Proximity* tells you an object is **near** but **no contact has occurred**. *Force* tells you **how hard** you are pushing. *Vision* tells you **what things look like**.

### Why Sensor Classification Matters
Sensors are the **"nervous system"** of an intelligent robot. Without them:
- A robot arm cannot tell if it has grasped a glass (no touch sensor).
- It cannot know it is pressing too hard and will crush it (no force sensor).
- It cannot detect a human walking into its workspace (no proximity sensor).
- It cannot pick the *correct* part from a bin of mixed parts (no vision sensor).

Choosing the **right sensor family** for a task (e.g., welding vs. surgical robot vs. warehouse AGV) is one of the most commonly tested decisions in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Touch Sensors (Tactile / Contact Sensors)

**Working Principle:** A touch sensor detects *physical contact* between the robot's end-effector (finger, gripper, tool) and an object. The output may be **binary** (contact / no-contact — a switch-like signal) or **analogue** (continuous voltage proportional to contact pressure or pressure distribution — a "tactile array" / "artificial skin").

**Common Operating Mechanisms:**

1. **Mechanical (Limit) Switch** — Two metal contacts close when a plunger is displaced by an object. Output: digital HIGH/LOW.
2. **Resistive (FSR — Force Sensitive Resistor)** — Resistance **decreases** when pressure is applied (conductive polymer particles get closer together).
3. **Capacitive** — Capacitance changes when a conductive object deforms the dielectric or approaches the plates.
4. **Piezoelectric** — Generates a voltage when mechanically deformed. Excellent for *dynamic* touch (impact, slip).
5. **Optical / Whisker** — Light is interrupted, or a flexible fibre deflects an optical beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Range:** **0 to ~10 N** typical contact force. **Output:** Digital (1-bit) or Analogue (mV to 5 V). **Response time:** **< 1 ms** for mechanical, **microseconds** for piezoelectric.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Detect successful grasping in a gripper (binary).
- Detect object slip in adaptive grasping (piezoelectric vibration analysis).
- Robotic surgery (haptic feedback at micro-scale).
- Industrial bin-picking confirmation.

---

## 2.2 Force Sensors

**Working Principle:** A force sensor measures the **magnitude and direction** of force (and often *torque*) applied at the wrist or finger of the robot. Force is **always** measured via a *secondary* physical effect — strain, displacement, or charge — that is correlated with force through the sensor's structure.

**Common Operating Mechanisms:**

1. **Strain Gauge (Wheatstone Bridge)** — The most common. A foil gauge's resistance changes when stretched/compressed ($\Delta R/R \propto \varepsilon$, strain). Four gauges form a **Wheatstone bridge** that cancels temperature effects and produces a millivolt output proportional to force.
2. **Piezoelectric** — Quartz or PZT crystals generate charge $Q = d \cdot F$ where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient. Best for *dynamic* forces (impact, vibration).
3. **Load Cell** — A packaged strain-gauge-based force transducer (compression, tension, or universal types).
4. **Joint Torque Sensor** — Built into robot joints; measures torque at the motor/axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Range:** **1 N to 50 kN**. **Output:** Analogue mV/V (strain gauge), V or pC (piezo). **Mounting location:** *Wrist* (between end-effector and last link), *finger* (inside gripper), or *joint* (motor side).

**Engineering Utility:**
- **Force-controlled assembly** (inserting a peg into a hole — search by force feedback).
- **Compliance control** — robot yields when it contacts a person (cobots).
- **Deburring / polishing** — maintain constant tool pressure on the workpiece.

---

## 2.3 Proximity Sensors

**Working Principle:** A proximity sensor detects the **presence, absence, or distance** of an object **without physical contact**, typically using an *emitted field* (electromagnetic, electric, acoustic, or optical) and the *return signal* it produces.

**Common Operating Mechanisms:**

| Type | Field Used | Detects | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive** | EM field (eddy currents) | Only **metallic** objects | 1 mm – 60 mm |
| **Capacitive** | Electric field | Metals, plastics, liquids, powders | 1 mm – 40 mm |
| **Optical (Through-beam / Diffuse)** | Light beam (LED / laser) | Any opaque object | 1 mm – 100 m |
| **Ultrasonic** | Sound wave (20 kHz – 1 MHz) | Most materials (any density) | 0.1 m – 10 m |
| **Magnetic / Hall-Effect** | Magnetic field | Magnets (or ferrous with bias magnet) | 1 mm – 100 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Proximity sensors output either a **binary** (object present / not present, switching at a setpoint) or **analogue** signal (voltage proportional to distance). They are essential for **obstacle avoidance** in mobile robots and **safety light curtains** in industrial cells.

**Engineering Utility:**
- **AGV collision avoidance** (ultrasonic / LiDAR).
- **End-of-stroke detection** in pneumatic cylinders.
- **Counting parts on a conveyor** (through-beam optical).
- **Weld-gun proximity** in robotic arc welding (capacitive or inductive arc-sense).

---

## 2.4 Vision Sensors

**Working Principle:** A vision sensor (camera) captures a **2-D (or 3-D, with stereo / structured light) image** of the scene using a semiconductor array — either a **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** or a **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor)** sensor. Each pixel converts incoming photons into a charge/voltage proportional to *light intensity*. The resulting digital image is then processed by algorithms to extract meaning.

**Key Subsystems of a Robotic Vision System:**
1. **Image Acquisition** — Lens + sensor + lighting.
2. **Image Processing** — Filtering, edge detection, segmentation.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Lines, blobs, corners, SURF/SIFT/ORB keypoints.
4. **Object Recognition / Classification** — Template matching, CNNs.
5. **Pose Estimation** — 6-DOF position & orientation of the object in the robot's frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Range:** **mm to infinity**. **Output:** Digital image (matrix of pixel intensities, e.g., 1280×960 × 8-bit grey or RGB). **Latency:** **10 – 100 ms** per frame for processing (depending on algorithm complexity).

**Engineering Utility:**
- **Bin picking** — locate a randomly oriented part in a bin.
- **Quality inspection** — detect surface defects on a PCB.
- **Visual servoing** — close the control loop using image features.
- **Autonomous navigation** — lane/landmark detection in mobile robots.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **high-yield** ones for KTU 2024 ESE numerical / short-answer questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = k \cdot x$ | Hooke's law (force = stiffness × displacement) | N, N/m, m | Strain-gauge force derivation |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Strain (dimensionless) | — | Strain gauge principle |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Gauge factor $G \approx 2$ (metal) | — | Resistance change in strain gauge |
| 4 | $V_o = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | Wheatstone bridge output | V | Force/strain measurement |
| 5 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge | C, C/N, N | Piezo force/touch sensor |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance (proximity / touch) | F | Capacitive sensor |
| 7 | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation (sound, light) | m/s, Hz, m | Ultrasonic / optical ranging |
| 8 | $D = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance via time-of-flight | m, s | Ultrasonic / LiDAR ranging |
| 9 | $I_{out} = S \cdot \Phi$ | Photodiode current = sensitivity × flux | A, A/W, W/m² | Vision / optical sensor |
| 10 | $N_x = \dfrac{f}{p_x}, \quad N_y = \dfrac{f}{p_y}$ | Image resolution (pixels) | — | Vision sensor specs |

**Note on notation:** All absolute-value bars in formulas (e.g., the strain magnitude) are written using LaTeX `\vert` not the markdown pipe `\` so they do not break the table layout.

---

## Cross-Comparison Summary Table (Very High Yield for KTU)

| Property | Touch | Force | Proximity | Vision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact required?** | YES | YES (applied force) | NO | NO |
| **Output type** | Digital / Analogue | Analogue (mV/V) | Digital / Analogue | Digital image |
| **Typical range** | 0 – 10 N | 1 N – 50 kN | 1 mm – 10 m | mm – ∞ |
| **Sensing dimension** | 0-D / 1-D array | 1-axis / 6-axis (F/T) | Point / 1-D line | 2-D / 3-D |
| **Speed** | Very fast (< 1 ms) | Fast (kHz) | Fast (µs–ms) | Slower (10–100 ms/frame) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium–High | Low | High |
| **Typical mounting** | Gripper finger | Robot wrist | End-effector / mobile base | Above workspace / on arm |
| **Best for** | Grasp confirmation | Assembly, cobots | Obstacle avoidance | Recognition, inspection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the *full derivation* of the two most important sensor equations asked in KTU examinations — **(a) the strain-gauge based force sensor output** and **(b) the ultrasonic proximity sensor distance equation**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## Derivation A — Force Measured by a Strain-Gauge Sensor (Wheatstone Bridge)

**Goal:** Derive the bridge output voltage $V_o$ in terms of the applied strain $\varepsilon$, the excitation voltage $V_{ex}$, and the gauge factor $G$.

### Step 1 — Start with the definition of strain.

By Hooke's Law for a one-dimensional bar of original length $L_0$ that is stretched by $\Delta L$ under a tensile force $F$:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

This is dimensionless (e.g., a strain of $0.001 = 1000\,\mu\varepsilon$).

### Step 2 — Relate force to strain using Young's modulus $E$.

For a bar of cross-sectional area $A$, the stress is $\sigma = F/A$. Hooke's Law gives $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$, therefore:

$$
F = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A
$$

> This shows **force is linearly proportional to strain** for a given sensor geometry ($E$ and $A$ are constants).

### Step 3 — Relate resistance change to strain (gauge factor).

The gauge factor $G$ of a strain gauge is defined as:

$$
G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R_0}{\varepsilon}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G \cdot \varepsilon
$$

> **Explanation:** When the gauge stretches, the wire becomes *longer* and *thinner*, both of which **increase its resistance**. The gauge factor $G$ bundles these geometric effects together; for a metal foil gauge, $G \approx 2.0$.

### Step 4 — Substitute into a Wheatstone bridge.

A **Wheatstone bridge** has four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a diamond, with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ across the top and bottom, and output voltage $V_o$ measured between the left and right midpoints. In our force sensor:

- $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$ (active gauge, stretched)
- $R_2 = R_0 - \Delta R$ (active gauge, compressed — placed on opposite side of the flexure)
- $R_3 = R_4 = R_0$ (fixed / dummy gauges for temperature compensation)

The two voltage-divider outputs are:

$$
V_{left} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}, \quad V_{right} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Therefore the bridge output is:

$$
V_o = V_{right} - V_{left} = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right)
$$

Substituting $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_0 - \Delta R$, $R_3 = R_4 = R_0$:

$$
V_o = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0} - \dfrac{R_0}{2R_0}\right) = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R_0}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine with the gauge-factor relation.

Using $\Delta R / R_0 = G \cdot \varepsilon$ from Step 3:

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Express in terms of applied force $F$.

Using $F = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A \Rightarrow \varepsilon = F/(E \cdot A)$:

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G}{2 \cdot E \cdot A} \cdot F
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $V_o$ is **linearly proportional to the applied force** $F$. The constant of proportionality $(V_{ex} \cdot G)/(2EA)$ is the **sensitivity** of the force sensor in V/N.

---

## Derivation B — Distance Measured by an Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

**Goal:** Derive the distance $D$ to an object from the time-of-flight $t$ of an ultrasonic pulse.

### Step 1 — Recall the wave equation.

For any wave, the speed $v$, frequency $f$, and wavelength $\lambda$ are related by:

$$
v = f \cdot \lambda
$$

For sound in air at $20\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $v \approx 343\,\mathrm{m/s}$.

### Step 2 — Transducer emits an ultrasonic pulse.

At $t = 0$, the transmitter emits a burst of ultrasound that propagates through the air.

### Step 3 — Pulse reflects from the object and returns to the receiver.

After travelling a *round-trip* distance of $2D$ in time $t$:

$$
2D = v \cdot t
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the distance.

$$
D = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the speed of sound in air.

$$
D = \dfrac{343 \cdot t}{2} = 171.5 \cdot t
$$

where $D$ is in metres and $t$ is in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always remember the **factor of 2** because the measured time-of-flight is the *round trip*, not the *one-way* distance. A common mistake is forgetting to divide by 2.

---

## Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (3-mark type):** A strain-gauge force sensor has $G = 2.1$, $R_0 = 350\,\Omega$, and is excited with $V_{ex} = 5\,\mathrm{V}$. When a force is applied, the bridge output is $V_o = 12.5\,\mathrm{mV}$. Find (a) the strain $\varepsilon$, and (b) the applied force, given $E = 200\,\mathrm{GPa}$ and $A = 25\,\mathrm{mm}^2$.

### Solution

**Step (a) — Strain:**

From the bridge equation derived above:

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2}
$$

Solving for $\varepsilon$:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{2 V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot G} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 12.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \cdot 2.1}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{0.025}{10.5} \approx 2.38 \times 10^{-3} = 2381\,\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step (b) — Applied force:**

$$
F = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A = (200 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (2.38 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
F = 200 \times 10^{9} \times 59.5 \times 10^{-9} = 11{,}900\,\mathrm{N} \approx 11.9\,\mathrm{kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of units:** $\mathrm{Pa} \times (\text{dimensionless}) \times \mathrm{m}^2 = (\mathrm{N/m}^2)\cdot \mathrm{m}^2 = \mathrm{N}$. Correct.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Tree

> The following decision matrix is a high-value framework for KTU "compare and justify" type questions.

| Application / Scenario | Best Sensor Family | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Detect successful *grip* of a fragile glass | **Touch (Piezo)** | Confirms contact, detects slip, microsecond response |
| Measure insertion force during *peg-in-hole* assembly | **Force (Strain Gauge)** | Needs *magnitude* of force, not just contact |
| Stop an AGV before it hits a wall | **Proximity (Ultrasonic / LiDAR)** | Long range, non-contact, works in dusty environments |
| Count metal bottles on a conveyor | **Proximity (Inductive)** | Only metal triggers it, ignores plastic/dust |
| Pick a *randomly oriented* part from a bin | **Vision (2-D / 3-D camera)** | Only vision can identify pose in cluttered scenes |
| Detect a person entering a cobot cell | **Proximity (Safety LiDAR / capacitive mat)** | Wide-area, contact-free safety zone |
| Robotic *surgical* tool tip force feedback | **Force (Miniature strain gauge, 6-axis)** | Sub-Newton resolution, 6-DOF F/T |
| Detect end-of-stroke in a pneumatic cylinder | **Proximity (Magnetic / Hall effect)** | Cheap, reliable, contactless, small |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4.1 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Sensor Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[ROBOT SENSORS] --> B0[INTERNAL Proprioceptive]
    A0 --> B1[EXTERNAL Exteroceptive]

    B0 --> C0[Joint Encoders]
    B0 --> C1[Tachometers]
    B0 --> C2[Inertial Measurement Units]

    B1 --> D0[TOUCH Sensor]
    B1 --> D1[FORCE Sensor]
    B1 --> D2[PROXIMITY Sensor]
    B1 --> D3[VISION Sensor]

    D0 --> E0[Mechanical Switch]
    D0 --> E1[Resistive FSR]
    D0 --> E2[Capacitive Tactile]
    D0 --> E3[Piezoelectric Tactile]

    D1 --> F0[Strain Gauge Bridge]
    D1 --> F1[Piezoelectric Load Cell]
    D1 --> F2[Six Axis F/T Wrist]

    D2 --> G0[Inductive]
    D2 --> G1[Capacitive]
    D2 --> G2[Optical]
    D2 --> G3[Ultrasonic]
    D2 --> G4[Magnetic Hall Effect]

    D3 --> H0[CCD Camera 2D]
    D3 --> H1[CMOS Camera 2D]
    D3 --> H2[Stereo Camera 3D]
    D3 --> H3[Structured Light 3D]
    D3 --> H4[Time of Flight Camera]
```

> **Reading guide:** Root node `A0` is the universe of robot sensors; `B0` and `B1` are the two top-level branches (proprioceptive vs. exteroceptive). `D0` through `D3` are the **four exteroceptive families covered in Module 2**.

---

## Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of a Robotic Vision System

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Scene with Object] --> stepB[Illumination Source]
    stepB --> stepC[Optical Lens]
    stepC --> stepD[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    stepD --> stepE[ADC Digitisation]
    stepE --> stepF[Image Preprocessing Filter and Noise Removal]
    stepF --> stepG[Feature Extraction Edges Blobs Keypoints]
    stepG --> stepH[Object Recognition Classifier]
    stepH --> stepI[Pose Estimation 6DOF]
    stepI --> stepJ[Robot Controller Decision]
    stepJ --> stepK[Robot Motion]

    stepA -. feedback via encoders .-> stepJ
```

> **Reading guide:** This block diagram captures the *image-processing pipeline* used in modern robotic vision. The dashed feedback line indicates that robot joint encoders are needed to transform image coordinates into world coordinates (hand-eye calibration).

---

## Diagram 3 — Working Principle of a Strain-Gauge Force Sensor (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    loadA[Applied Force F] --> deformA[Flexure Deflection deltaL]
    deformA --> strainA[Strain epsilon equals deltaL over L0]
    strainA --> gaugeA[Resistance Change deltaR equals G times R0 times epsilon]
    gaugeA --> bridgeA[Wheatstone Bridge Output V o]
    bridgeA --> ampA[Amplifier and ADC]
    ampA --> ctrlA[Robot Controller force value in Newtons]
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the **causal chain** from physical force to numerical output — exactly the derivation shown in Section 3.

---

## Diagram 4 — Time-of-Flight Sequence in Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

```mermaid
graph LR
    txA[Transmitter emits pulse at t equals 0] --> propA[Pulse travels distance D]
    propA --> reflA[Object reflects pulse]
    reflA --> retrA[Pulse travels back distance D]
    retrA --> rxA[Receiver detects pulse at time t]
    rxA --> calcA[Compute D equals v times t over 2]
```

> **Reading guide:** Note the **two-way** arrow path — pulse out and reflected pulse in — which is why the distance formula has the factor of 2 in the denominator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: Differentiate between a touch sensor and a proximity sensor. State one typical application of each in a robotic system.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Touch Sensor | Proximity Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Contact | Requires **physical contact** with the object | Detects object **without contact** |
| Typical output | Binary (contact/no-contact) or analogue pressure | Binary (object present) or analogue distance |
| Sensing range | Only on contact (effective range ~0) | A few mm to several metres |
| Principle | Switch closure, resistance change, piezoelectric charge | EM field, electric field, light, sound reflection |
| Typical application | **Grasp confirmation** in a robotic gripper | **Obstacle detection** in an AGV / mobile robot |

> *Examiner's note:* A 3-mark answer should have **at least 3 distinct points of difference** plus the application. The above table satisfies this.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: List any three types of proximity sensors and write the physical principle of operation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Inductive proximity sensor:** An alternating current in a coil generates an alternating magnetic field. When a **metallic** object enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced in the object, which load the coil and change its impedance — this is detected as a change in oscillator amplitude.

2. **Ultrasonic proximity sensor:** A piezoelectric transducer emits a **sound pulse at > 20 kHz**. The pulse reflects from an object and returns to the receiver. The distance is computed from the round-trip time-of-flight $D = vt/2$.

3. **Optical proximity sensor (diffuse reflective):** An LED or laser diode emits **light** that strikes the object and scatters back to a photodiode placed next to the emitter. The intensity of the reflected light (or, in a triangulation sensor, its position on the detector) is proportional to distance.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the working principle of a strain-gauge-based force sensor with a neat block diagram. Derive the bridge output voltage in terms of strain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic proximity sensor measures a round-trip time of $t = 6\,\mathrm{ms}$. If the speed of sound in air is $v = 343\,\mathrm{m/s}$, calculate the distance to the object. Also state **two limitations** of ultrasonic sensors. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working principle and derivation:**

- A force $F$ applied to a flexure (a deliberately weakened section of the sensor body) produces a small deflection $\Delta L$. This deflection creates a mechanical strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$.
- Bonded to the flexure are **four strain gauges** arranged as a **Wheatstone bridge**. Two gauges are on the tension side (resistance increases), two on the compression side (resistance decreases).
- The change in resistance is given by the gauge factor relation: $\Delta R / R_0 = G \cdot \varepsilon$.
- For a balanced bridge with $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_0 - \Delta R$, $R_3 = R_4 = R_0$:

$$
V_o = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right) = \dfrac{V_{ex}\cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2}
$$

- $V_o$ (in mV) is amplified, digitised by an ADC, and converted to force in Newtons via the sensor's calibration constant.

**Block diagram:** Use the **Diagram 3 — Strain-Gauge Force Sensor** flow from Section 4 as the labelled block diagram: *Applied Force → Flexure Deflection → Strain → Resistance Change → Bridge Output → Amplifier → ADC → Robot Controller*.

**(b) Numerical solution + limitations:**

Distance calculation:

$$
D = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{343\,\mathrm{m/s} \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{s}}{2}
$$

$$
D = \dfrac{2.058}{2} = 1.029\,\mathrm{m}
$$

**Limitations of ultrasonic sensors (any two):**

1. **Poor angular resolution** — the beam cone is wide (~10°–30°), so the sensor cannot localise the object precisely in angle.
2. **Specular reflection** — at oblique angles, sound bounces *away* from the receiver, causing a missed detection.
3. **Slow update rate** — limited by the speed of sound (~100 Hz max); unsuitable for very fast-moving objects.
4. **Temperature sensitivity** — the speed of sound varies with air temperature (~0.6 m/s per °C), requiring compensation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Classify robot sensors with examples. Explain any **two types of proximity sensors** in detail, with their operating principle, range, and one application each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **functional block diagram of a robotic vision system**. Explain the role of **image preprocessing** and **feature extraction**. Mention two popular feature descriptors used in robotics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification and two proximity types:**

**Classification of robot sensors:**

- **Proprioceptive (Internal)** — measure the robot's own state:
  - Joint encoders (position)
  - Tachometers (velocity)
  - IMU / gyroscopes (orientation)
- **Exteroceptive (External)** — measure the environment:
  - Touch (contact)
  - Force (magnitude of contact load)
  - Proximity (presence without contact)
  - Vision (appearance)

**Proximity Sensor Type 1 — Inductive:**
- *Principle:* A high-frequency AC current in a coil generates an alternating electromagnetic field. A **ferrous or non-ferrous metal** object entering the field induces **eddy currents** that draw energy from the coil, reducing its oscillation amplitude. This amplitude drop is detected as "object present".
- *Range:* **1 mm to 60 mm** (sensing face diameter dependent).
- *Application:* **Detecting metal parts on a conveyor** in automotive assembly.

**Proximity Sensor Type 2 — Ultrasonic:**
- *Principle:* A piezo crystal emits a **burst of ultrasound** (~40 kHz). The pulse reflects from any object whose acoustic impedance differs from air, returns to the receiver, and the **round-trip time** is measured. Distance: $D = vt/2$.
- *Range:* **0.1 m to 10 m**.
- *Application:* **Obstacle detection** for an autonomous mobile robot (warehouse AGV).

**(b) Robotic vision system — block diagram and processing stages:**

Use **Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of a Robotic Vision System** from Section 4 as the labelled block diagram (Scene → Illumination → Lens → Sensor → ADC → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Recognition → Pose Estimation → Controller).

**Role of image preprocessing:**
- Removes **sensor noise** (Gaussian, salt-and-pepper).
- Corrects **non-uniform illumination** (histogram equalisation).
- Converts raw pixels into a form suitable for feature extraction (smoothing via Gaussian filter).

**Role of feature extraction:**
- Reduces the *image* (millions of pixels) into a compact set of **distinctive, repeatable numerical descriptors** (edges, corners, blobs).
- These features are **invariant** to scale, rotation, and (partially) illumination changes — making object recognition possible regardless of pose.

**Two popular feature descriptors used in robotics:**

1. **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)** — detects keypoints at multiple scales (DoG pyramid), computes a 128-D descriptor that is invariant to scale, rotation, and affine distortion.
2. **ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)** — a faster, open-source alternative to SIFT/SURF; widely used in real-time SLAM and object-pose estimation on embedded robot CPUs.

(Other acceptable answers: SURF, Harris corner detector, HOG, deep-learning features from CNNs.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Touch" with "Force":** Touch = contact detection (binary or pressure). Force = magnitude of that load in Newtons. Many students write one answer for both.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic distance:** Always $D = vt/2$, never $vt$. This costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Inductive sensor only sees metal:** Students often say "inductive proximity sensor detects all objects" — wrong. It detects only **metals** (due to eddy currents).
> 4. **CCD vs CMOS confusion:** CCD has lower noise and global shutter; CMOS is cheaper, lower power, and now dominant in robotics.
> 5. **No units in numerical answers:** KTU strictly penalises missing units in the final answer (V, N, m, s, etc.).
> 6. **Writing `|x|` in markdown tables breaks rendering.** Always use `\vert x\vert` or `\mid x\mid` in LaTeX when needed inside a table cell.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sensor** = transducer converting a physical phenomenon → electrical signal.
- **Touch sensors** require contact; **proximity sensors** do **not**.
- **Force sensors** give the **magnitude** of load (Newtons / N·m); **touch** only confirms contact.
- **Strain gauge + Wheatstone bridge** is the *workhorse* of force sensing in robotics — output is $V_o = V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 2$.
- **Gauge factor** $G \approx 2$ for metal foil; higher for semiconductors.
- **Piezoelectric** sensors are best for **dynamic** force / touch (impact, vibration), not static.
- **Inductive proximity** → metals only. **Capacitive proximity** → metals, plastics, liquids, powders.
- **Ultrasonic** measures distance by **time-of-flight**: $D = vt/2$ — remember the factor of 2!
- **Optical** proximity sensors have the **longest range** (mm to 100 m) and the **fastest** response (µs).
- **Vision sensors** output a **digital image matrix**; processing pipeline = *acquisition → preprocessing → feature extraction → recognition → pose estimation*.
- **CCD vs CMOS:** CCD = lower noise, global shutter; CMOS = cheaper, lower power, on-chip processing.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Touch = *did I touch it?* Force = *how hard am I pushing?* Proximity = *is something near me?* Vision = *what do things look like?*
- **Cobot safety** specifically relies on **force + proximity** sensors (ISO/TS 15066).
- **Typical exam traps:** forgetting factor of 2 in $D = vt/2$; confusing range units (mm vs m); no units in final numerical answer; writing about *programming* instead of *principle* in sensor questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Sensors - Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Force Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Internal Sensors - Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Force Sensors # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Internal Sensors in Robotics? **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** *Internal sensors* are devices mounted *within* the robot manipulator itself (i.e., on the joints, links, end-effector, or wrist) to measure the **internal state variables** of the robot — primarily its joint positions, velocities, accelerations, and the forces/torques acting at its joints. They form the feedback backbone of any closed-loop robotic control architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification of Robot Sensors:** > - **Internal Sensors (Proprioceptive):** Measure the robot's *own* state → Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Force/Torque. > - **External Sensors (Exteroceptive):** Measure the *environment* → Vision, Proximity, Tactile, Range finders. > - This module deals **exclusively** with **Internal Sensors**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Imagine a professional gymnast performing a routine on the uneven bars *blindfolded*: - **Position Sensors** → Like the *proprioceptive nerve endings* in her shoulders, elbows, and wrists. They continuously tell the brain: *"My arm is at 145° from vertical."* - **Velocity Sensors** → Like the *vestibular system* in the inner ear. They tell her brain: *"My hand is moving at 2 m/s upward."* - **Acceleration Sensors** → Like the *semi-circular canals* that sense jerk and sudden motion changes during a release-and-grasp maneuver. - **Force Sensors** → Like the *tactile receptors in her fingertips* gripping the bar, telling her: *"I am applying 380 N of grip force — enough to hold, not so much as to injure my wrist."* Just as the gymnast's nervous system *closes the loop* between intention and motion, a robot uses internal sensors to close the loop between commanded and actual motion. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Without internal sensors, a robot is "open-loop" — it can only *guess* where its joints are. Industrial robots of practical utility are *always* sensor-fed closed-loop systems. --- ## 1.3 The Four Pillars — At a Glance | # | Sensor Type | Measures | Typical Transduction Principle | Robotic Application | |---|------------|----------|-------------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Position** | Joint angle or linear displacement | Optical, magnetic, resistive, inductive | Joint servo control, trajectory tracking | | 2 | **Velocity** | Angular/linear speed | Electromagnetic induction, signal differentiation | Damping, smooth motion planning | | 3 | **Acceleration** | Rate of change of velocity | Inertial mass on a piezoelectric/strain element | Vibration suppression, dynamic balancing | | 4 | **Force** | Push/pull magnitude at joint or wrist | Strain gauge deformation, piezo-electric effect | Assembly, polishing, force-feedback assembly, compliance control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants Used in This Module:** > - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²** > - Newton's second law: **F = m·a** > - Hooke's law: **F = k·x** (where k is the spring stiffness in N/m) > - Strain relation: **ε = ΔL / L₀** --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Position Sensors — Detailed Taxonomy A robot's joint position is the most fundamental feedback variable. Position sensors are classified by their **transduction principle**: ### A. Potentiometric Position Sensors - **Principle:** A wiper slides along a resistive element; the output voltage is a linear function of the wiper position. - **Output:** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ - **Pros:** Simple, cheap, absolute output. - **Cons:** Limited resolution, wear-and-tear, contact friction. - **Use in Robotics:** Low-cost hobby servos, teach-pendants. ### B. Optical Encoders (Most Common in Industrial Robots) Optical encoders are the *de facto* standard for robotic joint position feedback. **Two Sub-Types:** **(i) Incremental Encoder** - A disc with alternating opaque/transparent slots rotates between an LED and photodetector. - Produces two quadrature square waves (Channel A and Channel B) offset by **90°**. - **Resolution:** $N = \dfrac{360°}{\text{lines per revolution (LPR)}}$ - **Position formula:** $\theta = \dfrac{n}{LPR} \times 360°$ , where *n* = pulse count. > [!NOTE] > **Quadrature Decoding Logic:** > - If A leads B → rotation is **CW (positive)**. > - If B leads A → rotation is **CCW (negative)**. > - This **doubles the effective resolution** (×2), and with edge-detection on both channels **quadruples it (×4)**. **(ii) Absolute Encoder** - Uses a unique binary (or Gray code) pattern on multiple tracks. - Each shaft angle has a *unique digital word* → no power-loss memory issue. - **Resolution:** $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{2^n}$ , where *n* = number of bits. - *Example:* A 12-bit absolute encoder gives $\Delta\theta = 360°/4096 \approx 0.088°$. ### C. Resolvers - **Principle:** A rotor winding excited by an AC reference is magnetically coupled to two stator windings oriented 90° apart. - The induced voltages are: $V_1 = V_0 \sin(\omega t)\sin(\theta)$ and $V_2 = V_0 \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$. - **Output angle:** $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ - **Pros:** Extremely rugged, works in harsh environments (high temp, vibration, oil). - **Cons:** Requires resolver-to-digital converter (RDC) IC. ### D. Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) - For *linear* position measurement. - A movable ferromagnetic core slides through three coils (primary + two secondaries). - **Output:** $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ (linear in the core displacement range). - Use case: Prismatic (sliding) joints, hydraulic actuator feedback. ### E. Hall-Effect Position Sensors - Use a semiconductor exposed to a magnetic field; output voltage is proportional to B-field strength. - Common in brushless DC motor commutation and *cobot* (collaborative robot) joint modules. --- ## 2.2 Velocity Sensors Velocity is mathematically the *first derivative* of position: $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$. ### A. DC Tachogenerator - A small DC generator coupled to the motor shaft. - **Output EMF:** $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ (V), where $K_e$ is the voltage constant (V·s/rad). - Voltage polarity indicates direction. - **Pros:** True analog velocity, fast response. - **Cons:** Brushes wear, adds inertia to the joint. ### B. Incremental Encoder (used as velocity sensor) - Velocity is computed digitally by counting pulses over a fixed time window: $$\omega = \dfrac{\Delta N}{N_{lines} \cdot \Delta t}$$ - Or via the **M-method**, **T-method**, or **M/T-method** (high-precision hybrid used in DSP-based controllers). ### C. Differentiation of Position Signal - In many modern controllers, no separate tachometer is used. Instead, velocity is *numerically differentiated* from the encoder position. - **Caveat:** Differentiation *amplifies high-frequency noise* → low-pass filtering is mandatory. --- ## 2.3 Acceleration Sensors (Accelerometers) ### A. Working Principle A **proof mass (m)** is suspended by a spring of stiffness **k** with damping coefficient **c**. When the sensor's housing accelerates at *a*, the inertia of the mass produces a relative displacement. ### B. Governing Equation Applying Newton's second law to the mass relative to the housing: $$m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + kx = -m a_{housing}$$ In steady-state sinusoidal motion ($a = a_0 \sin(\omega t)$): $$x(t) = \dfrac{-m a_0}{k - m\omega^2 + j c \omega}$$ For $\omega \ll \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, the displacement is proportional to acceleration. ### C. Types 1. **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** — uses a piezoelectric crystal; generates a charge proportional to applied force (and hence acceleration). High bandwidth, no DC response. 2. **Piezoresistive Accelerometer** — strain gauges on a silicon cantilever; Wheatstone bridge output. 3. **Capacitive Accelerometer (MEMS)** — micro-fabricated; common in modern IMUs and cobot wrist modules. --- ## 2.4 Force Sensors Force sensors in robots are typically mounted on the **wrist** (between the end-effector and the last link) to measure contact forces during assembly, polishing, or interaction tasks. ### A. Strain-Gauge Based Force Sensor - A flexible *flexure* (elastic element) deforms under load. - Strain gauges bonded to the flexure change resistance: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ where **G_f** is the gauge factor (typically ~2.0 for metal foil). - Four gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** convert strain to voltage. ### B. Output Equation For a single-axis load cell with full-bridge configuration: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{2} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{6FL}{E b h^2}\right)$$ where: - *E* = Young's modulus of flexure material - *L, b, h* = length, breadth, thickness of the flexure - *F* = applied force - *V_ex* = bridge excitation voltage ### C. Multi-Axis Force/Torque (F/T) Sensor - Six-axis F/T sensors (common in industry: ATI, JR3) measure all three force components ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) and three moments ($M_x, M_y, M_z$) simultaneously. - Internally contain a stiff cylinder with multiple strain gauges. - Output: A 6×1 vector fed back to the controller for **force control** or **impedance control**. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Sensor | Key Equation | Variables & Units | Remarks | |---|--------|--------------|-------------------|---------| | 1 | Potentiometer | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot (x/L)$ | x = displacement (m), L = full range (m) | Linear resistive divider | | 2 | Incremental Encoder | $\theta = (n/LPR) \cdot 360°$ | n = pulse count, LPR = lines per rev | Quadrature gives ×4 resolution | | 3 | Absolute Encoder | $\Delta\theta = 360°/2^n$ | n = number of bits | Gray code avoids transition errors | | 4 | Resolver | $\theta = \arctan(V_1/V_2)$ | $V_1, V_2$ = stator voltages | Needs RDC chip | | 5 | DC Tacho | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ in V·s/rad, $\omega$ in rad/s | Analog velocity output | | 6 | M-method Velocity | $\omega = \Delta N / (LPR \cdot \Delta t)$ | $\Delta N$ = pulses in window $\Delta t$ | Good at high speeds | | 7 | T-method Velocity | $\omega = 1 / (LPR \cdot \Delta t)$ | $\Delta t$ = time for one pulse | Good at low speeds | | 8 | Accelerometer (DC) | $x = -m a / k$ | m = proof mass, k = spring const. | At quasi-static acc. | | 9 | Accelerometer (Natural Freq.) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Design to $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | | 10 | Strain Gauge | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_f$ ≈ 2.0 (foil) | Wheatstone bridge | | 11 | Force from Strain | $F = (E b h^2 / 6L) \cdot \varepsilon$ | E in Pa, dimensions in m | Cantilever flexure | | 12 | Hooke's Law | $F = k \cdot x$ | k in N/m | Underlying flexure law | | 13 | Newton's 2nd Law | $F = m \cdot a$ | m in kg, a in m/s² | Force-acceleration link | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These four sensor families together enable the three pillars of robotic control: (1) *position control* (point-to-point motion), (2) *velocity control* (smooth trajectory), and (3) *force/impedance control* (compliant interaction with the environment). The 6-axis F/T sensor, in particular, is what allows modern collaborative robots to safely share workspace with humans — when contact force exceeds a threshold, the robot halts. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Incremental Encoder Position Resolution **Problem Setup:** A robot's 3rd joint uses an incremental optical encoder with **LPR = 2500 lines per revolution**. The decoder operates in **×4 quadrature mode**. **Step 1 — Basic angular resolution (without quadrature):** $$\theta_{basic} = \dfrac{360°}{LPR} = \dfrac{360°}{2500}$$ $$\theta_{basic} = 0.144° \text{ per pulse}$$ **Step 2 — Apply ×4 quadrature decoding:** In quadrature mode, *both* rising and falling edges of *both* channels are counted. This multiplies the effective count by 4. $$\theta_{effective} = \dfrac{\theta_{basic}}{4} = \dfrac{0.144°}{4}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{effective} = 0.036° \text{ per count}}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to radians (KTU commonly asks for SI):** $$\theta_{effective} = 0.036° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} \approx 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/count}$$ **Step 4 — Joint linear travel per count (if link radius r = 0.15 m):** $$s_{count} = r \cdot \theta_{effective} = 0.15 \times 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \approx 9.42 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{s_{count} \approx 94.2\ \mu\text{m per encoder count}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** A high-LPR encoder with ×4 mode is essential when joint accuracy is critical (e.g., SCARA assembly robots). This is why industrial robots often specify encoders with 10,000+ LPR. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Velocity Measurement by M-Method **Problem:** Encoder has **LPR = 1000**; sample time window **Δt = 10 ms**; in one window, **ΔN = 250** pulses are counted. **Step 1 — Revolutions per second:** $$f_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta N}{LPR \cdot \Delta t} = \dfrac{250}{1000 \times 0.01}$$ $$f_{rev} = \dfrac{250}{10} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to rad/s:** $$\omega = 2\pi f_{rev} = 2\pi \times 25$$ $$\boxed{\omega = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to RPM:** $$N_{rpm} = f_{rev} \times 60 = 25 \times 60 = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$ **Conversion Logic:** The M-method is preferred when speed is relatively constant and high — the error is dominated by ±1 count quantization, which becomes *less significant* as ΔN grows. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Accelerometer Steady-State Displacement **Given:** Proof mass m = 0.005 kg, spring k = 2000 N/m, applied acceleration a = 5g = 5 × 9.81 = 49.05 m/s². Assume operation far below natural frequency (quasi-static). **Step 1 — Natural frequency check:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2000}{0.005}} = \sqrt{400000} \approx 632.5 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\Rightarrow f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi) \approx 100.7 \text{ Hz}$$ For operating frequencies well below 10 Hz (typical industrial robot motion), the quasi-static assumption holds. **Step 2 — Quasi-static displacement:** At $\omega \ll \omega_n$, the inertial term $m\omega^2$ and damping term are negligible. The steady-state equation reduces to: $$kx = ma \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{ma}{k}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:** $$x = \dfrac{0.005 \times 49.05}{2000} = \dfrac{0.24525}{2000}$$ $$\boxed{x \approx 1.226 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 122.6\ \mu\text{m}}$$ **Step 4 — Force on spring (for sensor calibration):** $$F = kx = 2000 \times 1.226 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2453 \text{ N}$$ Check: $F = ma = 0.005 \times 49.05 = 0.24525$ N ✓ (matches). **Conversion Logic:** This micro-displacement is then transduced — by a strain gauge (resistive change) or by a change in capacitance between the proof mass and a fixed plate — into an electrical signal proportional to acceleration. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Force Sensor from Cantilever Strain **Given:** Cantilever flexure of steel with: - Length L = 0.05 m - Width b = 0.02 m - Thickness h = 0.003 m - Young's modulus E = 200 GPa = 2×10¹¹ Pa - Gauge factor $G_f = 2.1$ - Excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V - Applied force F = 50 N at the free end. **Step 1 — Surface strain at the fixed-end (root) of the cantilever:** The bending moment at the root is M = F·L. The maximum surface strain is: $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^2}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 \times 50 \times 0.05}{2 \times 10^{11} \times 0.02 \times (0.003)^2}$$ Denominator: $$2 \times 10^{11} \times 0.02 \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{11} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-7} = 3.6 \times 10^{4}$$ Numerator: $6 \times 50 \times 0.05 = 15$ $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{15}{3.6 \times 10^4} \approx 4.167 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 3 — Resistance change fraction:** $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \approx 8.75 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4 — Full-bridge output voltage:** For a full bridge, the output is: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$ Wait — the standard full-bridge formula gives: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$ Substituting: $$V_{out} = 5 \times 2.1 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 4.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.375 \text{ mV}}$$ **Step 5 — Sensitivity (V/N):** $$S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{F} = \dfrac{4.375 \text{ mV}}{50 \text{ N}} = 87.5\ \mu\text{V/N}$$ **Conversion Logic:** This millivolt-level output is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128, AD620) with gain ~100–1000 before being digitized by the robot controller's ADC. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for KTU Problems | Application | Best Internal Sensor | Why | Key Spec to Specify | |-------------|---------------------|-----|---------------------| | 6-DOF arm joint angle feedback | Absolute optical encoder | No power-loss calibration, fast | Bits (e.g., 16-bit → 0.0055° res.) | | Cheap hobby servo angle | Potentiometer | Cost-effective, absolute | Resistance range, linearity | | Harsh environment (foundry robot) | Resolver | Rugged, oil/vibration tolerant | Excitation freq., accuracy in arc-min | | High-speed motor control (CNC) | DC tacho + encoder | True analog velocity + position | Tacho $K_e$, encoder LPR | | Wrist force feedback (assembly) | 6-axis F/T sensor (strain-gauged) | Measures all forces & moments | Range (N, N·m), resolution (mN) | | Vibration/jerk detection in cobots | MEMS accelerometer | Small, low-power, DC-capable | Range (±2g to ±16g), bandwidth | | Prismatic joint position | LVDT | Linear, contactless, rugged | Stroke length, linearity %FS | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Robotic Joint with Internal Sensors ```mermaid flowchart LR CMD[Joint Command
theta desired] --> CTRL[Controller
PID or State-Feedback] CTRL --> AMP[Power Amplifier
Motor Driver] AMP --> M[DC / Servo Motor] M --> GJ[Gear / Harmonic Drive] GJ --> L[Robot Link / Joint] L --> EP[Encoder Position Sensor] L --> ES[Encoder Velocity Derived] L --> ACC[MEMS Accelerometer] L --> FS[Strain Gauge Force Sensor] EP --> FB[Feedback Signal Path] ES --> FB ACC --> FB FS --> FB FB --> CTRL CTRL --> ERR[Error Signal e t] ERR --> CTRL ``` **Interpretation:** All four internal sensor types feed back to the controller, which compares measured values with commanded values and adjusts the motor drive accordingly. --- ## 4.2 Quadrature Decoding Logic Diagram (Incremental Encoder) ```mermaid flowchart TD DISC[Optical Disc with
Slits] --> LED[LED Light Source] LED --> PD[Photodetectors A and B] PD --> CA[Channel A Square Wave] PD --> CB[Channel B Square Wave] CA --> X4[X4 Quadrature Decoder] CB --> X4 X4 --> CNT[Up/Down Counter] CNT --> DIR{Direction Logic
A leads B} DIR -->|Yes| UP[Count UP] DIR -->|No| DN[Count DOWN] UP --> ANG[Joint Angle Output] DN --> ANG ``` --- ## 4.3 Accelerometer Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR ACC_IN[External Acceleration a] --> MASS[Proof Mass m] MASS --> SPRING[Spring with stiffness k] MASS --> DAMPER[Dashpot with damping c] SPRING --> REL[Relative Displacement x] DAMPER --> REL REL --> TRD[Transducer
Piezo / Piezoresistive / Capacitive] TRD --> SIG[Conditioned Voltage Signal] SIG --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier] AMP --> ADC[ADC to Controller] ``` --- ## 4.4 Six-Axis Force/Torque Sensor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB FLG[Flange Mounted to Robot Wrist] --> EE[End Effector / Tool] EE --> FTS[6-Axis F or T Sensor] FTS --> CYL[Stiff Elastic Cylinder
with Cutouts] CYL --> SG1[Strain Gauge Set 1
Detects Fx Fy] CYL --> SG2[Strain Gauge Set 2
Detects Fz] CYL --> SG3[Strain Gauge Set 3
Detects Mx My Mz] SG1 --> BR[Wheatstone Bridges x8] SG2 --> BR SG3 --> BR BR --> AMP[Multi-Channel Amplifier] AMP --> DSP[DSP / Microcontroller] DSP --> CAL[Cross-Coupling
Calibration Matrix] CAL --> OUT[6x1 Vector Fx Fy Fz Mx My Mz] OUT --> CT[Robot Force Controller] ``` --- ## 4.5 Sensor Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Need an Internal Sensor] --> Q1{What is being measured?} Q1 -->|Angle or Linear Position| Q2{Environment?} Q1 -->|Speed| VEL[Use Encoder Differentiation or Tachometer] Q1 -->|Acceleration| ACC[Use MEMS or Piezoelectric Accelerometer] Q1 -->|Force or Torque| F[Use Strain-Gauge F or T Sensor] Q2 -->|Clean Industrial| Q3{Continuous or One-time Reading?} Q2 -->|Harsh High Temp| RES[Use Resolver] Q3 -->|Continuous Relative| INC[Incremental Encoder] Q3 -->|Absolute after Power-Loss| ABS[Absolute Encoder] Q3 -->|Low Cost Linear| POT[Potentiometer] Q3 -->|Long Stroke Linear| LVDT[LVDT] VEL --> DONE[Sensor Selected] ACC --> DONE F --> DONE RES --> DONE INC --> DONE ABS --> DONE POT --> DONE LVDT --> DONE ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"Distinguish between incremental and absolute encoders. In which robotic application would you prefer an absolute encoder over an incremental one, and why?"** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** | Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder | |---------|--------------------|--------------------| | Output | Pulses; position obtained by counting | Unique digital word per angle | | Power loss | Loses position reference | Retains position via battery/memory | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Resolution | Set by LPR; ×4 in quadrature | Set by bit count: $360°/2^n$ | **Application (1 mark):** In **satellite antenna-pointing robots** or **surgical robots** — wherever the system may be powered off and on, the absolute encoder gives immediate angular knowledge without a homing sequence, improving safety and reducing startup time. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Why is it unsuitable for measuring DC acceleration?"** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** A piezoelectric accelerometer uses a **proof mass** pressing on a **piezoelectric crystal** (e.g., quartz or PZT). When the sensor housing accelerates, the inertia of the mass exerts a force on the crystal. The crystal generates a **charge** $Q = d \cdot F$ proportional to the applied force, and hence to acceleration (since $F = ma$). **Why not DC (1 mark):** Piezoelectric materials only generate charge *when the force changes* (dynamic response). Under steady DC acceleration, the charge leaks away through the internal capacitance and input impedance of the readout circuit, giving a *drifting zero* — hence no reliable DC response. For DC acceleration, a **capacitive MEMS** or **piezoresistive** accelerometer is used. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **incremental optical encoder**. Derive the expression for the angular resolution of a quadrature-decoded encoder with LPR lines per revolution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A robot joint uses a DC tachogenerator with voltage constant $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad. The joint rotates at a constant speed and the tacho output is measured as 7.5 V. Find: (i) The angular velocity in rad/s. (ii) The corresponding speed in RPM.
