(iii) If the encoder on the same joint has 1024 LPR operating in ×4 mode, how many pulses are generated in 0.5 seconds? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Working (≈ 4 marks):** - An incremental optical encoder consists of a disc patterned with **alternating opaque and transparent radial lines**, mounted on the rotating shaft. - A **light source (LED)** is placed on one side, and **photodetectors** on the other side of the disc. - As the disc rotates, the light beam is chopped, producing **square-wave pulses** at the photodetector output. - **Two photodetectors** offset by 90° produce **Channel A and Channel B** in **quadrature**. - The phase relationship (A leads B or B leads A) determines **direction of rotation**. - **Index pulse** (once per revolution) provides a reference for homing. **Resolution Derivation (≈ 3 marks):** Basic resolution (one channel, one edge): $$\theta_{basic} = \dfrac{360°}{LPR}$$ In **×4 quadrature**, both rising and falling edges of both channels are counted: $$\theta_{eff} = \dfrac{\theta_{basic}}{4} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \times LPR} = \dfrac{90°}{LPR}$$ **Final boxed formula:** $$\boxed{\theta_{eff} = \dfrac{90°}{LPR} \text{ per count, or } \dfrac{\pi}{2 \cdot LPR} \text{ rad/count}}$$ --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i)** Using $E = K_e \cdot \omega$: $$\omega = \dfrac{E}{K_e} = \dfrac{7.5}{0.05}$$ $$\boxed{\omega = 150 \text{ rad/s}}$$ **(ii)** Convert to RPM: $$N_{rpm} = \omega \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 150 \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{9000}{2\pi} = \dfrac{4500}{\pi}$$ $$\boxed{N_{rpm} \approx 1432.4 \text{ RPM}}$$ **(iii)** In ×4 mode, pulses per revolution = 4 × LPR = 4 × 1024 = 4096. Revolutions in 0.5 s: $N_{rev} = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} \times t = \dfrac{150}{2\pi} \times 0.5 = \dfrac{75}{2\pi} \approx 11.937$ rev. Total pulses: $N_{pulses} = 4096 \times 11.937$ $$\boxed{N_{pulses} \approx 48{,}895 \text{ pulses}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **six-axis force/torque (F/T) sensor** used in robotic wrists. How is the cross-coupling between axes compensated? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cantilever-beam force sensor has the following specifications: - Length L = 80 mm, width b = 25 mm, thickness h = 4 mm - Young's modulus E = 210 GPa - Gauge factor $G_f = 2.0$ - Bridge excitation $V_{ex} = 10$ V - Applied force F = 100 N at the free end. Find: (i) The strain at the root of the cantilever. (ii) The output voltage of a full Wheatstone bridge. (iii) The sensitivity in mV/N. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Six-Axis F/T Sensor (≈ 7 marks):** **Construction:** - A **stiff elastic cylinder** (often with three or four machined cutouts/web patterns to localise deformation) is sandwiched between the robot **wrist flange** and the **end-effector/tool flange**. - The cylinder is instrumented with **multiple strain gauges** (typically 8 or more) bonded at strategic locations. - The cutout pattern allows small, measurable elastic deformations when forces/moments are applied. **Working Principle:** - Each applied load ($F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$) produces a unique pattern of strains at the gauge locations. - Each gauge is wired into a **Wheatstone bridge** for high signal-to-noise ratio. - The six bridge outputs are digitised and fed to a microcontroller. **Cross-Coupling Compensation (≈ 2 marks):** - In an ideal sensor, each gauge responds to *one* axis only, but in practice, a force on the X-axis also produces *small* signals in Y and Z channels — this is called **cross-talk** or **cross-coupling**. - Compensation is done by a **calibration matrix [C] (6×6)** that maps the raw voltages to the actual loads: $$\begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \\ M_x \\ M_y \\ M_z \end{bmatrix} = [C]_{6 \times 6} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \\ V_3 \\ V_4 \\ V_5 \\ V_6 \end{bmatrix}$$ - The matrix [C] is determined experimentally using a **factorial calibration** — applying known loads along each axis and recording the outputs. --- **(b) Cantilever Force Sensor Calculation:** **Given:** L = 0.08 m, b = 0.025 m, h = 0.004 m, E = 2.1×10¹¹ Pa, $G_f = 2.0$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V, F = 100 N. **(i) Strain at the root:** $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^2}$$ Denominator calculation: $$E b h^2 = 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 0.025 \times (0.004)^2$$ $$= 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 0.025 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$= 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$= 8.4 \times 10^{4} = 84{,}000$$ Numerator: $6 \times 100 \times 0.08 = 48$ $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{48}{84{,}000} = 5.714 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 571.4\ \mu\varepsilon}$$ **(ii) Full-bridge output voltage:** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 10 \times 2.0 \times 5.714 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$V_{out} = 1.143 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 11.43 \text{ mV}}$$ **(iii) Sensitivity:** $$S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{F} = \dfrac{11.43 \text{ mV}}{100 \text{ N}}$$ $$\boxed{S = 0.1143 \text{ mV/N} = 114.3\ \mu\text{V/N}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting ×4 in quadrature** — Most students compute resolution using `360/LPR` and lose 2 marks. Always state *explicitly* whether ×2 or ×4 decoding is being used. > 2. **Unit mix-up in tacho problems** — $K_e$ in V·s/rad **requires rad/s**, *not* RPM. Convert RPM → rad/s *first* using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. > 3. **Strain gauge formula sign confusion** — $\Delta R/R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon$ is *dimensionless* (not in %). A strain of `500 microstrain` = $500 \times 10^{-6}$, not 500. > 4. **Cantilever flexure formula misuse** — The formula $\varepsilon = 6FL/(Ebh^2)$ is for *rectangular cross-section*. For circular cross-sections, a different formula is used. > 5. **Failing to state the "why" in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners reward *explanation* of the transduction principle and a *labelled diagram*. A numerical answer without a principle explanation typically caps the score near 60%. > 6. **Confusing "natural frequency" with "operating frequency" in accelerometers** — Always state the quasi-static assumption ($\omega \ll \omega_n$) explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Internal sensors measure the **robot's own state** — joint angle, joint velocity, joint acceleration, and joint/end-effector force. - ✅ **Position sensors:** Potentiometer (cheap, limited), Incremental encoder (industrial standard, needs homing), Absolute encoder (no homing, expensive), Resolver (rugged), LVDT (linear), Hall-effect (motor commutation). - ✅ **Encoder resolution (×4 mode):** $\theta_{eff} = 90°/\text{LPR}$. - ✅ **DC Tachogenerator:** $E = K_e \cdot \omega$. - ✅ **Encoder-derived velocity (M-method):** $\omega = \Delta N / (LPR \cdot \Delta t)$. - ✅ **Accelerometer equation (quasi-static):** $x = -m a / k$; natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. - ✅ **Piezoelectric accelerometers** have no DC response — use MEMS for DC. - ✅ **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, with $G_f \approx 2.0$ for metal foil. - ✅ **Cantilever flexure strain:** $\varepsilon = 6 F L / (E b h^2)$. - ✅ **Full-bridge output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$. - ✅ **Six-axis F/T sensor** requires a 6×6 calibration matrix to remove cross-coupling. - ✅ **Force-controlled assembly** (e.g., peg-in-hole) is impossible without wrist F/T sensors — position-only robots *cannot* detect contact. - ✅ **Physical constants to memorise:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $E_{\text{steel}} \approx 200$ GPa, $G_f \approx 2.0$. - ✅ **Always convert** to SI units (rad/s, N, m) before substituting into formulas in KTU numerical problems. --- > **END OF NOTES — Internal Sensors: Position, Velocity, Acceleration, Force (Module 2, PCADT502)**

topics.title: External Sensors - Contact Type, Non-Contact Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Sensors - Contact Type, Non-Contact Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – External Sensors in Robotics: Contact & Non-Contact Types

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are External Sensors?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *External sensors* (also called **exteroceptive sensors** or **end-effector/perception sensors**) are devices mounted on or around a robotic manipulator that measure physical quantities from the robot's *external environment* to enable perception, interaction, and intelligent decision-making. In KTU 2024 syllabus, they are classified based on the **physical principle of contact** with the target object: **Contact-type** and **Non-contact-type**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal sensors** (proprioceptive) measure the robot's *own* state — joint angles, velocities, motor currents (e.g., encoders, resolvers, potentiometers). **External sensors** (exteroceptive) measure the *surrounding world* — proximity, distance, force, vision, sound. This distinction is a frequent KTU short-answer question (3 marks).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot's Five Senses"

Imagine a human working in a dark, unfamiliar room:

- **Contact sensors** = the human's *fingertips and skin* — they only know something exists when they physically **touch** it (pressure, texture, slip).
- **Non-contact sensors** = the human's *eyes and ears* — they detect objects **from a distance**, without touching (light, sound waves, magnetic fields).

Just as a blindfolded person must grope to understand a surface (contact), and a sighted person can scan a room visually (non-contact), a robot uses the same two strategies. Modern industrial robots *fuse* both types (sensor fusion) to handle complex tasks like assembly, welding, and obstacle avoidance.

### 1.3 KTU 2024 Classification Overview

| Category | Contact Type Sensors | Non-Contact Type Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Interaction | Physical touch required | No physical touch required |
| Sensing Medium | Mechanical deflection / strain | EM waves, sound, magnetic field, light |
| Typical Use | Assembly, gripping, polishing | Inspection, navigation, obstacle avoidance |
| Examples | Limit switches, Tactile sensors, Force/Torque, Slip sensors | Proximity (Inductive/Capacitive), Ultrasonic, Laser range, IR, Vision (CCD/CMOS) |
| Response Speed | Generally slower (mechanical) | Generally faster (electromagnetic propagation) |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Memory Hook:** Think **"TOUCH"** vs **"SCAN"**. Touch sensors **deform mechanically** (Hooke's Law: $F = kx$); Non-contact sensors **emit and receive waves** (wave equation: $v = f\lambda$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Contact-Type Sensors (Detailed Breakdown)

Contact sensors require the end-effector or a probe to **physically touch** the workpiece or environment. The sensor transduces mechanical deformation, force, or displacement into an electrical signal.

### 2.1.1 Limit Switches (Mechanical Binary Contact Sensor)

- **Working Principle:** A mechanical actuator (plunger, lever, roller) is depressed upon contact. Internal contacts open/close a circuit, producing a binary HIGH/LOW signal.
- **Why it works:** Two metal contacts are separated by a spring; contact force compresses the spring and closes the gap — a purely electromechanical ON/OFF switch.
- **Application:** Detecting part arrival on conveyor, end-of-travel limits, fixture presence.
- **Boundary condition:** Rated for a finite number of cycles (typically $10^6$ to $10^7$).

### 2.1.2 Tactile / Touch Sensors (Analog Contact Sensor)

- **Working Principle:** An array of sensitive elements (taxels) measures the **spatial distribution of pressure** across a contact surface. Implemented using:
  - **Conductive elastomer / conductive rubber:** resistance drops as pressure increases ($R \propto 1/F$).
  - **Piezoresistive:** doped silicon whose resistance changes with strain.
  - **Capacitive:** parallel-plate capacitor whose capacitance changes with diaphragm deflection ($C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$).
- **Why it works:** Mechanical pressure $F$ → material deformation $x$ → change in electrical property ($R$, $C$, or voltage $V$).

### 2.1.3 Force / Torque (F/T) Sensors

- **Working Principle:** Strain gauges bonded to a machined elastic element deform under load. Deformation $\rightarrow$ change in resistance $\rightarrow$ measured via **Wheatstone bridge**.
- **Multi-axis F/T sensor:** A cross-shaped (or Maltese cross) elastic body with strain gauges arranged to decouple the six components $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$.
- **Why it works:** Gauge factor $GF$ quantifies sensitivity:
  $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
  where $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the axial strain.

### 2.1.4 Slip Sensors

- **Working Principle:** Detects relative micro-motion between gripped object and finger. Usually a small rolling ball with an encoder, or a vibration/piezo element. Output = slip velocity $v_s$.

### 2.1.5 Other Contact Sensors

- **Potentiometric position sensors** (slider contact on resistive track): $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L_{total}}$
- **Mechanical strain gauges** (foil type, bonded to robot links).

---

## 2.2 Non-Contact-Type Sensors (Detailed Breakdown)

Non-contact sensors detect objects using **wave propagation** (acoustic, optical, electromagnetic) or **field disturbance** (magnetic, capacitive, inductive). No physical deformation of the target is required.

### 2.2.1 Inductive Proximity Sensor

- **Working Principle:** Generates a high-frequency **alternating magnetic field** via a coil. When a *ferrous or conductive* target enters the field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target, which draw energy from the oscillator circuit. The resulting amplitude drop is detected and triggers the output.
- **Detection Range:** Typically **1 mm to 50 mm** (short range).
- **Targets:** Metals only. Non-ferrous metals require special high-frequency versions.
- **Why it works:** Lenz's law — induced eddy currents oppose the source field, damping the oscillator.

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Proximity Sensor

- **Working Principle:** One electrode is the sensor face; the target forms the other plate of a capacitor (with ground/earth as return). As distance $d$ decreases, **capacitance increases**:
  $$C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{A}{d}$$
  This changes the frequency of an RC oscillator, which is detected.
- **Detection Range:** **0 to 50 mm**; detects metals, plastics, wood, liquids, powders — *any material with dielectric constant different from air*.

### 2.2.3 Ultrasonic / Acoustic Sensor

- **Working Principle:** Emits a high-frequency sound pulse (typically **40 kHz**, beyond human hearing). Measures the **time-of-flight (ToF)** of the echo reflected from the target.
- **Range Equation:**
  $$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$$
  with $v_{sound} \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$ at 20 °C air.
- **Why the factor of 2?** Sound travels *to* the target and *back*, so the measured time corresponds to a round trip.
- **Range:** **0.1 m to 10 m**; insensitive to color, transparency, dust.

### 2.2.4 Infrared (IR) Sensor

- **Working Principle:** Emits IR light from an LED; a photodiode/phototransistor detects reflected light. Triangulation-based IR sensors give more accurate range.
- **Triangulation Equation:**
  $$d = \frac{b \cdot f}{x}$$
  where $b$ = baseline, $f$ = focal length, $x$ = image spot displacement on PSD.
- **Range:** mm to a few meters; sensitive to surface color/reflectivity.

### 2.2.5 Laser Range Finder (LiDAR / Time-of-Flight Laser)

- **Working Principle:** Emits a laser pulse; measures ToF or phase shift of reflected beam.
- **Phase-shift ranging:**
  $$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4\pi f}$$
  where $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $\Delta\phi$ = phase difference, $f$ = modulation frequency.
- **Range:** Up to hundreds of meters; mm-level accuracy.

### 2.2.6 Vision Sensors (CCD / CMOS Cameras)

- **Working Principle:** 2-D array of photosensitive pixels captures reflected light from the scene. Image processing (edge detection, blob analysis, template matching) extracts object features.
- **Why it matters:** Mimics the human eye — provides the richest information (color, shape, texture, position, orientation).

### 2.2.7 Magnetic / Hall-Effect Sensor

- **Working Principle:** A **Hall element** outputs a voltage proportional to the magnetic flux density $B$ perpendicular to it:
  $$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t} = R_H \cdot \frac{I \cdot B}{t}$$
  Detects permanent magnets — used to sense piston position in pneumatic cylinders, motor speed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **all high-yield formulas** for this topic. Memorize symbols, units, and boundary conditions. KTU problems (especially 14-mark derivations) test exactly these.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units | Applicable Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = kx$ | Hooke's Law: contact force ∝ deflection | $N, N/m, m$ | Tactile, F/T, Limit |
| 2 | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge Factor (sensitivity) | dimensionless | Strain-gauge F/T |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Potentiometric position | $V, m$ | Potentiometer |
| 4 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \dfrac{A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | $F$ | Capacitive proximity |
| 5 | $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$ | Ultrasonic range via ToF | $m, m/s, s$ | Ultrasonic |
| 6 | $d = \dfrac{b \cdot f}{x}$ | Triangulation range | $m$ | IR/Laser triangulation |
| 7 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4\pi f}$ | Phase-shift laser ranging | $m$ | Laser range finder |
| 8 | $V_H = R_H \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot B}{t}$ | Hall voltage | $V$ | Hall-effect |
| 9 | $v = f \lambda$ | Universal wave equation | $m/s, Hz, m$ | All wave-based |
| 10 | $v_{sound} \approx 331 + 0.6 T$ | Speed of sound in air vs temperature | $m/s, °C$ | Ultrasonic |
| 11 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC oscillator frequency (proximity) | $Hz$ | Inductive/Capacitive proximity |

> [!TIP]
> **Constants to remember:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $v_{sound} \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$ (at 20 °C air), $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Range Measurement via Time-of-Flight

**Problem Statement:** A robot-mounted ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse toward an obstacle. The echo is received after **6 ms**. Calculate the distance to the obstacle, given the speed of sound in air is **343 m/s** at 20 °C.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the physics principle.**

The ultrasonic sensor uses the **pulse-echo time-of-flight (ToF)** principle. The emitted sound wave travels to the target, reflects, and returns to the receiver. The total measured time $t_{ToF}$ corresponds to a *round trip*.

**Step 2 — Write the governing equation.**

Let $d$ = one-way distance to the target. The total path length travelled by the sound wave is $2d$. Therefore:

$$
\text{Total distance travelled} = v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF} = 2d
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $d$.**

$$
d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

Given $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$ and $t_{ToF} = 6 \text{ ms} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$:

$$
d = \frac{343 \text{ m/s} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator first.**

$$
343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} = 2058 \times 10^{-3} = 2.058 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by 2.**

$$
d = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — State the final answer with units.**

$$
\boxed{d \approx 1.03 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Students often forget the factor of 2 and report $d = 2.06$ m — this is the **most common 1-mark loss** in KTU ultrasonic problems. Always show the round-trip logic explicitly in your answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitance Change in a Capacitive Proximity Sensor

**Problem Statement:** A capacitive proximity sensor has electrode area $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2$ and detects a plastic target. As the target approaches, the gap $d$ changes from $5 \text{ mm}$ to $1 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the *change in capacitance*, given the dielectric constant of the plastic is $\varepsilon_r = 3.5$ and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the parallel-plate capacitor formula.**

$$
C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{A}{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert all quantities to SI units.**

$$
A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
d_1 = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}, \quad d_2 = 1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_1$ (initial capacitance at $d_1 = 5$ mm).**

$$
C_1 = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 3.5 \times \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify step by step.**

First, the fraction:

$$
\frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

Then multiply:

$$
C_1 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_1 = 8.854 \times 3.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-12-3}
$$

$$
C_1 = 154.945 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 1.55 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_2$ (capacitance at $d_2 = 1$ mm).**

By the same equation with $d_2$:

$$
C_2 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.5 \times \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
C_2 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.5 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_2 = 774.725 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 7.75 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the change in capacitance.**

$$
\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = 7.75 \times 10^{-13} - 1.55 \times 10^{-13} = 6.20 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}
$$

**Step 7 — State the final result.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta C = 0.62 \text{ pF} = 620 \text{ fF}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does $C$ increase as $d$ decreases?** Because $C \propto 1/d$. Halving the gap doubles the capacitance. This relationship is the *heart* of the sensor's working principle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hall-Effect Voltage in a Magnetic Proximity Sensor

**Problem Statement:** A Hall-effect sensor has a strip thickness $t = 0.5 \text{ mm}$. A control current $I = 5 \text{ mA}$ flows through it, perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B = 0.3 \text{ T}$. The Hall coefficient of the material is $R_H = 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^3/\text{C}$. Compute the Hall voltage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Hall voltage equation.**

$$
V_H = R_H \cdot \frac{I \cdot B}{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute SI values.**

$$
I = 5 \text{ mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}, \quad t = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
I \cdot B = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A·T}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by thickness.**

$$
\frac{I \cdot B}{t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3 \text{ A·T/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $R_H$.**

$$
V_H = 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 3 = 3.6 \times 10^{-10} \text{ V} = 0.36 \text{ nV}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\boxed{V_H = 0.36 \text{ nV}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result is extremely small. In practice, Hall sensors use **semiconductors** (high $R_H$) and **integrated amplifiers**. Pure metals give nV-level signals — another reason Si/InSb Hall ICs dominate industry.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Contact Type | Non-Contact Type |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing medium | Mechanical (force/displacement) | EM / acoustic waves, fields |
| Need physical contact? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Target dependency | Independent of material (mostly) | Strongly material-dependent (reflectivity, $\varepsilon_r$, $\mu_r$) |
| Typical range | $0$ to few cm | mm to hundreds of m |
| Response time | ms (mechanical) | $\mu$s to ns (electronic) |
| Wear and tear | Yes (limited lifetime) | Minimal (no contact) |
| Accuracy | High at point of contact | Depends on beam width, environmental noise |
| Cost | Generally low | Higher (lasers, cameras) |
| Best for | Assembly, force feedback, gripping | Inspection, navigation, mapping |
| Example in KTU syllabus | Tactile array, F/T sensor, Limit switch | Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, Camera |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of External Sensors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Sensors] --> B[Contact Type]
    A --> C[Non-Contact Type]
    B --> B1[Limit Switch]
    B --> B2[Tactile Sensor]
    B --> B3[Force Torque Sensor]
    B --> B4[Slip Sensor]
    B --> B5[Potentiometric Position]
    C --> C1[Inductive Proximity]
    C --> C2[Capacitive Proximity]
    C --> C3[Ultrasonic]
    C --> C4[Infrared IR]
    C --> C5[Laser LiDAR]
    C --> C6[Vision CCD CMOS]
    C --> C7[Hall Effect Magnetic]
    B2 --> B2a[Conductive Elastomer]
    B2 --> B2b[Piezoresistive]
    B2 --> B2c[Capacitive Array]
    C1 --> C1a[Eddy Current Based]
    C2 --> C2a[Dielectric Change Based]
    C3 --> C3a[Time of Flight]
    C4 --> C4a[Triangulation]
    C5 --> C5a[Pulsed ToF]
    C5 --> C5b[Phase Shift]
    C6 --> C6a[2D Image Processing]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Range Sensor (ToF Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Trigger Pulse] --> B[Microcontroller Timer Start]
    B --> C[40 kHz Burst Generator]
    C --> D[Piezo Transducer Emit]
    D --> E[Air Propagation]
    E --> F[Target Reflection]
    F --> G[Piezo Transducer Receive]
    G --> H[Amplifier and Filter]
    H --> I[Threshold Detector]
    I --> J[Microcontroller Timer Stop]
    J --> K[Distance d equals v times t divided by 2]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Capacitive Proximity Sensor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Oscillator LC Tank] --> B[Sensor Electrode Face]
    B --> C[Target Dielectric]
    C --> D[Change in Capacitance C]
    D --> E[Frequency Shift in Oscillator]
    E --> F[Detector Circuit]
    F --> G[Threshold Comparator]
    G --> H[Output Switch ON or OFF]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Vision-Based Sensor Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Scene Illumination] --> B[Lens Optics]
    B --> C[CCD or CMOS Sensor Array]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Image Buffer RAM]
    E --> F[Image Processing Unit]
    F --> G1[Edge Detection]
    F --> G2[Blob Analysis]
    F --> G3[Template Matching]
    G1 --> H[Object Recognition and Pose]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Robot Controller Decision]
```

## 4.5 Force/Torque Sensor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Applied Load Fx Fy Fz Mx My Mz] --> B[Elastic Element Maltese Cross]
    B --> C1[Strain Gauge 1]
    B --> C2[Strain Gauge 2]
    B --> C3[Strain Gauge 3]
    B --> C4[Strain Gauge 4]
    C1 --> D[Wheatstone Bridge]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Amplifier]
    E --> F[ADC]
    F --> G[Microprocessor]
    G --> H[Decoupled 6 Axis Force Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Aid Insight:** Examiners reward clear, *labelled* diagrams. Always include arrows showing **signal flow direction** and annotate each block with the *transduction principle*. Mermaid graphs are ideal for theory answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proprioceptive sensors** measure the *internal state* of the robot — its own joint angles, velocities, accelerations, and motor currents. **Exteroceptive sensors** (external sensors) measure the *external environment* — distance to objects, force from contact, visual features.
>
> **Examples of proprioceptive:** (1) Optical encoder on a motor shaft. (2) Potentiometer at a joint.
> **Examples of exteroceptive:** (1) Ultrasonic range sensor. (2) Vision camera (CCD).

*(1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for proprioceptive examples, 1 mark for exteroceptive examples.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an inductive proximity sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An inductive proximity sensor consists of a **coil**, an **LC oscillator**, a **detector circuit**, and a **solid-state output switch**. When a metallic target enters the alternating magnetic field of the coil, **eddy currents** are induced in the target. These currents drain energy from the oscillator, reducing its amplitude. The detector senses the amplitude drop and switches the output. Detection range is typically **1–50 mm**; only metallic (preferably ferrous) targets are detected. (3 marks: 1 for parts, 1 for eddy current principle, 1 for range/material note.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the working principle of an **ultrasonic range sensor** with a block diagram. Derive the distance measurement equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A robot-mounted ultrasonic sensor operates at 40 kHz in air. It emits a burst that returns after 12 ms. Compute (i) the distance to the obstacle, and (ii) the wavelength of the emitted wave. (Take $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s.) (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

The ultrasonic range sensor is a **pulse-echo time-of-flight** device. A piezoelectric crystal (transducer) is excited by a high-voltage pulse at its resonant frequency (typically 40 kHz), producing a short acoustic burst. The burst propagates through air at the speed of sound, strikes the target, and is partially reflected back. The same (or a second) piezoelectric transducer converts the returning acoustic echo into a small electrical signal. A detector circuit identifies the echo, and a timer circuit measures the elapsed time between emission and reception.

**Distance Equation (2 marks):**

Let $t_{ToF}$ be the measured time of flight and $v_{sound}$ the speed of sound. The acoustic wave travels a total distance $2d$ (to and back), so:

$$
d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}
$$

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to Section 4.2)

Emitter → 40 kHz Burst Generator → Transducer → Air Path → Target → Reflected Echo → Receiving Transducer → Amplifier → Threshold Detector → Timer → Microcontroller → Distance Output.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given:** $f = 40 \text{ kHz} = 40{,}000 \text{ Hz}$, $t_{ToF} = 12 \text{ ms} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$, $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$.

**Part (i) — Distance (3 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF}}{2} = \frac{343 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

Numerator: $343 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} = 4116 \times 10^{-3} = 4.116 \text{ m}$.

$$
d = \frac{4.116}{2} = 2.058 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 2.06 \text{ m}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Wavelength (4 marks):**

Using the wave equation $v = f \lambda$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{v_{sound}}{f} = \frac{343}{40{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 8.575 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 8.575 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify external sensors used in robotics. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **capacitive proximity sensor**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A capacitive proximity sensor has electrode area $A = 40 \text{ mm}^2$ and detects a glass target ($\varepsilon_r = 7$). When the target is at $d_1 = 4 \text{ mm}$, the capacitance is $C_1$. When it moves to $d_2 = 2 \text{ mm}$, the capacitance becomes $C_2$. Find (i) $C_1$, (ii) $C_2$, and (iii) the change in capacitance $\Delta C = C_2 - C_1$. (Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.) (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Classification (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram. Two major classes: **Contact** and **Non-contact**. Sub-classes: Contact → limit switch, tactile, F/T, slip. Non-contact → inductive, capacitive, ultrasonic, IR, laser, vision, Hall-effect.

**Working of Capacitive Proximity Sensor (3 marks):**

The sensor face acts as one plate of a capacitor; the target (any material with $\varepsilon_r \neq 1$) acts as the other plate, with the air gap as the dielectric. The sensor face is connected to an **LC oscillator** so its capacitance forms part of the tank circuit. The oscillator frequency is:

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
$$

As the target approaches, the gap $d$ decreases, and the capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A/d)$ increases. The oscillator frequency shifts. A detector compares this shift against a threshold and triggers a solid-state output. The sensor can detect **metals, plastics, glass, wood, liquids, and powders**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.3. Oscillator → Electrode → Target → Capacitance change → Frequency shift → Detector → Comparator → Output.

**Advantages and Limitations (1 mark):**

- *Advantages:* detects non-metals, no contact/wear, fast response, reliable.
- *Limitations:* short range, sensitive to humidity/temperature, requires careful shielding.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Given:** $A = 40 \text{ mm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $\varepsilon_r = 7$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $d_1 = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $d_2 = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

**Part (i) — $C_1$ at $d_1$ (2 marks):**

$$
C_1 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{A}{d_1} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 7 \times \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Fraction: $\frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 10 \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-2}$ m.

$$
C_1 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 7 \times 10^{-2} = 61.978 \times 10^{-14} = 6.198 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_1 \approx 0.620 \text{ pF}}
$$

**Part (ii) — $C_2$ at $d_2$ (2 marks):**

$$
C_2 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 7 \times \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Fraction: $\frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 20 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m.

$$
C_2 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 7 \times 2 \times 10^{-2} = 123.956 \times 10^{-14} = 1.240 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_2 \approx 1.240 \text{ pF}}
$$

**Part (iii) — $\Delta C$ (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = 1.240 \times 10^{-12} - 6.198 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
\Delta C = 12.40 \times 10^{-13} - 6.198 \times 10^{-13} = 6.202 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta C \approx 0.620 \text{ pF}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Since $d$ is halved, $C$ should double, and $\Delta C = C_1$. The numbers confirm this — the answer $0.620$ pF matches $C_1$ exactly, validating the calculation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losses:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic ToF: gives answer double the correct value. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up units** (mm vs m, kHz vs Hz): silent unit mismatch is a **2-mark deduction** in calculation problems.
> 3. **Missing the working principle diagram**: a 7-mark sub-question without a labelled diagram is capped at 5 marks in most valuation keys.
> 4. **Confusing inductive and capacitive proximity** — inductive detects *metals only*; capacitive detects *both metals and dielectrics*. Mixing up is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Writing "Hall effect uses magnetic field"** without specifying **perpendicularity** to the current — partial credit only.
> 6. **Forgetting the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$** in capacitive calculations — answer will be off by a factor of $\varepsilon_r$ (~3–10). Always state the material.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Module 2 — External Sensors: 60-Second Revision Checklist**

- **Two master categories:** Contact (mechanical deformation) vs Non-contact (wave/field).
- **Contact sensors:** limit switch (binary), tactile (array of taxels), F/T (Wheatstone bridge + strain gauge), slip (micro-motion), potentiometric.
- **Key contact formula:** $F = kx$ (Hooke's Law), $GF = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$ (gauge factor).
- **Non-contact sensors:** inductive (eddy current, metals only), capacitive (dielectric change, all materials), ultrasonic (ToF, $d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{ToF} / 2$), IR (triangulation $d = bf/x$), laser (ToF or phase), vision (2D image processing), Hall effect ($V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B / t$).
- **Universal constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **Hall effect voltage depends on perpendicular $B$ and current $I$** — remember the perpendicularity.
- **Speed of sound depends on temperature:** $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6T$ (m/s, $T$ in °C).
- **Round-trip factor of 2** is non-negotiable for ToF sensors.
- **Sensor fusion** is the industrial trend — combine tactile + vision + range for robust manipulation.
- **KTU-favorite trick questions:**
  - *Inductive proximity can detect plastic?* → **No** (only metals).
  - *Capacitive proximity can detect water level?* → **Yes** ($\varepsilon_r$ of water is high).
  - *Ultrasonic range = v × t?* → **No**, it is $v \times t / 2$ (round trip).
  - *Strain gauge measures force directly?* → **No**, it measures strain; force is inferred.
- **High-yield engineering applications:** robotic assembly (tactile + F/T), AGV navigation (ultrasonic + LiDAR), bin picking (vision + IR), part sorting (capacitive + inductive), welding seam tracking (laser + vision).
- **Performance selection rule of thumb:**
  - Need < 50 mm range + metal target → **inductive**.
  - Need < 50 mm range + non-metal target → **capacitive**.
  - Need 0.1–10 m range, low cost → **ultrasonic**.
  - Need mm-accuracy, long range → **laser/LiDAR**.
  - Need 2D/3D scene info → **vision system**.
  - Need force feedback during contact → **strain-gauge F/T** or **tactile array**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Cameras - CCD, CMOS, Omnidirectional
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Cameras - CCD, CMOS, Omnidirectional

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📷 Digital Cameras in Robotics: CCD, CMOS & Omnidirectional Vision Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Anchor Concept:** Digital cameras are the **primary exteroceptive sensors** in modern robotic systems, providing dense, high-bandwidth 2D (and reconstructed 3D) information about the robot's operating environment. KTU 2024 emphasizes the **physical transduction mechanism** (photon → electron → voltage), the **on-chip architecture** (CCD vs. CMOS), and the **wide field-of-view (FOV) geometric strategies** used in omnidirectional vision.

## 1.1 What is a Digital Camera in Robotics?

A **digital camera** in the robotics context is an electro-optical transducer that converts incoming photons from a scene into a discrete, digitized 2D intensity (or multi-spectral) array — an *image* $I(x, y, t)$ — suitable for real-time processing by perception algorithms (SLAM, object detection, visual odometry).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"A digital imaging sensor is a 2D array of photosensitive elements (pixels) on a semiconductor substrate, which, when integrated with appropriate optics and readout electronics, performs spatio-temporal sampling of the radiant flux $Φ_e(x, y, t)$ reflected or emitted by a scene, producing a digital image $I_{ij} \in \mathbb{Z}^{M \times N}$ whose intensity values are proportional to the incident irradiance."*

## 1.2 The Intuition: Camera = "Mechanical Retina"

Think of a digital camera as an **artificial, electronic human eye**:

| Human Eye Analogue | Digital Camera Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cornea + Lens (focuses light) | Objective lens system (focuses light) |
| Iris (aperture control) | Mechanical/electronic aperture (f-stop) |
| Retina (photoreceptor mosaic) | Focal plane sensor array (CCD/CMOS) |
| Rods & Cones (spectral response) | RGB Bayer filter over pixels |
| Optic nerve (transmits signals) | Readout electronics + ADC |
| Visual cortex (image processing) | On-board / host computer algorithms |

**In one sentence:** The camera's job is *capture the "shape" of light* in a way that a robot (or computer) can understand, much like how your eye captures the shape of the world and your brain decodes it.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Robotic Vision Hardware

KTU Module 2 specifically focuses on **three architectural families**:

1. **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** — *Serial readout, high fidelity, mature technology*.
2. **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** — *Per-pixel amplification, low power, dominant in modern robotics*.
3. **Omnidirectional Cameras** — *Wide FOV (often $360°$ horizontal) using catadioptric, dioptric, or polydioptric optical configurations*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Physics: Photon-to-Electron Conversion

Every digital image sensor — CCD or CMOS — relies on the **internal photoelectric effect** in silicon.

### Step-by-Step Photon Capture Logic

1. **Photon Incidence** — A photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ strikes a silicon photodiode. Capture requires $E_{ph} > E_g$ (silicon bandgap, **1.12 eV at 300 K**), corresponding to $\lambda < 1100$ nm.
2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation** — The absorbed photon frees a conduction electron, leaving a hole. This produces a *photoelectron* in the pixel's potential well.
3. **Charge Accumulation** — Over the integration (exposure) time $t_{int}$, photoelectrons accumulate: $N_{e^-} = \eta \cdot \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{pixel} \cdot t_{int}$, where $\eta$ is the **quantum efficiency** and $\Phi_{ph}$ is the photon flux density.
4. **Charge Readout** — The accumulated packet is transferred to an output node and converted to a voltage.
5. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Voltage is digitized to a **bit-depth** value (typically 8, 10, 12, or 16 bits).

## 2.2 CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) — Architecture

In a **CCD**, every pixel's photo-charge is **bucket-brigade transferred** across the chip to a single output amplifier at the corner. This is the defining architectural feature.

### CCD Operational Phases

- **Photo-generation phase:** Photons generate electron-hole pairs in the depletion region of each MOS capacitor pixel.
- **Charge storage phase:** Electrons are trapped in a potential well beneath the gate electrode (held at a positive bias).
- **Charge transfer phase:** Gate voltages are clocked in a precise 3- or 4-phase sequence ($\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3, \phi_4$), shifting charge packets row-by-row to the output register.
- **Charge-to-voltage conversion:** A floating diffusion converts charge $Q$ to voltage $V = Q / C_{FD}$, where $C_{FD}$ is the floating diffusion capacitance (typically $\sim 10$ fF).
- **Amplification & digitization:** A single on-chip output amplifier and external ADC digitize the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key CCD Property:** Because only **one** output amplifier is used, all pixels share the *same* gain and noise floor, yielding exceptionally **uniform images** with very **low temporal noise** (down to **1–3 e⁻ RMS**).

## 2.3 CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) — Architecture

In a **CMOS (Active Pixel Sensor — APS)** image sensor, *each pixel* contains its **own amplifier and readout transistor circuitry**, fabricated using standard CMOS processes.

### CMOS Pixel Readout

- **Per-pixel amplification:** Each pixel contains typically 3–4 transistors (the canonical **4T pixel**: Transfer gate, Reset, Source-follower, Row-select).
- **Column-parallel ADCs:** Modern CMOS sensors place ADCs at the bottom of each column for **massive parallelism**.
- **Random addressing:** Any pixel can be read non-destructively and non-sequentially via **row/column decoders** (enabling **region-of-interest readout** and **global vs. rolling shutter** modes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key CMOS Property:** **Per-pixel amplification** gives CMOS enormous advantages in **speed, power, and integration**, but introduces **Fixed-Pattern Noise (FPN)** due to transistor mismatches, requiring **Correlated Double Sampling (CDS)**.

## 2.4 CCD vs. CMOS — The KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | CCD | CMOS (APS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Output stage** | Single shared amplifier | Per-pixel amplifier |
| **Readout mechanism** | Serial charge transfer (bucket-brigade) | Random-access addressing |
| **Temporal noise** | Very low (1–3 e⁻) | Higher (5–20 e⁻) |
| **Fixed-Pattern Noise (FPN)** | Negligible | Requires CDS correction |
| **Power consumption** | High (multiple clock phases) | Low (10–100× less) |
| **Quantum Efficiency (QE)** | Higher (back-illuminated >90%) | Slightly lower (front-illuminated ~50–70%) |
| **Shutter type** | Global (full-frame, frame-transfer) or rolling | Rolling (default) or global (GS-CMOS) |
| **Fill factor** | ~100% (with micro-lens) | Lower (transistors occupy area), improveable with micro-lens |
| **Integration with logic** | Difficult (special process) | Excellent (standard CMOS) |
| **On-chip functions** | Limited (just output buffer) | ADC, ISP, HDR, exposure control |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower (mass-produced) |
| **Robotic application** | Scientific, astronomy, high-end machine vision | Mobile robots, drones, smartphones, automotive |

## 2.5 Omnidirectional Cameras — Geometric Strategies

An **omnidirectional camera** captures a **hemispherical or full-spherical field of view** in a single frame. KTU Module 2 covers three implementation strategies:

### (a) **Catadioptric** — Mirror + Lens
A curved mirror (hyperbolic, parabolic, or spherical) is placed in front of a conventional perspective camera. The mirror's curvature mathematically guarantees a single **effective viewpoint** (the **center of projection**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Viewpoint (SVP) Condition:** For a catadioptric system, the camera's center $C$, the mirror's focal point $F_m$, and the mirror's focus $F$ must be **collinear**. This holds for:
> - **Parabolic mirror** + **orthographic lens**
> - **Hyperbolic mirror** + **perspective lens**
> - **Spherical mirror** (approximate, SVP only at the equator)

### (b) **Dioptric** — Fish-eye Lens
A complex multi-element lens produces ultra-wide FOV ($180°$–$220°$) by bending rays. **No mirror** is used. The SVP condition generally *does not hold* for fish-eye lenses, causing geometric distortions in reconstruction.

### (c) **Polydioptric** — Multi-Camera Array
Multiple cameras (e.g., 4–6) are arranged to cover a full sphere. Examples: **Point Grey's Ladybug**, **Ricoh Theta**, **Matterport** rigs. Image stitching (panoramic mosaicking) or depth-from-stereo is used.

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU ESE Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $\lambda_{cutoff} = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$ | Cutoff wavelength for Si: $\lambda_{cutoff} \approx 1107$ nm |
| 3 | $N_{e^-} = \eta \cdot \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{pixel} \cdot t_{int}$ | Collected photoelectrons; $\eta$ = quantum efficiency |
| 4 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{FD}}$ | Charge-to-voltage conversion at floating diffusion |
| 5 | $FF = \dfrac{A_{photosensitive}}{A_{pixel}}$ | Fill Factor (0 < FF ≤ 1) |
| 6 | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S_{signal}}{N_{noise}}\right) \text{ dB}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels |
| 7 | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{sat}}{N_{dark\_noise}}\right) \text{ dB}$ | Dynamic Range in dB |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{h \cdot d}{H}$ | Thin-lens imaging: $h$ = sensor height, $H$ = object height, $d$ = object distance |
| 9 | $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2f}\right)$ | Angular FOV of a perspective camera |
| 10 | $\text{Spatial Resolution} = \dfrac{2d \tan(\theta/2)}{N_{pixels}}$ | Ground footprint per pixel (m/pixel) |
| 11 | $\dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Thin-lens equation (Gaussian optics) |
| 12 | $f_{catadioptric} = \dfrac{R_{mirror}}{2}$ | Effective focal length of parabolic catadioptric (for unit-magnification) |
| 13 | $I(r) = I_0 \cdot f(r)$ | Omnidirectional image remapping: $r$ = radial distance, $f(r)$ = mirror profile function |
| 14 | $t_{int} = \dfrac{N_{e^-, sat} \cdot h\nu}{\eta \cdot A_{pixel} \cdot L_{scene} \cdot \pi \cdot (f/\#)^2}$ | Required integration time for full well |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile robots & AGVs:** Omnidirectional vision gives **simultaneous situational awareness** without rotating the sensor head.
- **Drones (UAVs):** Global-shutter CMOS sensors eliminate **rolling-shutter artifacts** during agile flight.
- **Self-driving cars:** Multi-Mpixel CMOS with on-chip HDR (≥ 120 dB) handles **tunnel exits and oncoming headlights**.
- **Warehouse robotics:** CMOS sensors at **60–120 fps** enable real-time SLAM at AGV speeds.
- **Surgical / Medical robots:** CCD's **low noise** preserves diagnostic detail in endoscopy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on this topic frequently test your ability to *derive* the relationship between sensor area, lens parameters, FOV, and image resolution. Below are the canonical derivations you must master.

## 3.1 Derivation I: Photon-to-Electron Conversion & Quantum Efficiency

**Given:** A pixel of area $A_{pixel} = 25\,\mu m^2$, quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.6$ (60%), wavelength $\lambda = 550$ nm (green peak), irradiance $E_e = 0.1$ W/m², integration time $t_{int} = 10$ ms.

**Find:** Number of photoelectrons collected.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \frac{hc}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s})}{550 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}} \\
&= 3.614 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J per photon}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{ph} &= \frac{E_e}{E_{ph}} \\
&= \frac{0.1\,\text{W/m}^2}{3.614 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}} \\
&= 2.767 \times 10^{17}\,\text{photons / (m}^2\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{pixel} \cdot t_{int} &= (2.767 \times 10^{17}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= 6.918 \times 10^{4}\,\text{photons incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{e^-} &= \eta \cdot \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{pixel} \cdot t_{int} \\
&= 0.6 \cdot 6.918 \times 10^{4} \\
&= 4.151 \times 10^{4}\,\text{photoelectrons} \\
&= \mathbf{41{,}510\,e^-}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is well below typical full-well capacity ($\sim$30,000–60,000 e⁻ for a 25 µm² pixel), so the image will not saturate.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Dynamic Range from Photon Statistics

**Given:** Full-well capacity $N_{sat} = 40{,}000$ e⁻; read noise $\sigma_{read} = 4$ e⁻ RMS.

**Find:** Dynamic range in dB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_{sat}}{\sigma_{read}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{40{,}000}{4}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) \\
&= 20 \cdot 4 \\
&= \mathbf{80\,dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** A DR of 80 dB corresponds to roughly **13.3 bits** of effective linear range ($80/6.02 \approx 13.3$), so a 14-bit ADC is appropriate.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Angular FOV and Ground Resolution

**Given:** Camera with focal length $f = 8$ mm, sensor height $h = 4.8$ mm, mounting height above ground $d = 2$ m, image height $N_{rows} = 1080$.

**Find:** (a) Vertical FOV, (b) Ground resolution at nadir (m/pixel).

### Part (a) — Vertical FOV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{vert} &= 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2f}\right) \\
&= 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{4.8\,\text{mm}}{2 \cdot 8\,\text{mm}}\right) \\
&= 2 \arctan(0.3) \\
&= 2 \cdot 16.699^{\circ} \\
&= \mathbf{33.40^{\circ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Ground Resolution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Res}_{ground} &= \frac{2d \tan(\theta_{vert}/2)}{N_{rows}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 2\,\text{m} \cdot \tan(16.699^{\circ})}{1080} \\
&= \frac{4 \cdot 0.3000}{1080} \\
&= \frac{1.2}{1080} \\
&= \mathbf{1.111 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m/pixel}} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.11\,\text{mm/pixel at nadir}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Sufficient for detecting small obstacles (e.g., 1 cm leg of a chair) on a mobile robot floor — about 9 pixels across the obstacle's width.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Catadioptric Single-Viewpoint Condition

**Given:** A hyperbolic mirror with parameters such that the two foci are at distances $a$ and $b$ from the mirror vertex, separated by $2c$, where $c^2 = a^2 + b^2$ (hyperbola identity).

**Find:** Condition for SVP.

> **Derivation logic:** For a *hyperbolic* mirror, a ray from focus $F_1$ reflects to focus $F_2$ (by the conic-section reflection property). If the perspective camera's optical center is placed at $F_1$ and its image plane is conjugate to $F_2$, then **all reflected rays appear to emanate from $F_2$**, which acts as the single effective viewpoint.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mirror equation:}\quad \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} &= 1 \\
\text{Constraint:}\quad c^2 &= a^2 + b^2,\quad c = \frac{a + d_{F_2}}{1} \\
\text{SVP condition:}\quad C_{lens} \equiv F_1,\quad I_{lens}\text{-plane} &\perp \text{mirror axis at } F_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The SVP holds **iff** the camera center coincides with one focus and the camera's image plane is focused on the other focus. This is the geometric foundation of unified central catadioptric image formation, used in **cubic-panorama unwrapping** for SLAM.

## 3.5 Derivation V: Pixel Output Voltage and Bit Depth

**Given:** $N_{e^-} = 41{,}510$ from §3.1, floating diffusion capacitance $C_{FD} = 10$ fF.

**Find:** Output voltage, and required ADC bits for 1 mV LSB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{Q}{C_{FD}} = \frac{N_{e^-} \cdot q}{C_{FD}} \\
&= \frac{41{,}510 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}}{10 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}} \\
&= \frac{6.65 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-14}} \\
&= \mathbf{0.665\,V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits} &= \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{V_{FS}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{0.665}{0.001}\right) = \log_{2}(665) = \mathbf{9.38\,bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Takeaway:** A **10-bit ADC** is the minimum practical choice for 1 mV quantization; for 8-bit output, $V_{LSB} = 0.665/255 \approx 2.6$ mV.

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Imaging Architectures

| Architecture | Photon → Electron | Charge Transfer | Readout | Noise Floor | FOV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Frame CCD** | Photodiode | Vertical shift then horizontal | Global shutter | ~1–3 e⁻ | Lens-limited |
| **Frame-Transfer CCD** | Photodiode | Fast to masked storage | Global shutter | ~2–4 e⁻ | Lens-limited |
| **Interline CCD** | Photodiode | Per-column transfer | Global shutter | ~5–10 e⁻ | Lens-limited |
| **CMOS (4T-APS)** | Photodiode + transfer gate | In-pixel to FD | Rolling shutter (default) | ~5–20 e⁻ | Lens-limited |
| **Global-Shutter CMOS** | Photodiode + storage node | In-pixel storage | Global shutter | ~10–25 e⁻ | Lens-limited |
| **Catadioptric** | Per pixel | Per pixel | Per pixel | Per pixel | **~360° horiz** |
| **Dioptric (Fish-eye)** | Per pixel | Per pixel | Per pixel | Per pixel | **~180°–220°** |
| **Polydioptric (multi-cam)** | Per pixel | Per pixel | Per pixel | Per pixel | **~360°×360° (sphere)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]

## 4.1 Block Architecture — CCD Sensor Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Scene Photons] --> A2[Optical Lens System]
    A2 --> A3[CCD Pixel Array MxN]
    A3 --> A4[Vertical Shift Register]
    A4 --> A5[Horizontal Shift Register]
    A5 --> A6[Floating Diffusion Node]
    A6 --> A7[Output Amplifier Single]
    A7 --> A8[Correlated Double Sampler]
    A8 --> A9[External ADC]
    A9 --> A10[Image Buffer RAM]
    A10 --> A11[Host Processor]
    A11 --> A12[SLAM OR Object Detection]
    A1 -. "Reflected light from scene" .- A2
    A3 -. "Photocharge accumulation" .- A4
    A7 -. "Single shared amplifier" .- A8
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — CMOS APS Sensor Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Scene Photons] --> B2[Micro-lens Array]
    B2 --> B3[Color Filter Array Bayer]
    B3 --> B4[Active Pixel Array 4T per pixel]
    B4 --> B5[Column Amplifier per column]
    B5 --> B6[Column Parallel ADC]
    B6 --> B7[Digital Correlated Double Sampler CDS]

    B10 --> B11[Perception Stack]

```

graph TD
    C1[Surrounding Scene 360 degree] --> C2[Curved Mirror Hyperbolic or Parabolic]
    C2 --> C3[Perspective Camera Lens]

graph TD
    D1[Top Camera Forward] --> E1[Synchronization Trigger]

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Imager for a Robotic Task

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Robot Vision Task] --> F2{Need full 360 degree FOV}
    F2 -- YES --> F3{Indoor static OR outdoor dynamic}
    F3 -- INDOOR STATIC --> F4[Catadioptric Parabolic Mirror]
    F3 -- OUTDOOR DYNAMIC --> F5[Polydioptric Multi-Camera Rig]
    F2 -- NO --> F6{Frame rate requirement}
    F6 -- GT 60 fps AND low light --> F7[Interline CCD]
    F6 -- GT 120 fps OR on-chip ISP --> F8[Global Shutter CMOS]
    F6 -- 30 fps smartphone style --> F9[Rolling Shutter CMOS]
    F4 --> F10[Central Catadioptric SLAM]
    F5 --> F11[Multi-Camera Visual Odometry]

    F8 --> F13[Drone OR AGV Visual SLAM]

```

```mermaid
graph TD
    G1[Photon] --> G2[Photodiode PD]
    G2 --> G3[Transfer Gate TX]
    G3 --> G4[Floating Diffusion FD]

    G4 --> G6[Source Follower Amplifier SF]
    G6 --> G7[Row Select Transistor RS]
    G7 --> G8[Column Bus]

```
