# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the "Kidnapped Robot Problem" in the context of localization. How does it differ from global localization?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Kidnapped Robot Problem** is a worst-case scenario in which a fully operational robot, whose pose is currently well-estimated, is **teleported abruptly to an arbitrary new location** without being told. The robot must then re-localize from scratch.
>
> **Difference from global localization:**
> - *Global localization*: robot starts in an unknown pose from the beginning.
> - *Kidnapped robot*: robot was localized, but the environment suddenly contradicts its belief.
>
> The kidnapped problem is **harder** because the filter must detect the failure (the posterior will become inconsistent) and recover by re-spawning multiple hypotheses. Particle filters handle this via **random injection** of new particles; Kalman-based methods usually fail.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Bayes Filter recursion equations for robot localization. Briefly explain the role of the normalizer $\eta$.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The Bayes Filter computes the posterior belief recursively:
>
> **Prediction:**
> $$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$
>
> **Correction:**
> $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$
>
> The constant $\eta$ is the **normalizer**, defined as:
> $$\eta = \left[\int P(z_t \mid x_t') \, \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t' \right]^{-1}$$
>
> **Role of $\eta$:** It ensures the resulting $bel(x_t)$ integrates to 1, i.e., is a valid probability density. Since $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ is a likelihood (not a probability in $x_t$), the un-normalized product $P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ generally does not sum/integrate to 1, so $\eta$ rescales it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization** algorithm. Derive the prediction and update equations, and clearly state the role of the Jacobian matrices $G_t$ and $H_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses a 1D EKF for localization. The initial state estimate is $\hat{x}_0 = 0$ m with variance $P_0 = 1$ m$^2$. The process noise variance is $Q = 0.4$ m$^2$ and measurement noise variance is $R = 0.6$ m$^2$. The robot moves with control $u_1 = 1.5$ m and receives a measurement $z_1 = 2.0$ m. Compute the predicted state, Kalman gain, updated state, and updated variance. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The **EKF** extends the linear Kalman Filter to non-linear systems by linearizing the motion and observation models around the current estimate via first-order Taylor series (Jacobians).

**Block diagram:**

```
+----------+      ut       +-----------+     xt_pred, P_pred    +-----------+     Kt    +-----------+
| bel x    | ------------> | Motion    | --------------------> | Update    | -------> | Pose      |
| (x, P)   |               | Model g   |                       | Model h   |          | x_hat_t   |
+----------+               +-----------+                       +-----------+          +-----------+
                                  ^                                 ^
                                  |                                 |
                              Gt Jacobian                       Ht Jacobian
```

**Motion model:** $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$

**Observation model:** $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$

**Jacobians (linearization at current estimate):**
$$
G_t = \left. \frac{\partial g(x_{t-1}, u_t)}{\partial x_{t-1}} \right|_{x_{t-1}=\hat{x}_{t-1 \mid t-1}}, \quad
H_t = \left. \frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t} \right|_{x_t=\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}}
$$

**Prediction:**
$$
\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = g(\hat{x}_{t-1 \mid t-1}, u_t)
$$
$$
P_{t \mid t-1} = G_t \, P_{t-1 \mid t-1} \, G_t^\top + Q_t
$$

**Update:**
$$
K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top (H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}
$$
$$
\hat{x}_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}))
$$
$$
P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \mid t-1}
$$

**Role of Jacobians:**
- $G_t$ converts the non-linear motion model into a local linear map so that the covariance $P$ can be propagated.
- $H_t$ does the same for the observation model, enabling the Kalman gain calculation.

The EKF is **first-order accurate**; it diverges under strong non-linearity (e.g., global localization), in which case a **particle filter** is preferred.

---

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1$, $Q = 0.4$, $R = 0.6$, $u_1 = 1.5$, $z_1 = 2.0$.

**Step 1 — Prediction (motion):**
$$
\hat{x}_{1 \mid 0} = \hat{x}_{0 \mid 0} + u_1 = 0 + 1.5 = \mathbf{1.5 \text{ m}}
$$
$$
P_{1 \mid 0} = P_{0 \mid 0} + Q = 1 + 0.4 = \mathbf{1.4 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Kalman gain:**
$$
K_1 = \frac{P_{1 \mid 0}}{P_{1 \mid 0} + R} = \frac{1.4}{1.4 + 0.6} = \frac{1.4}{2.0} = \mathbf{0.7}
$$

**Step 3 — Updated state:**
$$
\hat{x}_{1 \mid 1} = \hat{x}_{1 \mid 0} + K_1 (z_1 - \hat{x}_{1 \mid 0}) = 1.5 + 0.7 \times (2.0 - 1.5)
$$
$$
\hat{x}_{1 \mid 1} = 1.5 + 0.7 \times 0.5 = 1.5 + 0.35 = \mathbf{1.85 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Updated variance:**
$$
P_{1 \mid 1} = (1 - K_1) P_{1 \mid 0} = (1 - 0.7) \times 1.4 = 0.3 \times 1.4 = \mathbf{0.42 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Summary table:**

| Quantity | Predicted | Updated |
|---|---|---|
| State | 1.5 m | **1.85 m** |
| Variance | 1.4 m$^2$ | **0.42 m$^2$** |
| Kalman gain | — | **0.7** |

The 70 % weight on the sensor reflects the higher sensor reliability (lower $R$) than the motion model (lower $Q$).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** algorithm. Describe each step in detail and state the importance of the **resampling** step. Why is particle filter preferred over EKF for global localization? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot operates in a 4-cell 1D corridor with cells labelled A (0 m), B (1 m), C (2 m), D (3 m). It is given **3 particles**, all initialized uniformly. The robot moves one cell to the right (control $u = +1$ m) and a sensor reports proximity $z = 1.0$ m to the nearest wall, with likelihood $P(z=1 \mid \text{at cell } i) = \{A: 0.1, B: 0.8, C: 0.2, D: 0.05\}$. Apply **one iteration of MCL** and determine the final belief. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization, MCL)** represents the belief $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles):
$$
\mathcal{X}_t = \{ \langle x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]} \rangle \}_{i=1}^{N}
$$
where $x_t^{[i]}$ is a hypothesis pose and $w_t^{[i]}$ its importance weight.

**Algorithm steps (one iteration):**

1. **Sampling (Prediction):** For each particle, draw a new state from the motion model:
   $$x_t^{[i]} \sim P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$
   This propagates particles forward by simulating noisy motion.

2. **Importance Weighting (Correction):** Set the weight to the measurement likelihood:
   $$w_t^{[i]} = P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$
   Particles that are more consistent with the observation receive higher weights.

3. **Normalization:** Ensure weights sum to 1:
   $$w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{[j]}}$$

4. **Resampling:** Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, with probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$. This concentrates computational effort on the most plausible regions of the state space.

5. **Pose Estimate:** Compute the weighted mean (or MAP):
   $$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$$

**Why resampling is important:** Without resampling, many particles end up in low-probability regions and waste compute. Resampling **culls unlikely particles** and **replicates likely ones**, keeping the filter computationally focused. A common trigger is the **Effective Sample Size (ESS)**:
$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}
$$
Resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$.

**Why PF preferred over EKF for global localization:**
- EKF is restricted to **unimodal Gaussian** beliefs — it cannot represent the multi-modal distribution that occurs when the robot does not know its initial pose.
- PF makes **no Gaussianity assumption** and can represent arbitrarily complex (even multi-modal) beliefs.
- PF is **non-parametric** and naturally supports the kidnapped robot problem via random particle injection.

**Drawbacks:** high memory usage, particle depletion, sensitivity to proposal distribution.

---

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Initial 3 particles (uniform):** $\{A, C, A\}$ (positions: $0$ m, $2$ m, $0$ m), each with weight $w_0 = 1/3$.

**Step 1 — Sampling (motion $u=+1$ m, deterministic):**
Each particle shifts one cell to the right:
- Particle 1: A → B (1 m)
- Particle 2: C → D (3 m)
- Particle 3: A → B (1 m)

**Step 2 — Weighting using likelihood $P(z=1 \mid \text{cell})$:**

| Particle | New State | Likelihood $w$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | B (1 m) | 0.80 |
| 2 | D (3 m) | 0.05 |
| 3 | B (1 m) | 0.80 |

**Step 3 — Normalize:** Sum = $0.80 + 0.05 + 0.80 = 1.65$.
- $w_1 = 0.80/1.65 \approx \mathbf{0.485}$
- $w_2 = 0.05/1.65 \approx \mathbf{0.030}$
- $w_3 = 0.80/1.65 \approx \mathbf{0.485}$

**Step 4 — Resample with replacement** (probabilities = 0.485, 0.030, 0.485). Expected outcome over many runs: ~2 particles in B, ~1 particle in D. We will resample to $\{B, B, D\}$.

**Step 5 — Final belief estimate:**
$$
\hat{x}_1 = \sum_i w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]} = 0.485(1) + 0.030(3) + 0.485(1) = 0.485 + 0.090 + 0.485 = \mathbf{1.06 \text{ m}}
$$

**Result:** The robot is now estimated to be at **B (1 m)**, which is the most likely cell given both the motion and the sensor reading. The particle originally at D (low likelihood) has near-zero weight and will be removed by resampling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $bel$ and $\overline{bel}$** — students frequently omit the prediction step and write the update equation as a direct posterior. The predicted belief $\overline{bel}$ is **mandatory** before the correction.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization constant $\eta$** — the corrected belief will not integrate to 1 and examiners specifically check for $\eta$ in the Bayes Filter equation. (1-mark loss in Part A.)
> 3. **Mixing up Jacobian definitions** in EKF — $G_t$ is the Jacobian of $g$ w.r.t. $x$ (motion), $H_t$ is the Jacobian of $h$ w.r.t. $x$ (observation). Many students write $G_t = \partial g / \partial u$, which is $V_t$.
> 4. **Kalman gain calculation** — students often forget to invert the $(H_t P H_t^\top + R_t)$ term correctly. Show the matrix dimensions explicitly in the exam.
> 5. **Particle filter resampling** — students write "resample the particles" without specifying *how* (with replacement, proportional to weights). Always state the resampling rule.
> 6. **Units and dimensional analysis** — the KTU examiner **deducts marks** if the state and covariance units are not consistent (e.g., m vs. m$^2$ for variance).
> 7. **Conflating Localization with SLAM** — in the 14-mark questions, mixing up the two is a fatal mistake. Always state the assumption "map is known" explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Localization =** estimating robot pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ given controls $u_{1:t}$, observations $z_{1:t}$, and a known map.
- **Three problem types:** Position Tracking (easy), Global Localization (medium), Kidnapped Robot (hard).
- **Bayes Filter is the parent algorithm** of all localization methods.
- **Kalman Filter** = optimal for **linear-Gaussian** models; produces mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$.
- **EKF** = linearizes non-linear models using Jacobians $G_t, H_t$ — works for position tracking.
- **Particle Filter (MCL)** = non-parametric, sample-based, supports **multi-modal** beliefs and global localization.
- **Markov Localization** = uses a discrete grid; conceptually simple but memory-hungry.
- **Kalman Gain $K_t$** controls the trade-off between motion prediction and sensor update.
- **Resampling** prevents particle depletion in MCL; trigger via **Effective Sample Size**.
- **The Kidnapped Robot Problem** is the strongest test of a localization algorithm; solved by random particle injection.
- **Odometry error** is typically modelled as additive Gaussian noise; LIDAR as range-bearing Gaussian noise.
- **Always normalize** the corrected belief using $\eta$.
- **Mention assumptions explicitly** in 14-mark answers: "Markov-1", "Linear-Gaussian", "Map is known."
- **Cross-verify computations** by checking that posterior variance is **smaller** than predicted variance (it must always be).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges in Localization - Continuous Representations, Decomposition Strategies, Map Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Challenges in Localization - Continuous Representations, Decomposition Strategies, Map Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Challenges in Mobile Robot Localization

**Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose (position and orientation) relative to a given map of the environment. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups its challenges into three closely interlinked sub-problems: **continuous representation of the robot's belief state**, **decomposition strategies of the configuration space**, and **map representation of the environment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Localization* is the estimation of a robot's pose $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ at time $t$, given a map $m$, a history of sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$, and a history of control actions $u_{1:t}$. The fundamental challenges arise from **sensor noise, perceptual aliasing, real-time constraints, and uncertainty in data association**.

### 1.1.1 Why Localization is Hard — The Core Challenges

| # | Challenge | Plain-English Intuition |
|---|-----------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Sensor Noise** | The sonar sometimes "lies" because it detects echoes from the wrong wall. |
| 2 | **Perceptual Aliasing** | Two different corridors look identical — the robot cannot tell which one it is in. |
| 3 | **Position Uncertainty** | The robot's true pose is unknown; only a probability distribution is known. |
| 4 | **Computational Complexity** | Searching every possible pose in continuous space is intractable. |
| 5 | **Real-Time Constraints** | The robot moves while it is still thinking — decisions must be made in **< 100 ms** typically. |
| 6 | **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Someone picks up the robot and places it elsewhere — the belief must recover. |
| 7 | **Data Association** | Matching a current sensor reading to the correct landmark on the map. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Most of these challenges are *solved* (or at least mitigated) by the **choice of representation**. That is why the syllabus spends the next three sub-topics — continuous representations, decomposition, and map representation — on this single design decision.

## 1.2 Continuous Representations

A **continuous representation** describes the environment, the configuration space, or the belief state without quantising it into discrete bins. Mathematically, quantities are stored as real-valued functions, vectors, or probability density functions over $\mathbb{R}^n$.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Fog of Possibilities":**
Imagine walking blindfolded in a long office corridor. You don't know exactly where you are, so you imagine a **smooth fog of probability** that spreads along the corridor — denser where you are likely standing, thinner at impossible locations. This *fog* is a **continuous representation** of your belief. A discrete representation would instead say "either I am at door 1, door 2, or door 3" — choppy and abrupt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Examples in KTU Robotics:**
> - **Belief as a Gaussian density** $b(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ — Kalman Filter.
> - **Belief as a particle cloud** — Monte Carlo Localization (MCL).
> - **Belief as a multi-modal distribution** — handles the global-localization ambiguity.
> - **Configuration space as a continuous manifold** $C \subset \mathbb{R}^n$.

## 1.3 Decomposition Strategies

**Decomposition** is the partitioning of the continuous configuration space (or workspace) into smaller, finite, tractable cells or regions. It is the bridge that allows *continuous* problems to be attacked with *discrete* algorithms (graph search, dynamic programming, etc.).

**Conceptual Analogy — "Cutting a Cake into Pieces":**
Before you can decorate a huge cake, you cut it into manageable pieces. Some people cut the cake into **equal square pieces** (fixed-cell decomposition), some into **irregularly shaped pieces that exactly fit the frosting area** (exact cell decomposition), and some keep cutting a piece only when they need more detail (**adaptive / quadtree decomposition**). The robot world does the same with its free space.

## 1.4 Map Representation

A **map representation** is the data structure used to store what the robot knows about the environment. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with three principal classes:

1. **Geometric / Metric Maps** — exact coordinates of features, walls, obstacles.
2. **Topological Maps** — graph of places (nodes) and their connectivity (edges).
3. **Hybrid Maps** — combination of metric accuracy + topological abstraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Map Representation vs Discrete Map Representation:** A *continuous* geometric map stores walls as line segments $y = mx + c$ with real-valued parameters. A *discrete* grid map stores each cell's occupancy as a binary (or probabilistic) number. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises **continuous** forms (line maps, landmark maps, feature maps) and contrasts them with **discrete** grid maps.

---

> **Summary in one line:** Localization is hard because of noise and ambiguity, and the three big design choices — *how you represent the belief continuously*, *how you decompose the space*, and *how you represent the map* — together determine whether a robot can localise at all.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Localization Problem

Let the robot's pose be $x_t \in SE(2)$ or $SE(3)$ (Special Euclidean group). The **belief** of the robot at time $t$ is:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This is the *posterior* probability distribution over the robot's pose. The classical **Bayes filter** recursive update is the foundation of every modern localization algorithm.

### 2.1.1 The Bayes Filter Recursion (Two Steps)

**Step 1 — Prediction (motion update):**
$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 2 — Correction (measurement update):**
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

where:
- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ = **motion model** (probabilistic, handles slip, noise).
- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ = **measurement model** (sensor likelihood given pose + map).
- $\eta$ = **normalising constant**, $\eta = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x'_t, m) \overline{bel}(x'_t) dx'_t\right]^{-1}$.
- $m$ = map.

> [!NOTE]
> The continuous integral in Step 1 is the *reason* why we need representations and decompositions — it is **analytically intractable** for most realistic non-linear, non-Gaussian systems.

### 2.1.2 Why the Challenges Exist — Root Cause Analysis

| Root Cause | Resulting Challenge | Mitigation via Representation |
|------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Sensor returns are real-valued & noisy | Continuous belief needed | Gaussian / particle representation |
| Pose space is unbounded (no clipping possible) | Global localisation hard | Multi-hypothesis beliefs |
| Workspace is huge | Decomposition explosion | Hierarchical decomposition |
| Multiple indistinguishable features | Perceptual aliasing | Topological / semantic maps |

## 2.2 Continuous Representations — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Parametric (Gaussian) Continuous Representation

The pose is modelled as a **single-mode Gaussian**:
$$bel(x_t) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma_t\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x_t-\mu_t)^{T}\Sigma_t^{-1}(x_t-\mu_t)\right)$$

**Strengths:** Closed-form Kalman update, very fast.
**Weakness:** Cannot represent *multi-modal* beliefs (the global-localization case).

### 2.2.2 Non-Parametric Continuous Representation — Particle Filters

The belief is approximated by a weighted set of $N$ samples (particles):
$$bel(x_t) \approx \{(x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}) \mid i = 1, \dots, N\}$$

The continuous density is reconstructed as:
$$p(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$$

**Importance sampling** for the weights:
$$w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$$

### 2.2.3 Continuous vs Discrete — Direct Comparison

| Property | Continuous | Discrete |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| Pose accuracy | Arbitrary precision | Limited to grid spacing |
| Memory | Often less (few params) | Often more (one per cell) |
| Multi-modal belief | Trivial (particles) | Trivial (multi-hypothesis grid) |
| Computation | Variable (often high) | Predictable |
| Symbolic planning | Hard | Easy (graph search) |

## 2.3 Decomposition Strategies — Detailed Theory

A **decomposition strategy** is a function $D: C_{free} \rightarrow \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ that partitions the free configuration space $C_{free}$ into a finite set of non-overlapping cells. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly enumerates the following four families.

### 2.3.1 Fixed Cell Decomposition (Uniform Grid)

- The workspace is divided into **equal-sized rectangular cells** of dimension $\Delta \times \Delta$.
- A cell $C_{i,j}$ is *free* if no obstacle intersects it, *occupied* otherwise, *partly* (mixed) is conservatively marked occupied or further subdivided.

**Properties:** Simple, easy to implement, resolution scales with $\Delta^{-2}$.

### 2.3.2 Adaptive Cell Decomposition (Quadtree)

- Starts with one large cell; **subdivides only the cells that contain obstacle boundaries** into 4 children.
- Produces a **variable-resolution** map: dense near walls, sparse in open space.
- Worst-case cell count is $O(k \log(R/\Delta_{min}))$ for $k$ obstacle edges.

### 2.3.3 Exact Cell Decomposition

- The decomposition boundary follows the **exact polygonal contours** of the obstacles.
- The free space is represented by a set of **non-overlapping, non-convex polygonal regions** whose union is exactly $C_{free}$.
- **Disadvantage:** The boundaries themselves are non-convex, requiring bookkeeping during path planning.

### 2.3.4 Approximate Cell Decomposition

- A **finer** fixed grid; the boundary is approximated to within the grid resolution $\Delta$.
- Trade-off: smaller $\Delta$ → better approximation → more cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Favourite Question:** *Distinguish between exact and approximate cell decomposition.* Memorise the table below.

| Feature | Exact | Approximate |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| Boundary fidelity | Perfect | Within $\Delta$ |
| Cell shapes | Convex (with care) or non-convex | Always convex (squares) |
| Computational cost | High (depends on obstacle geometry) | Predictable $O(R^2/\Delta^2)$ |
| Use case | High-fidelity planning, simulation | Real-time navigation, large maps |

### 2.3.5 Other Notable Strategies (Bonus for KTU)

- **Voronoi Decomposition:** cells are *Voronoi regions* of obstacle vertices; maximises clearance.
- **Visibility Graph Decomposition:** cells are bounded by mutual visibility rays.
- **Probabilistic Cell Decomposition:** cells carry occupancy probabilities; foundation of occupancy grid maps.

## 2.4 Map Representation — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Geometric (Metric) Map

Stores the world as a set of geometric primitives with absolute coordinates:
$$m = \{(f_i, p_i) \mid i = 1, \dots, M\}$$
where $f_i$ is a feature (line, point, arc) and $p_i$ its parameters (endpoints, centre, radius, etc.).

**Sub-types:**
- **Point-feature map** — landmarks as $(x_i, y_i)$ points.
- **Line-feature map** — walls as line segments $(x_{1},y_{1}) \rightarrow (x_{2},y_{2})$.
- **Polygon map** — closed polygons bounding obstacles.

### 2.4.2 Topological Map

Stores the world as a **graph** $G = (V, E)$:
- $V$ = distinct places (rooms, junctions, corridor segments).
- $E$ = connectivity relations (corridors, doorways).

**Pros:** Compact, supports symbolic planning, robust to metric noise.
**Cons:** Hard to construct automatically, ambiguous if places are perceptually similar.

### 2.4.3 Occupancy Grid Map (Probabilistic, Discrete)

A 2D regular grid; each cell $c_{i,j}$ stores a probability of being occupied:
$$p(m_{i,j}) = \text{Prob}( \text{cell } c_{i,j} \text{ is occupied} )$$

The **inverse sensor model** update is:
$$\text{log-odds update: } \quad L(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t}) = L(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t-1}) + L(m_{i,j} \mid z_t) - L_0$$

where $L = \log \frac{p}{1-p}$ and $L_0 = \log \frac{p_0}{1-p_0}$ is the prior.

### 2.4.4 Hybrid Map

Combines a **topological skeleton** with **local metric patches** at each node — used in modern SLAM systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Occupancy grids dominate indoor robot vacuums and warehouse AMRs. Topological maps dominate long-distance delivery and warehouse routing (think of a Google-Map-like layer above the metric grid).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Belief (posterior) | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Probability density over pose |
| 2 | Bayes prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Chapman–Kolmogorov marginalisation |
| 3 | Bayes correction | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ is a normaliser |
| 4 | Gaussian density | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert\Sigma\vert}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right)$ | Used by EKF localisation |
| 5 | Particle weight | $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$ | MCL importance sampling |
| 6 | Number of grid cells | $N_{cells} = \lceil W/\Delta \rceil \cdot \lceil H/\Delta \rceil$ | $W,H$: world size in m; $\Delta$: cell size in m |
| 7 | Quadtree depth | $d = \lceil \log_2(R/\Delta_{min}) \rceil$ | $R$: root cell size; $\Delta_{min}$ : smallest cell |
| 8 | Occupancy log-odds | $L_{i,j} := \log\frac{p(m_{i,j})}{1-p(m_{i,j})}$ | $L_{i,j} \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ |
| 9 | Occupancy update | $L_{i,j,t} = L_{i,j,t-1} + L_{i,j,z} - L_0$ | Additive in log-odds |
| 10 | Information gain (entropy reduction) | $IG(c) = H(p \mid z_{1:t-1}) - E[H(p \mid z_{1:t}, c \text{ observed})]$ | Drives adaptive decomposition |
| 11 | Voronoi cell | $V(p_i) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^2 : d(x,p_i) \le d(x,p_j) \ \forall j\}$ | Maximises clearance |
| 12 | Path length lower bound (Voronoi) | $L_{Voronoi} \ge L_{shortest} \ge L_{Euclidean}$ | Used in clearance-maximising planners |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe character hazard:** In KTU examinations, you may write $\vert x \vert$, $\vert \Sigma \vert$, or $\mid z \mid$ for absolute value or determinant. **Never** write them as `|x|` inside markdown tables — it breaks the table. Use the LaTeX forms shown above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Why derivations matter for KTU:** Examiners award high marks when you *derive* a key result, not just state it. This section provides the three derivations most often asked in Module 4 questions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bayes Filter Recursion for Localization

We want to derive the recursive update formula
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$$

**Starting point** — by definition of conditional probability:
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step A — Markov simplification of the measurement likelihood:**
The sensor reading $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and the static map $m$ (Markov assumption). Hence:
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step B — Simplification of the prior over $x_t$ via total probability:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step C — Recognising the integrand as the prior belief:**
By definition,
$$bel(x_{t-1}) = p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$$

So we can write:
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step D — Combining A, B, C:**
$$bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step E — Identifying the normaliser $\eta$:**
The denominator does not depend on $x_t$, so we collect it as the normalising constant:
$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

Therefore, the **final form** is:
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \underbrace{\int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}_{\overline{bel}(x_t)}$$

**Step F — Specialisation to Kalman Filter (linear-Gaussian):**
If motion and measurement are linear with Gaussian noise,
$$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$$
with $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$, $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$, then the integral above becomes a Gaussian convolution yielding a closed-form Kalman update:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

where $\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$ are the *predicted* mean and covariance, and $K_t$ is the **Kalman gain**.

> [!NOTE]
> The *non-linear* / *non-Gaussian* case forces us to abandon the closed-form Kalman update. The continuous representation is no longer a single Gaussian — and this is precisely where **particle filters** (MCL), **histogram filters**, and **decomposition into discrete cells** enter the picture.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update Rule

We want to show why occupancy grid updates can be written as a *simple addition* in log-odds space.

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes' rule for cell occupancy:**
$$p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_{i,j}, z_{1:t-1}) \, p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 2 — Take the odds ratio $o = p/(1-p)$ on both sides:**
$$\frac{p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_{i,j}, z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_{i,j}, z_{1:t-1})} \cdot \frac{p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t-1})} \cdot \frac{1 - p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the inverse-sensor-model term:**
The term $\frac{p(z_t \mid m_{i,j}, z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_{i,j}, z_{1:t-1})}$ is the *odds of the current measurement favouring occupancy* of cell $c_{i,j}$. Call it $o(m_{i,j} \mid z_t)$.

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithm to convert products to sums:**
Define $L = \log o$ (log-odds). The Bayes' rule becomes:
$$L(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t}) = L(m_{i,j} \mid z_t) + L(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t-1}) - L_0$$

where $L_0 = \log \frac{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$ is a constant offset that disappears in practice because we use the *inverse sensor model* which already accounts for it.

**Step 5 — Final recursive form:**
$$\boxed{L_{i,j,t} = L_{i,j,t-1} + L_{i,j,z_t} - L_0}$$

**Step 6 — Conversion back to probability:**
$$p(m_{i,j}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(L_{i,j})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** The additive form means each new sensor reading just *adds* a constant to a stored integer per cell. This is exactly why occupancy grids run in real time on embedded hardware in robot vacuums.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resolution vs Coverage Trade-off in Fixed Decomposition

We want to derive the relationship between **map resolution** $\Delta$ and **storage cost** for a workspace of size $W \times H$.

**Step 1 — Number of cells:**
$$N_{cells}(\Delta) = \left\lceil \frac{W}{\Delta} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{H}{\Delta} \right\rceil \approx \frac{W \cdot H}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 2 — Memory required (single byte per cell for log-odds integer):**
$$M(\Delta) = N_{cells}(\Delta) \times B \; \text{bytes} \approx \frac{B \cdot W \cdot H}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 3 — Doubling resolution quadruples memory:**
$$\frac{M(\Delta/2)}{M(\Delta)} = \left(\frac{\Delta}{\Delta/2}\right)^2 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Lower bound on $\Delta$ from localisation accuracy:**
To localise within tolerance $\epsilon$, the cell size must satisfy:
$$\Delta \le \epsilon \cdot k_\sigma$$
where $k_\sigma$ is a confidence multiplier (e.g., 2 for 95% confidence under Gaussian noise).

**Step 5 — Combined cost-accuracy objective:**
The total cost is:
$$J(\Delta) = \alpha \cdot \frac{B \cdot W \cdot H}{\Delta^2} + \beta \cdot (\Delta - k_\sigma \epsilon)^2 \quad \text{for } \Delta > k_\sigma \epsilon$$

Differentiate with respect to $\Delta$ to find the **optimal resolution** that minimises memory + accuracy error:
$$\frac{dJ}{d\Delta} = -\frac{2\alpha B W H}{\Delta^3} + 2\beta(\Delta - k_\sigma \epsilon) = 0$$

Solving:
$$\Delta_{opt} = \left( \frac{\alpha B W H}{\beta} \right)^{1/4} \quad \text{(approximate, ignoring } k_\sigma \epsilon \text{ term)}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering rule of thumb:**
For an indoor room of size $W = H = 10$ m, choosing $\Delta = 0.05$ m gives:
$$N_{cells} = (10 / 0.05)^2 = 200^2 = 40{,}000 \text{ cells}$$

For a warehouse of size $100 \times 100$ m at the same $\Delta$:
$$N_{cells} = 2000^2 = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ cells}$$

**This 100× increase motivates the use of *adaptive* decomposition (quadtree, octree).**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Algorithm / Representation | Belief Type | Strength | Weakness | Best Use Case |
|---------------------------|------------|----------|----------|---------------|
| **Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Single-mode Gaussian | Fast, closed form | Cannot track multi-modal | Local tracking, known initial pose |
| **Histogram Filter** | Discrete grid | Handles multi-modal | Resolution-bounded | Global localisation in small grid maps |
| **Particle Filter (MCL)** | Continuous particle cloud | Handles any density, global | Particle depletion | Global localisation, kidnapping |
| **Fixed Grid Decomposition** | Discrete occupancy | Simple, predictable | Memory cost $O(\Delta^{-2})$ | Indoor maps $< 50 \times 50$ m |
| **Quadtree Decomposition** | Hierarchical | Memory-efficient | Traversal overhead | Large open spaces, mixed detail |
| **Exact Cell Decomposition** | Polygonal cells | Boundary-perfect | Geometric complexity | High-fidelity path planning |
| **Geometric Map** | Metric primitives | Compact, accurate | Needs good feature detection | Indoor structured environments |
| **Topological Map** | Graph | Compact, symbolic | Hard to auto-build | Large-scale, long routes |
| **Hybrid Map** | Combined | Best of both | Implementation complexity | Production SLAM systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
