# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1 — 3 marks]**
> **Q:** List any **four challenges** in mobile robot localization.
>
> **Model Answer (target length: 4–5 lines):**
> 1. **Sensor noise and uncertainty** — real sensors are noisy, giving imperfect measurements.
> 2. **Perceptual aliasing** — different places look identical to the sensor.
> 3. **Computational complexity** — searching a continuous pose space is intractable in real time.
> 4. **Data association ambiguity** — matching a sensor reading to the correct map feature is non-trivial.
> 5. *(Optional bonus)* The kidnapped robot problem — sudden jumps in pose.
> *(Any 4 well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2 — 3 marks]**
> **Q:** Differentiate between **exact** and **approximate** cell decomposition.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | Exact | Approximate |
> |---|---|---|
> | Boundary fidelity | Matches obstacles precisely | Within grid resolution $\Delta$ |
> | Cell shape | Convex or non-convex polygonal | Convex rectangles |
> | Memory | Depends on obstacle complexity | $O(WH/\Delta^2)$ |
> Exact is preferred in high-fidelity planning, approximate in real-time large-scale navigation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Set 1 — 14 marks]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **continuous representation of belief** in robot localization. Discuss **parametric (Gaussian)** and **non-parametric (particle)** representations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe **exact cell decomposition** and **approximate cell decomposition** strategies. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):** The belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is the posterior over the robot's pose. A *continuous representation* stores this distribution as a real-valued function over $\mathbb{R}^n$ rather than as discrete bins.

**Parametric / Gaussian Representation (2.5 marks):**
- The belief is a single-mode Gaussian: $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- Mean $\mu_t \in SE(2)$ gives the most likely pose; covariance $\Sigma_t$ encodes the uncertainty ellipse.
- Used by the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization** algorithm.
- **Advantage:** closed-form update, computationally cheap.
- **Disadvantage:** cannot represent multi-modal beliefs, fails in global localization.

**Non-Parametric / Particle Representation (2.5 marks):**
- Belief is represented by $N$ weighted samples $\{(x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$:
$$bel(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$$
- Weights updated as $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$.
- Used by **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)**.
- **Advantage:** can represent any distribution, handles multi-modality and kidnapping.
- **Disadvantage:** particle depletion, higher compute.

**Block diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Belief BT"]:::in --> B["Representation Choice"]:::proc
    B --> C["Parametric Gaussian EKF"]:::gauss
    B --> D["Non Parametric Particle MCL"]:::part
    C --> E["Pose Mean and Covariance"]:::out
    D --> F["Pose Particle Cloud"]:::out
    classDef in fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef proc fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef gauss fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef part fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    classDef out fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
```

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Exact Cell Decomposition (3 marks):**
- The free space $C_{free}$ is partitioned into non-overlapping cells whose boundaries **coincide exactly with obstacle boundaries**.
- Each cell is a **convex polygon** in the simplest case; the connectivity of cells forms a graph.
- A path planner can then run a graph search (e.g., A*) over this cell graph.
- **Advantage:** boundary-perfect, no approximation error.
- **Disadvantage:** algorithmic complexity, harder to maintain, sensitive to obstacle geometry.

**Approximate Cell Decomposition (3 marks):**
- The workspace is overlaid with a **uniform grid** of resolution $\Delta$.
- Cells fully inside obstacles are marked *occupied*; cells fully in free space are *free*; **mixed** cells are conservatively marked *occupied* or further refined.
- **Advantage:** simple, predictable, $O(WH/\Delta^2)$ cells, easy to update online.
- **Disadvantage:** approximate — maximum path error is $O(\Delta)$.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Exact | Approximate |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary fidelity | Perfect | Bounded by $\Delta$ |
| Cell shape | Convex polygons | Convex squares |
| Update complexity | Geometric | Constant per cell |
| Real-time use | Often no | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B Qn A):** Students commonly **confuse exact and approximate decomposition** with exact and *approximate* path planning. The KTU syllabus uses the terms strictly for **decomposition of free space**, not for the planner. If you write "exact = exact path, approximate = sub-optimal path" you will lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Set 2 — 14 marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **map representation** problem in robotics. Compare **geometric**, **topological**, and **occupancy grid** maps with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With relevant equations, derive the **occupancy grid log-odds update rule**. State its engineering advantages. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):** A map representation $m$ is the data structure used by the robot to remember its environment. The choice of representation determines the storage, update rule, and supported algorithms.

**Geometric (Metric) Map (2 marks):**
- Stores the environment as geometric primitives (points, lines, polygons) with **absolute coordinates**.
- Example: walls stored as line segments with endpoints $(x_1,y_1)$ and $(x_2,y_2)$.
- **Used in:** structured indoor environments, factory floors.
- **Strength:** compact, accurate. **Weakness:** feature detection required.

**Topological Map (2 marks):**
- Stores the environment as a **graph** $G = (V, E)$.
- $V$ = distinct places (rooms, junctions); $E$ = connections (corridors, doorways).
- **Used in:** long-range delivery robots, museum guides.
- **Strength:** symbolic, scalable. **Weakness:** hard to build automatically, ambiguity.

**Occupancy Grid Map (2 marks):**
- Discrete 2D grid; each cell stores a **probability of being occupied**.
- Updated via the **inverse sensor model** and Bayes' rule.
- **Used in:** robot vacuums, warehouse AMRs.
- **Strength:** easy to build from raw sensor sweeps, supports arbitrary obstacle shapes.
- **Weakness:** memory grows as $\Delta^{-2}$.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks) — see Section 3.2 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps to write in the exam:**

1. Start from Bayes' rule applied to cell occupancy $p(m_{i,j} \mid z_{1:t})$.
2. Form the odds ratio $\frac{p}{1-p}$ on both sides.
3. Apply logarithm to obtain log-odds $L = \log \frac{p}{1-p}$.
4. Show that the product of odds becomes a **sum of log-odds**:
$$L_{i,j,t} = L_{i,j,t-1} + L_{i,j,z_t} - L_0$$
5. Convert back to probability using:
$$p(m_{i,j}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(L_{i,j})}$$

**Engineering advantages (2 marks):**
- **Additivity:** each new measurement is just one addition per cell — extremely fast.
- **Numerically stable:** log-odds avoids the underflow issues of raw probabilities.
- **Parallelisable:** each cell update is independent — perfect for GPU/FPGA.
- **Streaming:** map can be updated incrementally as the robot moves.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B Qn B):** When deriving the log-odds rule, students often **drop the constant $L_0$** without justifying it. The KTU answer key expects you to state explicitly that $L_0$ is the prior log-odds and disappears when an *inverse sensor model* is used. Skipping this justification costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- **Localization** = estimating pose $x_t$ from sensors, controls, and a map $m$.
- Core challenges: **sensor noise, perceptual aliasing, computational complexity, kidnapped robot**.
- Belief is mathematically the posterior $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — a **continuous representation** of the robot's knowledge.
- Bayes filter has two steps: **prediction** (motion) and **correction** (measurement).
- **Parametric (Gaussian / EKF)** representation — fast, single-mode, fails for global localization.
- **Non-parametric (Particle / MCL)** representation — handles any density, robust to kidnapping, but suffers particle depletion.
- **Histogram filter** is the discrete-grid counterpart of the particle filter.
- **Decomposition strategies** convert the continuous configuration space into finite cells:
  - **Fixed** — uniform grid, $O(WH/\Delta^2)$ cells.
  - **Adaptive (quadtree)** — variable resolution, splits only mixed cells.
  - **Exact** — perfect boundary fidelity, complex geometry.
  - **Approximate** — grid-aligned, predictable cost.
  - **Voronoi** — maximises clearance, ideal for safe paths.
- **Map representations** in KTU scope:
  - **Geometric** — points, lines, polygons with absolute coordinates.
  - **Topological** — graph of places and connections.
  - **Occupancy grid** — probabilistic 2D cells.
  - **Hybrid** — combines metric + topological layers.
- **Occupancy grid log-odds update** is additive: $L_{i,j,t} = L_{i,j,t-1} + L_{i,j,z_t} - L_0$.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** doubling the resolution $\Delta$ **quadruples** the memory.
- **Real-time threshold** for localization: typically **< 100 ms per update** for indoor mobile robots.
- **Modern systems** (e.g., warehouse AMRs) use a *hybrid* stack: continuous particle belief + adaptive grid map + topological place graph on top.
- **Key insight:** the *three sub-topics* of this module are deeply coupled — you cannot choose a decomposition strategy without considering the map representation, and you cannot choose a map representation without considering the belief type.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary to memorise:** *"Localization challenges are dominated by the choice of continuous belief, the choice of decomposition strategy, and the choice of map representation — these three are not independent."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Kalman Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Kalman Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Kalman Method)

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Kalman Method)** is a recursive state estimation technique in which a robot determines its pose (position and orientation) within a known environment by fusing uncertain motion (odometry) data with uncertain sensor measurements, assuming all state variables and noise processes are represented by **Gaussian distributions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> The Kalman Filter is an *optimal recursive estimator* for linear-Gaussian systems. It computes the posterior belief of a continuous state by maintaining a mean vector $\mu_t$ and a covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$, updated through two phases: **Prediction** (using the motion model) and **Correction** (using the sensor/measurement model).

### Key Terminology
- **Belief (Bel):** The robot's internal probabilistic estimate of its state.
- **State Vector $x_t$:** A vector representing the robot's pose (typically $\begin{bmatrix} x & y & \theta \end{bmatrix}^T$).
- **Map $m$:** A predefined representation of the environment (feature-based, occupancy grid, etc.).
- **Linearization:** Approximation of non-linear motion/measurement models via Taylor series (used in the **Extended Kalman Filter - EKF**).

---

## 2. Conceptual Analogy: The Sailing Captain

Imagine a captain navigating a ship across a foggy ocean at night.

- The **captain's notebook** contains the ship's *belief* about its position (mean) and a margin of *uncertainty* (covariance).
- After every hour of sailing using the compass and speed log (odometry), the captain **predicts** a new position. Because of wind, currents, and instrument error, the uncertainty region *grows* — represented by a wider cloud of possible positions.
- When a lighthouse beacon is sighted (a sensor measurement), the captain **corrects** the estimate. The cloud of uncertainty *shrinks* because the measurement intersects with the prediction.
- The Kalman Filter is mathematically what allows the captain to optimally combine the *noisy prediction* with the *noisy measurement* to produce the **best possible estimate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Gaussian "Bell Curve" Concept:**
> The robot's belief is modeled as a multivariate normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. The bell curve is centered on the best estimate $\mu_t$, and the spread $\Sigma_t$ captures the confidence — a narrow bell means high confidence, a wide bell means high uncertainty.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **State dimension $n$:** Typically **3** for 2D ground robots ($x, y, \theta$).
- **Measurement dimension $k$:** Depends on sensor (range = 1, range-bearing = 2, landmark positions = 2 per landmark).
- **Process Noise Covariance $R_t$:** Encodes uncertainty in motion; standard values in the order of **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²** for indoor robots.
- **Measurement Noise Covariance $Q_t$:** Encodes sensor uncertainty; standard values around **$10^{-2}$ m²** for laser range finders.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Probabilistic Localization Framework

The robot maintains a belief distribution over its state $x_t$ given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

The Kalman Filter is derived under three restrictive but powerful assumptions:

1. **Linear Motion Model:** $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t$
2. **Linear Measurement Model:** $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$
3. **Gaussian Noise:** $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$

Where the belief is propagated recursively using **Bayes' Rule** and the **Markov assumption** (current state depends only on the previous state).

---

## 2. The Two-Phase Recursive Cycle

### Phase 1 — Prediction (Time Update)
The robot uses its motion model and the previous control $u_t$ to project the belief forward in time.

**Why?** Because the robot has moved, but no new sensor data has arrived. Uncertainty *grows* due to the additive process noise $R_t$.

**How?**
- The mean is shifted according to the kinematic equation.
- The covariance is propagated through the linear map and *inflated* by the process noise.

### Phase 2 — Correction (Measurement Update)
The robot fuses the new sensor measurement $z_t$ to refine the predicted belief.

**Why?** Measurements carry independent information that reduces uncertainty.

**How?**
- The **innovation** (or *residual*) $z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$ measures the discrepancy between prediction and observation.
- The **Kalman Gain $K_t$** weights the innovation optimally to minimize the posterior covariance.

---

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Motion Model** | $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t$ | $A_t$ = state matrix, $B_t$ = input matrix, $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | SI |
| 2 | **Measurement Model** | $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$ | $C_t$ = observation matrix, $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | SI |
| 3 | **Predicted Mean** | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | Best estimate before measurement | m, rad |
| 4 | **Predicted Covariance** | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | Uncertainty *grows* by $R_t$ | m², rad² |
| 5 | **Kalman Gain** | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Optimal weight: prediction vs. measurement | dimensionless / rad |
| 6 | **Innovation** | $\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$ | Residual: prediction error | m, rad |
| 7 | **Corrected Mean** | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ | Posterior estimate | m, rad |
| 8 | **Corrected Covariance** | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Uncertainty *shrinks* after update | m², rad² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** The algorithm requires initialization with $\mu_0$ (initial pose) and $\Sigma_0$ (initial uncertainty). A common choice is $\Sigma_0 = 0$ (perfect prior knowledge) or $\Sigma_0 = \epsilon I$ with small $\epsilon > 0$ to avoid singularities.

---

## 4. Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Fuses GPS, IMU, LiDAR, and odometry for centimeter-level localization.
- **Aerospace:** Apollo-era and modern spacecraft navigation; GPS receiver position estimation.
- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robots) localize within dense indoor environments.
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical tool tracking in confined anatomical spaces.
- **Underwater ROVs (AUVs):** Localization where GPS is unavailable, fusing DVL and sonar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation of the Kalman Gain $K_t$

The Kalman Gain is *defined* as the value that minimizes the **posterior (corrected) covariance** $\Sigma_t$, ensuring the estimate is optimal in the minimum mean-square error (MMSE) sense.

### Step 1 — Express the Posterior Mean

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Posterior Error

The error between the true state $x_t$ and the estimate is $e_t = x_t - \mu_t$. Substituting the measurement model $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$:

$$
e_t = x_t - \bar{\mu}_t - K_t (C_t x_t + \delta_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
e_t = (I - K_t C_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) - K_t \delta_t
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Posterior Covariance

$$
\Sigma_t = E[e_t e_t^T]
$$

Since the prediction error $(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)$ and the measurement noise $\delta_t$ are *independent zero-mean* random variables, the cross-terms vanish:

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t C_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Expression

$$
\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T K_t^T + K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t) K_t^T
$$

### Step 5 — Minimize the Trace of $\Sigma_t$

The optimal $K_t$ is found by setting the derivative of $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$ w.r.t. $K_t$ equal to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)}{\partial K_t} = -2 (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t)^T + 2 K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t) = 0
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $K_t$

$$
K_t (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t) = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T
$$

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

This is the **optimal Kalman Gain** that minimizes the posterior covariance.

---

## 2. Derivation of the Posterior Covariance Simplification

Starting from the expanded form, substitute the definition of $K_t$ back:

$$
\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - K_t C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Proof:** Both the *expanded* form and the *compact* form $(I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ are mathematically equivalent, but the compact form is computationally cheaper ($O(n^2)$ vs. $O(n^3)$).

---

## 3. Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 1D robot moves with state $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \varepsilon_t$, with $A = 1$, $B = 1$. Initial state $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 0.5$. Control $u_1 = 1$ m, process noise $R = 0.1$, measurement $z_1 = 1.4$, $C = 1$, $Q = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Prediction:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = 1 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.1 = 0.6
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation:**

$$
\nu_1 = 1.4 - 1 \cdot 1 = 0.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = \frac{0.6}{0.6 + 0.3} = 0.667
$$

**Step 4 — Correction:**

$$
\mu_1 = 1 + 0.667 \cdot 0.4 = 1.267 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Sigma_1 = (1 - 0.667) \cdot 0.6 = 0.200
$$

**Result:** Posterior estimate $\mu_1 = 1.267$ m with reduced uncertainty $\Sigma_1 = 0.2$ (down from predicted 0.6).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Kalman Filter Localization Loop (Block Diagram)

The recursive nature of the Kalman Filter is best visualized as a feedback control loop where the state estimate is continuously refined.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State mu0, Sigma0] --> B[Input Control u_t from Odometry]
    B --> C[Prediction Step mu_bar A mu_prev plus B u_t]
    D[Process Noise Covariance R_t] --> C
    C --> E[Predicted Covariance Sigma_bar A Sigma_prev A_transpose plus R_t]
    C --> F[Predicted Belief N mu_bar, Sigma_bar]
    G[Sensor Measurement z_t] --> H[Innovation nu_t equals z_t minus C mu_bar]
    F --> H
    I[Measurement Noise Covariance Q_t] --> J[Kalman Gain K_t]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Corrected Mean mu_t equals mu_bar plus K_t nu_t]
    H --> K
    J --> L[Corrected Covariance Sigma_t equals I minus K_t C Sigma_bar]
    E --> L
    K --> M[Output Posterior Belief N mu_t, Sigma_t]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Next Time Step}
    N -- yes --> B
    N -- no --> O[Final Pose Estimate]
```

---

## 2. Conceptual Map-Based Localization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Environment Map m] --> FE[Feature Extraction]
    S1[LiDAR / Camera] --> FE
    FE --> Z[Measurement Vector z_t]
    U[Wheel Odometry] --> U1[Control Vector u_t]
    KF[Kalman Filter Engine] --> O[Robot Pose mu_t, Sigma_t]
    U1 --> KF
    Z --> KF
    KF --> PLC[Path Planning and Control]
    PLC --> ACT[Motor Commands to Wheels]
    ACT --> U
```

---

## 3. Comparison: KF vs. EKF Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LinearKF[Standard Kalman Filter]
        L1[Linear Motion Model] --> L2[Linear Measurement Model]
        L2 --> L3[Direct Matrix Multiplication]
    end
    subgraph ExtendedKF[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
        E1[Non Linear Motion Model] --> E2[Compute Jacobian F_t]
        E2 --> E3[Compute Jacobian H_t]
        E3 --> E4[Linearized Update]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** Node IDs above use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `LinearKF`, `ExtendedKF`) and clean uppercase text inside labels to comply with KTU rendering safeguards. Reserved keywords such as `end` and `graph` are avoided as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Kalman Filter and state the two fundamental assumptions required for its application.**

**Model Answer:**
The Kalman Filter is an optimal recursive estimator used to compute the posterior belief of a continuous state from noisy sensor measurements and control inputs. The two fundamental assumptions are:
1. The system dynamics and measurement models are **linear** in the state variables.
2. All noise processes and the initial state are **Gaussian** (i.e., normally distributed) and independent.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Kalman Gain? Explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The Kalman Gain $K_t$ is the optimal weighting factor that determines how much the new sensor measurement influences the updated state estimate. Physically, it represents the *relative trustworthiness* of the measurement versus the prediction. If $K_t \approx 1$, the measurement is highly reliable (low $Q_t$); if $K_t \approx 0$, the prediction is highly trusted (low $R_t$).

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the recursive equations of the **Kalman Filter** algorithm under linear-Gaussian assumptions. Clearly state the prediction (time update) and correction (measurement update) steps, defining all variables used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot moves in 1D with the motion model $x_t = x_{t-1} + 0.5 + \varepsilon_t$, where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.2)$. The initial state is $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 1.0$. The first measurement is $z_1 = 0.8$ with $C = 1$ and $Q = 0.4$. Compute the posterior estimate $\mu_1$ and $\Sigma_1$ after the first update. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1: State the Linear Models**

Motion model: $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

Measurement model: $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$, with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

**Step 2: Prediction Step**

The predicted belief is obtained by propagating the previous Gaussian through the linear motion model:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

**Step 3: Kalman Gain Derivation**

By minimizing the trace of the posterior covariance, the optimal gain is:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Correction Step**

The innovation is $\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$. The posterior belief is:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

This completes the recursive cycle.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $A = 1$, $B = 1$, $u_1 = 0.5$, $R = 0.2$, $z_1 = 0.8$, $C = 1$, $Q = 0.4$, $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 1.0$.

**Step 1: Prediction of Mean**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = 1 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Prediction of Covariance**

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = 1 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1 + 0.2 = 1.2
$$

**Step 3: Compute Innovation**

$$
\nu_1 = z_1 - C \bar{\mu}_1 = 0.8 - 0.5 = 0.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Kalman Gain**

$$
K_1 = \frac{1.2}{1.2 + 0.4} = \frac{1.2}{1.6} = 0.75
$$

**Step 5: Compute Posterior Mean**

$$
\mu_1 = 0.5 + 0.75 \cdot 0.3 = 0.5 + 0.225 = 0.725 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Posterior Covariance**

$$
\Sigma_1 = (1 - 0.75) \cdot 1.2 = 0.25 \cdot 1.2 = 0.30
$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_1 = 0.725$ m, $\Sigma_1 = 0.30$

---

### Question B — Alternative (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **probabilistic map-based localization**. Compare the Kalman Filter approach with the Histogram Filter and the Particle Filter, highlighting linearity, state space, and computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the working of an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for a non-linear robot localization problem. Why is linearization necessary, and what are the limitations of EKF? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Concept and Comparison

Probabilistic map-based localization estimates the robot's pose within a known map by maintaining a probability distribution (belief) over all possible poses. The belief is recursively updated using Bayes' Rule.

| Property | Kalman Filter | Histogram Filter | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State Representation** | Continuous Gaussian | Discrete grid | Set of weighted samples |
| **Linearity** | Requires linear models (EKF for non-linear) | Can handle any distribution | Handles any distribution |
| **Computational Cost** | Low ($O(n^2)$) | Medium (grid size) | High (number of particles) |
| **Accuracy** | Optimal for linear-Gaussian | Approximate, grid-limited | Asymptotically exact |
| **Best Use Case** | Tracking in feature-rich maps | Global localization, discrete spaces | Global localization, non-Gaussian noise |

#### Part (b) — EKF Block Diagram and Linearization

**Need for Linearization:** Real robots exhibit non-linear motion (e.g., odometry with $\sin\theta, \cos\theta$) and non-linear measurements (e.g., range-bearing to landmarks). The standard Kalman Filter cannot be applied directly; hence, the system is *linearized* about the current estimate using first-order Taylor expansion (Jacobians).

**Key EKF Equations:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \quad \text{(non-linear motion)}
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

Where $G_t$ and $H_t$ are the Jacobians of $g(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ evaluated at the current state.

**Limitations of EKF:**
- Only first-order approximation; large errors accumulate when the system is highly non-linear.
- Requires differentiability of models.
- Sensitive to initial state estimate (can diverge).
- Computationally expensive Jacobians in high-dimensional states.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the predicted covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ with the posterior $\Sigma_t$; mark deduction of 2 marks is common.
> - The transpose on $A_t$ in $A_t \Sigma A_t^T$ is **mandatory** — students frequently omit it.
> - Forgetting to **add the process noise $R_t$** after matrix multiplication in prediction costs 1 mark.
> - Kalman Gain is **not symmetric**; writing $K_t = C^T (C \bar{\Sigma} C^T + Q)^{-1} \bar{\Sigma}$ instead of $\bar{\Sigma} C^T (\cdot)^{-1}$ is a frequent mistake.
> - EKF requires *Jacobians* $G_t$ and $H_t$, not the matrices $A_t$ and $C_t$ — many students incorrectly reuse the linear KF matrices.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two Phases:** *Prediction* (motion) and *Correction* (measurement) — memorize the equation order.
- **Gaussian Assumption:** Both the prior belief and the noise are multivariate normal distributions.
- **Markov Assumption:** The current state is conditionally independent of past states given the previous state.
- **Innovation Vector:** $z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$ represents the *discrepancy* between predicted and observed values.
- **Kalman Gain $K_t$:** Optimal weight, derived by minimizing posterior covariance trace.
- **Initialization:** $\mu_0$ and $\Sigma_0$ must be specified; $\Sigma_0 = 0$ implies perfect initial knowledge.
- **EKF = Linearized KF:** Used for non-linear motion/measurement models; uses Jacobians $G_t, H_t$.
- **Uncertainty Behavior:** Covariance *grows* during prediction (adds $R_t$) and *shrinks* during correction.
- **Failure Modes:** Divergence under high non-linearity, sensitivity to noise covariance tuning, singularity if $C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t$ is not invertible.
- **KTU High-Yield Diagrams:** Always include a block diagram of the prediction-correction loop.
- **Common Mark Scheme:** Derivation of Kalman Gain (3 marks), Prediction equations (2 marks), Correction equations (2 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Map Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Map Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Autonomous Map Building** is the process by which a mobile robot incrementally constructs, updates, and maintains a spatial representation of an unknown environment using onboard sensors, while simultaneously estimating its own pose within that evolving map. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is tightly coupled with **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**, which is formally defined as the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's location within it.

The joint posterior probability distribution governing the SLAM problem is expressed as:

$$p(x_{1:t},\ m \mid z_{1:t},\ u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_{1:t}$ represents the sequence of robot poses from time 1 to $t$
- $m$ denotes the map of the environment
- $z_{1:t}$ represents the sequence of sensor measurements
- $u_{1:t}$ represents the sequence of control inputs (odometry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Autonomous map building is the foundation for truly autonomous navigation. Without an accurate map, a robot cannot perform path planning, obstacle avoidance, or task execution in unknown environments. This is a **14-mark high-weightage** topic that combines probability theory, sensor fusion, and algorithmic reasoning.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a completely unknown building and asked to escape through the exit. You have a walking stick, a tape recorder, and a compass. As you walk, you:
1. **Estimate your own position** (using the stick to feel walls, counting steps as crude odometry, using the compass for orientation)
2. **Sketch the layout** of rooms, corridors, and obstacles on a notepad
3. **Recognize when you return to a familiar place** (loop closure) and correct any accumulated errors in your sketch

This simultaneous "figuring out where you are" and "drawing the map" is exactly what autonomous map building means for a robot. The robot's sensors act as the walking stick and compass, while its algorithms serve as the brain that fuses all information into a coherent map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The chicken-and-egg problem in SLAM — *you need a map to localize, but you need localization to build a map* — is the fundamental challenge. Probabilistic methods like **Bayesian filtering** resolve this by maintaining a joint probability distribution over both the pose and the map.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used in autonomous map building:

- **Map Resolution:** Typically **0.05 m to 0.5 m** per cell for occupancy grids
- **Sensor Range:** **LiDAR: 0.1 m to 100 m**, Sonar: **0.1 m to 10 m**, Stereo Camera: **0.5 m to 20 m**
- **Update Rate:** **10 Hz to 40 Hz** for most mobile robot SLAM systems
- **Computational Complexity:** EKF-SLAM is $O(n^2)$ where $n$ is the number of landmarks
- **Path Length Scale:** Maps are typically built over distances of **10 m to 1 km**

### Types of Maps in Autonomous Map Building

| Map Type | Description | Storage | Best Use Case |
|----------|-------------|---------|---------------|
| **Metric (Grid)** | Discretizes space into uniform cells with occupancy values | $O(N^2)$ for $N \times N$ grid | Indoor environments, obstacle avoidance |
| **Feature-based** | Stores geometric primitives (points, lines, corners) | $O(n)$ for $n$ features | Structured environments, outdoor |
| **Topological** | Nodes represent places, edges represent connections | $O(n)$ for $n$ nodes | Large-scale navigation |
| **Hybrid** | Combines metric and topological representations | Variable | Real-world complex environments |
| **Semantic** | Adds object labels (door, chair, wall) to metric maps | $O(n)$ with labels | Human-robot interaction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The SLAM Problem Decomposition

The SLAM problem is decomposed into two interacting sub-problems through probabilistic factorization:

$$p(x_{1:t},\ m \mid z_{1:t},\ u_{1:t}) = p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t},\ u_{1:t}) \cdot p(m \mid x_{1:t},\ z_{1:t})$$

This means:
- The **localization posterior** $p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t},\ u_{1:t})$ estimates where the robot has been
- The **map posterior** $p(m \mid x_{1:t},\ z_{1:t})$ builds the map conditioned on the robot trajectory

### Why and How: The Bayesian SLAM Framework

**The Why:**
- Sensors are noisy → we cannot trust a single measurement
- Odometry drifts over time → we need correction from observations
- The environment is unknown → we cannot pre-program the map
- Mathematical rigor is required to fuse uncertain information over time

**The How (operational steps):**

1. **Prediction Step:** Use the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ to predict the new pose based on control input
2. **Measurement Update:** Use the observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ to correct the predicted pose
3. **Map Update:** Add new observations to the map using inverse sensor models
4. **Loop Closure Detection:** Identify when the robot revisits a previously mapped area
5. **Global Optimization:** Apply graph-based optimization (e.g., Pose Graph SLAM) to redistribute errors

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---------|---------|-------------|-----------|
| SLAM Posterior | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Joint posterior over poses and map | $x$: pose, $m$: map, $z$: measurements, $u$: controls |
| Bayes Filter | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Recursive state estimation | $\eta$: normalizer |
| Motion Model | $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ | Probabilistic motion with noise | $w_t$: motion noise |
| Observation Model | $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$ | Probabilistic sensor model | $v_t$: sensor noise |
| Occupancy Update | $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inverse\_sensor}(m_i, x_t, z_t) - l_0$ | Log-odds update for grid cell $i$ | $l_0 = 0$ (prior) |
| EKF State Vector | $\mu_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t, l_{1x}, l_{1y}, ..., l_{nx}, l_{ny}]^T$ | Robot pose + landmark positions | $n$: number of landmarks |
| EKF Covariance | $\Sigma_t$ of size $(3+2n) \times (3+2n)$ | Uncertainty in SLAM state | Grows quadratically |
| Particle Filter Weight | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Importance weight for particle $i$ | $m$: current map |
| Loop Closure Gain | $H_l = J^T \Sigma^{-1} J$ | Information gain from loop closure | $J$: Jacobian |
| ICP Error | $E(R, t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \vert q_i - (R p_i + t) \vert \vert^2$ | Iterative Closest Point scan matching | $R, t$: rotation and translation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The **log-odds representation** of occupancy grids is mathematically equivalent to the probability form but computationally more efficient because it converts multiplicative updates into additive updates. This appears frequently in KTU numerical problems.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Companies like Waymo, Tesla, and Cruise use SLAM variants to build HD maps and localize in real-time
2. **Warehouse Robotics:** Amazon's Kiva robots use SLAM to navigate fulfillment centers without floor markings
3. **Search and Rescue:** Robots map disaster zones (collapsed buildings, minefields) where GPS is unavailable
4. **Underwater Exploration:** AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles) use SLAM to map ocean floors
5. **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance) use visual SLAM for autonomous navigation
6. **Domestic Robots:** Robot vacuums (Roomba) use lightweight SLAM for home mapping

### The Three Pillars of Autonomous Map Building

**Pillar 1: Perception (Sensing)**
- Range sensors: LiDAR, ultrasonic, infrared
- Visual sensors: monocular, stereo, RGB-D cameras
- Proprioceptive sensors: wheel odometry, IMU

**Pillar 2: Representation (Memory)**
- Choosing the right map type for the environment
- Managing memory efficiently (for large-scale maps)
- Handling dynamic objects (people, moving cars)

**Pillar 3: Reasoning (Intelligence)**
- Probabilistic inference to handle uncertainty
- Data association to match observations to landmarks
- Loop closure to correct accumulated drift

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Bayesian Filter for Map Building

We derive the recursive Bayesian update for occupancy grid mapping.

**Step 1: Start with the prior map belief**

Let $m_i$ be the occupancy state of cell $i$ (occupied = 1, free = 0). The prior belief is:

$$p(m_i) = 0.5 \quad \text{(maximum uncertainty, equal probability of free and occupied)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' rule to update with measurement $z_t$ at pose $x_t$**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 3: Introduce the odds representation**

Define the **odds** of cell $i$ being occupied:

$$\text{odds}(m_i) = \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

Taking the ratio of the posterior odds to the prior odds gives the **Bayes factor**:

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 4: Convert to log-odds for computational efficiency**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 5: Define the log-odds notation**

Let $l_{t,i} = \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$ and let the **inverse sensor model** be:

$$\text{inv\_sensor}(m_i, x_t, z_t) = \log \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid x_t, z_t)}{p(m_i = 0 \mid x_t, z_t)} = \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)}$$

**Step 6: Obtain the final recursive update equation**

The complete recursive occupancy grid update rule is:

$$l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(m_i, x_t, z_t) - l_0$$

where $l_0 = \log \frac{p(m_i = 1)}{p(m_i = 0)} = \log 1 = 0$ for the uniform prior.

**Why this matters:** This elegant additive form is what makes occupancy grid mapping computationally tractable. Each new measurement simply adds its log-odds contribution, and we can recover the probability via:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_{t,i})}$$

---

### Derivation 2: EKF-SLAM Prediction and Update Steps

The Extended Kalman Filter SLAM maintains a Gaussian estimate over the augmented state vector.

**Step 1: Define the augmented state vector**

$$\mu_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \\ l_{1x} \\ l_{1y} \\ \vdots \\ l_{nx} \\ l_{ny} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3+2n}$$

The corresponding covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$ has dimensions $(3+2n) \times (3+2n)$.

**Step 2: Motion prediction (Time Update)**

The state prediction is computed using the nonlinear motion model $g$:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$$

For a differential drive robot, the motion model is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_t &= x_{t-1} + \Delta x_t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) - \Delta y_t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \\
\bar{y}_t &= y_{t-1} + \Delta x_t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) + \Delta y_t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \\
\bar{\theta}_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Linearize the motion model using the Jacobian**

The Jacobian $G_t$ of the motion model with respect to the state is computed:

$$G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial (x, y, \theta)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta x_t \sin\theta - \Delta y_t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta x_t \cos\theta - \Delta y_t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Propagate the covariance**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

where $R_t$ is the motion noise covariance matrix:

$$R_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_y^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_\theta^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Measurement prediction (Observation Update)**

For observing landmark $j$ with measurement $z_t = [r, \phi]^T$ (range and bearing):

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_{jx} - \bar{x}_t)^2 + (l_{jy} - \bar{y}_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_{jy} - \bar{y}_t, l_{jx} - \bar{x}_t) - \bar{\theta}_t \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Kalman gain**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

where $H_t$ is the Jacobian of the observation model and $Q_t$ is the sensor noise covariance.

**Step 7: Update state and covariance with measurement**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - \hat{z}_t)$$
$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Why this matters:** EKF-SLAM shows that the **cross-correlation terms** in $\Sigma_t$ between the robot pose and landmarks are what make SLAM tractable. When the robot observes a known landmark, both the pose uncertainty AND the landmark uncertainty reduce simultaneously — this is the magic of SLAM.

---

### Derivation 3: Data Association using Maximum Likelihood

When a measurement $z_t$ is observed, we must decide which landmark it corresponds to.

**Step 1: Compute the innovation**

$$v_t^j = z_t - \hat{z}_t^j$$

This is the difference between the actual measurement and the predicted measurement for landmark $j$.

**Step 2: Compute the innovation covariance**

$$S_t^j = H_t^j \bar{\Sigma}_t (H_t^j)^T + Q_t$$

**Step 3: Compute the Mahalanobis distance**

$$D_j^2 = (v_t^j)^T (S_t^j)^{-1} v_t^j$$

**Step 4: Apply the validation gate**

The measurement is associated with landmark $j^*$ if:

$$D_j^2 \leq \chi^2_{d, \alpha}$$

where $\chi^2_{d, \alpha}$ is the chi-squared threshold with $d$ degrees of freedom (2 for range-bearing) and significance level $\alpha$ (typically 0.05).

**Why this matters:** Incorrect data association causes catastrophic failure in SLAM. A single wrong association can corrupt the entire map. The Mahalanobis distance provides a statistically principled way to validate associations.

---

### Numerical Example: Occupancy Grid Update

Suppose a robot observes cell $i$ and the inverse sensor model returns the log-odds value $0.85$ (indicating evidence of occupation). The previous log-odds was $l_{t-1,i} = 0.30$.

**Step 1: Apply the update rule**

$$l_{t,i} = 0.30 + 0.85 - 0 = 1.15$$

**Step 2: Convert back to probability**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{1.15}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 3.158} = 1 - 0.2405 = 0.7595$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

The probability of cell $i$ being occupied has increased from approximately $57.4\%$ (corresponding to $l=0.30$) to $75.95\%$ (corresponding to $l=1.15$).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the SLAM problem mathematically. What are the two key sub-problems it addresses?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** problem is formally defined as estimating the joint posterior probability distribution:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $x_{1:t}$ is the robot trajectory, $m$ is the map, $z_{1:t}$ are sensor measurements, and $u_{1:t}$ are control inputs.

**The two key sub-problems are:**
1. **Localization:** Estimating the robot's pose $x_t$ given the map and sensor data
2. **Mapping:** Constructing the map $m$ given the robot's trajectory and sensor data

These are intertwined because accurate localization requires a good map, and accurate mapping requires precise localization.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between metric and topological maps. Give one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|--------|------------|-----------------|
| Representation | Continuous or discretized coordinates | Graph with nodes (places) and edges (paths) |
| Storage | $O(N^2)$ for $N \times N$ grid | $O(n)$ for $n$ nodes |
| Precision | High geometric accuracy | Approximate, relative only |
| Best For | Small indoor spaces | Large-scale environments |

**Advantage of Metric Map:** Precise obstacle avoidance because the robot knows exact distances to nearby objects.

**Advantage of Topological Map:** Scales efficiently to very large environments (entire cities) and supports high-level reasoning about connectivity.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Bayesian update rule for occupancy grid mapping. Derive the log-odds recursive update equation.**

**(b) [7 Marks] A robot observes a cell three times. The inverse sensor model returns log-odds values of 0.6, -0.4, and 0.9 for the three observations respectively. Compute the final probability that the cell is occupied.**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Bayesian Update Rule for Occupancy Grid Mapping:**

Let $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ denote the occupancy of cell $i$ (0 = free, 1 = occupied). We want to estimate $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ recursively.

**Step 1: Bayes' Rule Application**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 2: Odds Ratio Formulation**

Taking the odds of the posterior divided by the odds of the prior:

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 3: Log-Odds Transformation**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(m_i, x_t, z_t) - l_0$$

where the **log-odds** is defined as:

$$l_{t,i} = \ln \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$$

and the **inverse sensor model** is:

$$\text{inv\_sensor}(m_i, x_t, z_t) = \ln \frac{p(m_i = 1 \mid x_t, z_t)}{p(m_i = 0 \mid x_t, z_t)}$$

For a uniform prior, $l_0 = 0$.

**Step 4: Recovering the Probability**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_{t,i})}$$

**Key Advantage:** The log-odds form is **additive**, making updates computationally efficient. Each measurement simply adds its contribution to the running sum, avoiding repeated multiplication of probabilities.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Three observations with inverse sensor model log-odds values:
- Observation 1: $0.6$
- Observation 2: $-0.4$
- Observation 3: $0.9$

**Step 1: Initialize the log-odds**

With a uniform prior, the initial log-odds is:

$$l_{0,i} = 0$$

**Step 2: Apply the recursive update for each observation**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{1,i} &= l_{0,i} + 0.6 - 0 = 0.6 \\
l_{2,i} &= l_{1,i} + (-0.4) - 0 = 0.6 - 0.4 = 0.2 \\
l_{3,i} &= l_{2,i} + 0.9 - 0 = 0.2 + 0.9 = 1.1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Convert the final log-odds to probability**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:3}, x_{1:3}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(1.1)}$$

Calculating $\exp(1.1)$:

$$\exp(1.1) \approx 3.0042$$

Therefore:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:3}, x_{1:3}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 3.0042} = 1 - \frac{1}{4.0042} = 1 - 0.2497 = 0.7503$$

**Final Answer:** The probability that the cell is occupied is approximately **$0.7503$ or $75.03\%$**.

**Interpretation:** The first observation provided strong positive evidence ($+0.6$). The second observation provided negative evidence ($-0.4$), reducing the certainty. The third observation added strong positive evidence ($+0.9$), resulting in a net belief that the cell is more likely occupied than free.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the EKF-SLAM algorithm with its prediction and update steps. Explain why the cross-correlation terms in the covariance matrix are crucial for SLAM consistency.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the computational complexity of EKF-SLAM and FastSLAM. For a map with 500 landmarks, which algorithm is preferred and why?**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**EKF-SLAM Algorithm:**

The Extended Kalman Filter SLAM maintains a Gaussian estimate $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ over the augmented state:

$$\mu_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t, l_{1x}, l_{1y}, ..., l_{nx}, l_{ny}]^T$$

**Step 1: Prediction (Time Update)**

Using the motion model $g$ and control input $u_t$:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$$

Computing the Jacobian $G_t$ of the motion model:

$$G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta x_t \sin\theta - \Delta y_t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta x_t \cos\theta - \Delta y_t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Propagating the covariance:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 2: Update (Measurement Update)**

For a measurement of landmark $j$:

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$

Computing the Jacobian $H_t$ of the observation model and the Kalman gain:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

Updating the state and covariance:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - \hat{z}_t)$$
$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Why Cross-Correlation Terms are Crucial:**

The full covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$ has the structure:

$$\Sigma_t = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{xx} & \Sigma_{xm} \\ \Sigma_{mx} & \Sigma_{mm} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\Sigma_{xm}$ represents the **cross-correlation** between the robot pose and the landmarks.

**Key Insight:** When the robot observes a known landmark, the Kalman filter updates both the pose estimate AND the landmark estimate simultaneously, with the cross-correlation term $\Sigma_{xm}$ serving as the bridge.

**Mathematical Demonstration:** When the robot pose is updated, the change in pose estimate $\delta x$ induces a change in all landmark estimates $\delta m$ via:

$$\delta m = \Sigma_{mx} \Sigma_{xx}^{-1} \delta x$$

This means that improving pose knowledge (reducing $\Sigma_{xx}$) automatically improves map knowledge (reducing $\Sigma_{mm}$). This is the fundamental property that makes SLAM consistent over long trajectories.

**Consequence:** As the robot continues to operate, the cross-correlations grow stronger, and eventually, all landmarks become correlated with each other. This is why EKF-SLAM is **$O(n^2)$** in computation and storage.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Computational Complexity Comparison:**

**EKF-SLAM:**
- **State vector size:** $3 + 2n$ (3 for pose, 2 per landmark)
- **Covariance matrix size:** $(3+2n) \times (3+2n)$
- **Update cost per step:** $O(n^2)$ due to matrix multiplication and inversion
- **Total storage:** $O(n^2)$ for the covariance matrix

**FastSLAM:**
- Decomposes SLAM into $n+1$ independent problems:
  - 1 localization problem solved by particle filter: $O(M)$ for $M$ particles
  - $n$ independent 2D landmark EKF problems: $O(1)$ each
- **Update cost per step:** $O(M \log n)$ for tree-based data structures
- **Total storage:** $O(Mn)$ but can be reduced to $O(M \log n)$

**For a map with $n = 500$ landmarks:**

| Metric | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Covariance size | $1003 \times 1003$ matrix | $M$ particles, each with 500 tiny 2x2 EKFs |
| Per-step update | $\sim 10^6$ operations | $\sim 1000 \cdot \log 500 \approx 9000$ operations |
| Memory | $\sim 8$ MB (double precision) | $\sim 80$ KB per particle |

**Preferred Algorithm: FastSLAM**

**Reasons:**
1. **Scalability:** EKF-SLAM's $O(n^2)$ complexity becomes prohibitive for $n = 500$ landmarks. The covariance matrix inversion alone requires millions of operations per step.
2. **Practical real-time performance:** FastSLAM's particle filter structure allows parallelization and incremental updates.
3. **Decomposition benefit:** By treating landmarks as conditionally independent given the robot path, FastSLAM breaks the $O(n^2)$ curse.
4. **Empirical success:** FastSLAM has been demonstrated on maps with thousands of landmarks, while EKF-SLAM typically fails beyond a few hundred landmarks.
5. **Robustness to data association:** FastSLAM's Rao-Blackwellized formulation provides better handling of ambiguous data association in cluttered environments.

**Conclusion:** For a 500-landmark map, **FastSLAM is preferred** due to its superior computational efficiency, scalability, and proven performance in real-world deployments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing SLAM with Localization:** Students often write only the localization posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ without including the map. The full SLAM posterior is the **joint** distribution $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
> 2. **Log-odds mistakes:** Forgetting to subtract the prior $l_0$ or confusing the sign convention (positive = occupied, negative = free) costs 1-2 marks easily.
> 3. **Sigmoid transformation errors:** Writing $\frac{1}{1 + \exp(-l)}$ instead of $1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l)}$ is a common error. Always double-check the direction of the inequality.
> 4. **Complexity analysis:** Stating EKF-SLAM is $O(n^3)$ instead of $O(n^2)$. The cubic complexity is for **batch** matrix inversion; per-step update is quadratic.
> 5. **Mixing up EKF and FastSLAM formulas:** EKF maintains a **single Gaussian** over the augmented state. FastSLAM uses **particles** for the path plus **independent EKFs** for landmarks. This distinction is critical.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SLAM Core Equation:** The full problem is $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — joint posterior over trajectory and map
- **Map Types:** Metric (grids), Feature-based (landmarks), Topological (graphs), Hybrid, Semantic
- **Occupancy Grid Update:** $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(m_i, x_t, z_t) - l_0$
- **Probability Recovery:** $p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_{t,i})}$ — always use this exact form
- **EKF-SLAM Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ in both time and space, where $n$ is the number of landmarks
- **FastSLAM Complexity:** $O(M \log n)$ using Rao-Blackwellized particle filter decomposition
- **Loop Closure:** Identifying previously visited places to correct accumulated drift; triggers global optimization
- **Data Association:** Matching observations to landmarks using Mahalanobis distance; $D^2 \leq \chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ is the validation gate
- **Cross-Correlation in EKF-SLAM:** $\Sigma_{xm}$ links pose and landmark estimates — observing a landmark reduces both uncertainties
- **Sensors:** LiDAR (precise, expensive), Cameras (rich, processing-heavy), Sonar (cheap, noisy), IMU (inertial, high-rate)
- **Dynamic Environments:** Standard SLAM assumes static worlds; handling dynamic objects requires additional techniques (e.g., semantic segmentation, moving object detection)
- **Common Algorithms:** Occupancy Grid Mapping, EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM 1.0/2.0, GraphSLAM, Cartographer, ORB-SLAM
- **Probabilistic Foundation:** All SLAM methods are rooted in Bayesian inference; the choice of distribution (Gaussian, particles, grids) determines the algorithm
- **Open-Source Tools:** ROS (Robot Operating System), GMapping, Cartographer, RTAB-Map, ORB-SLAM2/3
- **Real-World Applications:** Autonomous vehicles, warehouse robots, search and rescue, planetary rovers, domestic vacuums
- **Key Trade-off:** Accuracy vs. Computational Cost — feature-based methods are precise but don't represent free space; grid-based methods are dense but memory-intensive
- **Initialization Challenge:** SLAM requires a known starting pose and a distinguishable initial set of landmarks; "kidnapped robot problem" remains an open research area

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is a foundational computational problem in autonomous robotics, formally defined as the process by which a mobile robot incrementally constructs a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that very map being built.

The problem is formally cast as a **state estimation problem** in the probabilistic framework. Given:
- The sequence of robot control inputs: $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$
- The sequence of sensor observations: $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$

SLAM seeks to compute the **posterior probability distribution** over both the robot's trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$ of the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Online SLAM Posterior (Most Common KTU Formulation):**
>
> $$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$
>
> This estimates the current pose $x_t$ and the map $m$ at the current time step only.

**Full SLAM Posterior (Alternate Formulation):**
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$
This estimates the entire robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ along with the map $m$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Variables in SLAM Notation (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> - $x_t$ — Robot pose at time $t$ (typically $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ for 2D or $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ for 3D)
> - $m = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n\}$ — Set of $n$ landmarks defining the map
> - $z_t$ — Observation vector at time $t$
> - $u_t$ — Control input at time $t$
> - $w_t$ — Process/measurement noise (assumed Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, Q)$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Explorer

Imagine being **blindfolded** and dropped in the middle of an unfamiliar building. You are given:
1. A **walking stick** (sensors) that tells you the distance to nearby walls and pillars.
2. A **step counter and compass** (odometry) that roughly estimates how far and in which direction you have walked.

You must:
- **Map** the building layout (rooms, doors, obstacles).
- **Localize** yourself — that is, determine exactly where you stand on the map you are still drawing.

Every step you take is uncertain. The compass drifts. The stick gives noisy readings. The more you walk, the more your map and position estimates **drift apart** — unless you use **SLAM**, which uses a clever recursive loop:

> **Estimate Pose → Observe Landmarks → Correct Pose → Update Map → Repeat**

This is the famous **"chicken-and-egg" problem** of robotics: *you cannot map well without knowing your pose, and you cannot localize well without a good map.* SLAM solves both jointly.

## 1.3 The Two Recursive Bayesian Update Steps

SLAM operates on two fundamental probabilistic models:

**1. Motion Model (State Transition):**
$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$$

**2. Observation/Measurement Model:**
$$z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$$

where $w_t$ and $v_t$ are zero-mean Gaussian noise vectors with covariances $Q_t$ and $R_t$ respectively.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is SLAM Hard?**
> - The map dimension grows unboundedly with new landmarks.
> - Each landmark introduces a correlation between pose and every other landmark estimate.
> - The full covariance matrix is of size $(3n+3) \times (3n+3)$ — computationally expensive.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Map resolution (2D) | **0.05 m – 0.30 m/cell** |
| Map resolution (3D) | **0.01 m – 0.10 m/voxel** |
| Update rate | **10 Hz – 100 Hz** |
| Acceptable localization error | **< 0.1 m** |
| Acceptable heading error | **< 1°** |
| Sensor range (Lidar) | **10 m – 100 m** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLAM Problem Decomposed

The SLAM problem is decomposed into three coupled sub-problems that must be solved recursively:

1. **Prediction Step (Time Update):** Use the motion model $f$ to predict the new pose from previous pose and control input.
2. **Observation/Update Step (Measurement Update):** Use the sensor model $h$ to correct the predicted pose by matching observed features with map landmarks.
3. **Map Augmentation:** Add newly observed, previously unseen landmarks to the state vector.

### Logical Flow of SLAM:
- **Step 1 → Initialization:** Begin with $x_0$ known and empty map $m = \{\}$.
- **Step 2 → Prediction:** Propagate pose estimate using $u_t$ via motion model $f$.
- **Step 3 → Observation Capture:** Acquire sensor reading $z_t$ and identify features.
- **Step 4 → Data Association:** Match observed features to existing landmarks (or flag as new).
- **Step 5 → Update:** Apply Bayesian filter (Kalman, Particle, etc.) to correct pose and landmark estimates.
- **Step 6 → Map Update:** Add new landmarks and their covariances to the map.
- **Step 7 → Loop Closure (Optional but critical):** Detect revisited places to globally correct the map.

## 2.2 Classification of SLAM Types

SLAM algorithms are classified along **multiple axes**. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following taxonomy:

### A. By Estimation Algorithm Used

| Type | Core Algorithm | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EKF-SLAM** | Extended Kalman Filter | Mathematically elegant, well-studied | Quadratic complexity $O(n^2)$ |
| **FastSLAM** | Particle Filter (Rao-Blackwellized) | Handles non-linearities well | Particle degeneracy |
| **Graph-SLAM** | Pose Graph Optimization | Globally consistent, sparse | Requires loop closures |
| **UKF-SLAM** | Unscented Kalman Filter | Better than EKF for high non-linearity | Still $O(n^2)$ |
| **DP-SLAM** | Distributed Particle SLAM | Multi-robot capable | Communication overhead |

### B. By Sensor Modality

- **Lidar-SLAM:** Uses 2D/3D laser scanners. High accuracy, robust to lighting.
- **Visual SLAM (vSLAM):** Uses monocular, stereo, or RGB-D cameras. Low cost, rich data.
- **Sonar SLAM:** Used underwater (AUVs) where light/laser fail.
- **Radar SLAM:** Robust to dust, smoke, and adverse weather.
- **Multi-sensor / Sensor Fusion SLAM:** Combines Lidar + Camera + IMU + GPS.

### C. By Map Representation

- **Metric SLAM:** Grid-based or feature-based with explicit coordinates.
- **Topological SLAM:** Nodes represent places, edges represent connectivity.
- **Hybrid (Topological + Metric):** Combines both benefits.

### D. By Dimensionality

- **2D SLAM:** Planar maps; common in indoor mobile robots.
- **3D SLAM:** Full volumetric maps; used in drones, autonomous cars.

### E. By Temporal Scope

- **Online SLAM:** $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — current pose only.
- **Full SLAM:** $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — entire trajectory.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Online SLAM posterior | Probability |
| 2 | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full SLAM posterior | Probability |
| 3 | $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ | Motion model | m, rad |
| 4 | $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$ | Observation model | m, rad |
| 5 | $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | Process noise | Covariance $\mathbb{R}^{3\times 3}$ |
| 6 | $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | Measurement noise | Covariance $\mathbb{R}^{2\times 2}$ |
| 7 | $\Sigma_t$ (EKF-SLAM) | Full state covariance matrix | $(3n+3)\times(3n+3)$ |
| 8 | $K_t = \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T (H \Sigma_{t \vert t-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | Dimensionless matrix |
| 9 | $M$ particles in FastSLAM | Computational cost $O(M \cdot n)$ | — |
| 10 | Information matrix $\Omega = \Lambda^T \Lambda$ | Graph-SLAM information form | Sparse matrix |

> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time 3D SLAM using Lidar + Camera fusion.
> - **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** 2D SLAM for inventory transport.
> - **Search and Rescue (DARPA SubT):** Multi-sensor SLAM in GPS-denied caves.
> - **AR/VR Devices:** Visual-Inertial SLAM for tracking headsets.
> - **Space Rovers (NASA):** Visual SLAM on Mars for terrain mapping.

## 2.4 The Chicken-and-Egg Coupling — Why Joint Estimation?

The localization problem requires the map, and the mapping problem requires accurate localization. Mathematically, the joint posterior is **inseparable** because:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \neq p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

The cross-correlation terms $p(x_t, m_i)$ between pose and landmarks cannot be neglected — they grow with every observation, which is the *key insight* that makes SLAM convergent (i.e., uncertainty in the map decreases as the robot explores).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the SLAM Posterior

The SLAM posterior is derived from first principles of Bayesian filtering.

### Step 1 — Express the Posterior Using Bayes' Theorem

By definition of conditional probability:

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Explanation:** We invert the conditional probability of observations $z_t$ to obtain the posterior over the joint state $(x_t, m)$. The denominator is a normalization constant ensuring the posterior integrates to 1.

### Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption

The current observation $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and map $m$, not on the history. This is the **Markov property**:

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)
$$

**Explanation:** The robot's sensors take instantaneous readings — past observations do not affect the current measurement, given the current state.

### Step 3 — Marginalize Over the Previous Pose

The joint prior $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is obtained by integrating (marginalizing) over the previous pose $x_{t-1}$:

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **Chapman–Kolmogorov prediction** — we propagate the previous posterior through the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.

### Step 4 — Recursive Bayesian Form Achieved

Combining Steps 1–3, we obtain the **recursive SLAM update equation**:

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

where $\eta = 1/p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is the normalizing constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **This recursive form is the foundation of all SLAM algorithms** — EKF, Particle Filter, and Graph-based SLAM differ only in how they approximate this integral.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: EKF-SLAM Linearized Update Equations

For **EKF-SLAM**, we linearize the non-linear models via first-order Taylor expansion.

### Step 1 — Linearize the Motion Model

Expand $f$ around the current estimate $\hat{x}_{t-1}$:

$$
f(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t) + F_t (x_{t-1} - \hat{x}_{t-1}) + w_t
$$

The **Jacobian of the motion model** is:

$$
F_t = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right|_{\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t}
$$

**Explanation:** We approximate the non-linear motion model by its tangent plane at the operating point. The Jacobian $F_t$ propagates small perturbations.

### Step 2 — Linearize the Observation Model

Expand $h$ around the predicted state $\bar{x}_t$ and predicted map $\bar{m}$:

$$
h(x_t, m) \approx h(\bar{x}_t, \bar{m}) + H_t (x_t - \bar{x}_t) + v_t
$$

The **Jacobian of the observation model** is:

$$
H_t = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial (x, m)} \right|_{\bar{x}_t, \bar{m}}
$$

### Step 3 — Predicted State Mean and Covariance

$$
\bar{x}_t = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \hat{\Sigma}_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t
$$

**Explanation:** The predicted covariance grows due to the process noise $Q_t$ added at every step.

### Step 4 — Compute the Kalman Gain

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}
$$

**Explanation:** The Kalman gain $K_t$ weights how much we trust the new measurement $z_t$ versus the prediction. If $R_t$ is small (sensor is precise), $K_t$ is large and we trust the measurement more.

### Step 5 — Posterior State Update

$$
\hat{x}_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{x}_t, \bar{m}))
$$

$$
\hat{\Sigma}_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Explanation:** The innovation $z_t - h(\bar{x}_t, \bar{m})$ (measurement residual) is scaled by $K_t$ to correct the predicted state. The posterior covariance shrinks because we gained information.

### Step 6 — Complexity Analysis

The full state vector dimension is $N = 3 + 2n$ (3 for pose $(x, y, \theta)$ + 2 per landmark $(m_{x,i}, m_{y,i})$). The covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$ is of size $N \times N$, so:

$$
\text{Time complexity of EKF-SLAM update} = O(N^2) = O(n^2)
$$

This **quadratic complexity** is the chief limitation of EKF-SLAM for large maps.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of SLAM Types

| Property | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM 1.0/2.0 | Graph-SLAM | Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Estimator** | Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Nonlinear Least Squares | Bundle Adjustment + KF |
| **Time complexity** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(M \log n)$ | $O(n^3)$ per optimization | $O(n)$ per frame |
| **Map type** | Feature-based | Feature-based | Pose graph | Sparse feature map |
| **Non-linearity handling** | First-order linearization | Sample-based | Iterative optimization | Lie algebra on $SE(3)$ |
| **Loop closure** | Weak (no explicit relinearization) | Moderate | Native & strong | Yes (DBoW2) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (small maps, $n < 100$) | Good (medium maps) | Excellent (large maps) | Excellent |
| **Computational demand** | Low | Moderate (M particles) | High during optimization | Moderate |
| **Real-time capable** | Yes | Yes | Often batch/online variants | Yes (ORB-SLAM3) |
| **Typical sensor** | Lidar/Range | Lidar | Lidar/Camera | Monocular/Stereo/RGB-D |
| **Open-source examples** | — | — | Cartographer, g2o | ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level SLAM Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Initialized at x0] --> B[Acquire Control Input u_t]
    B --> C[Motion Model f - Prediction Step]
    C --> D[Predicted Pose and Covariance]
    D --> E[Acquire Sensor Observation z_t]
    E --> F[Data Association - Match to Map]
    F --> G{New or Existing Landmark?}
    G -- New Landmark --> H[Augment Map State]
    G -- Existing Landmark --> I[Compute Innovation Residual]
    I --> J[Compute Kalman Gain K_t]
    J --> K[Update Pose and Map]
    H --> K
    K --> L[Loop Closure Check]
    L -- Place Revisited --> M[Global Graph Optimization]
    L -- No Loop --> N[Output Updated Map and Pose]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Continue or Stop?}
    O -- Continue --> B
    O -- Stop --> P[Final Consistent Map and Trajectory]
```

## 4.2 SLAM Algorithm Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[SLAM Taxonomy]
    Root --> A1[By Algorithm]
    Root --> A2[By Sensor]
    Root --> A3[By Map Type]
    Root --> A4[By Dimensionality]

    A1 --> B1[EKF SLAM]
    A1 --> B2[FastSLAM - Particle Filter]
    A1 --> B3[Graph SLAM]
    A1 --> B4[UKF SLAM]

    A2 --> C1[Lidar SLAM]
    A2 --> C2[Visual SLAM vSLAM]
    A2 --> C3[Sonar SLAM]
    A2 --> C4[Multi-Sensor Fusion SLAM]

    A3 --> D1[Metric SLAM]
    A3 --> D2[Topological SLAM]
    A3 --> D3[Hybrid SLAM]

    A4 --> E1[2D SLAM]
    A4 --> E2[3D SLAM]
    A4 --> E3[2.5D Elevation SLAM]
```

## 4.3 EKF-SLAM Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMU[IMU Odometry u_t] --> Pred[Prediction Block]
    PrevState[Previous State x_t-1] --> Pred
    Pred[Prediction x_t_predicted, Sigma_pred] --> Innov[Innovation Computation]

    Lidar[2D Lidar Scan z_t] --> DA[Data Association]
    Map[Known Landmarks m] --> DA
    DA --> Innov[Innovation Computation]

    Innov --> KF[Kalman Gain Computation]
    KF --> Upd[State and Covariance Update]
    Upd --> NewState[Updated State x_t and Sigma_t]
    Upd --> MapAug[Map Augmentation if new landmark]
    MapAug --> NewState

    NewState --> Out[Publish to Path Planner and Mapper]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the EKF-SLAM block diagram above, trace the two parallel streams — the **odometry stream** (left) and the **sensor stream** (right) — they **converge** at the innovation computation block, which is the heart of the Kalman filter update.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Write the mathematical posterior formulation for Online SLAM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
SLAM is the computational problem in which a mobile robot simultaneously estimates its pose and constructs a map of an unknown environment, using sensor observations and control inputs.

The **Online SLAM posterior** is defined as:

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $x_t$ is the current robot pose, $m$ is the map, $z_{1:t}$ are observations up to time $t$, and $u_{1:t}$ are control inputs up to time $t$. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for posterior, 1 mark for variable explanation)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Online SLAM and Full SLAM with mathematical formulations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Online SLAM | Full SLAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Posterior** | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ |
| **Estimates** | Only current pose $x_t$ | Entire trajectory $x_{1:t}$ |
| **Computational cost** | Lower (constant) | Higher (grows with time) |
| **Use case** | Real-time navigation | Post-processing, mapping |
| **Memory** | Bounded | Grows linearly with trajectory |

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
