### ⭐ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Derive the recursive Bayesian formulation of the SLAM problem and explain the role of the motion and observation models. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Posterior (1 Mark):**

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

This is the joint posterior over the current pose $x_t$ and the map $m$ given the history of observations and controls.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule (1 Mark):**

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $\eta$ is the normalization constant.

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov Assumption (1 Mark):**

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)
$$

The current observation depends only on the current pose and map, not on history.

**Step 4 — Integrate (Marginalize) over Previous Pose (2 Marks):**

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This is the **Chapman–Kolmogorov prediction** through the motion model.

**Step 5 — Final Recursive Form (1 Mark):**

$$
\boxed{p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}
$$

**Step 6 — Role of Models (1 Mark):**
- The **motion model** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ propagates the pose estimate forward in time using odometry.
- The **observation model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ corrects the pose estimate using sensor data and known landmark locations.

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the EKF-SLAM algorithm with its key equations and discuss its computational complexity. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — EKF-SLAM Overview (1 Mark):**
EKF-SLAM uses the Extended Kalman Filter to jointly estimate the robot pose and all landmark positions in a single Gaussian state vector. The state is augmented with each new landmark.

**Step 2 — Augmented State Vector (1 Mark):**

$$
X_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ m_1 \\ m_2 \\ \vdots \\ m_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(3+2n) \times (3+2n)}
$$

**Step 3 — Prediction Step (2 Marks):**

$$
\bar{x}_t = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t), \qquad \bar{\Sigma}_t = F_t \hat{\Sigma}_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t
$$

where $F_t$ is the Jacobian of the motion model.

**Step 4 — Update Step (2 Marks):**

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{x}_t, \bar{m}))
$$

$$
\hat{\Sigma}_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Step 5 — Complexity Discussion (1 Mark):**
The full covariance matrix update costs $O(n^2)$ per step. For $n > 100$ landmarks, real-time performance becomes infeasible, limiting EKF-SLAM to small-scale environments.

---

### ⭐ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — Compare and contrast EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM algorithms across at least six parameters. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Core estimator** | Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Pose Graph Optimization |
| **2. State representation** | Single joint Gaussian | $M$ particles × $n$ independent EKF landmark filters | Sparse pose graph |
| **3. Time complexity** | $O(n^2)$ per step | $O(M \log n)$ per step | $O(n^3)$ per optimization (but sparse) |
| **4. Non-linearity handling** | First-order Taylor linearization | Sample-based (no linearization) | Iterative non-linear least squares |
| **5. Loop closure** | Weak (no global relinearization) | Moderate (resampling-based) | Strong (global optimization) |
| **6. Scalability** | Poor ($n < 100$) | Good (medium maps) | Excellent (large-scale, e.g., Google Cartographer) |
| **7. Typical use** | Small indoor, educational | Research benchmarks | Real-world deployment |

**(Any six parameters — 1 mark each, with brief explanation)**

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Graph-SLAM is the modern preferred choice for large-scale maps due to sparsity exploitation, while EKF-SLAM is mainly pedagogical and FastSLAM bridges the two eras.

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the FastSLAM algorithm. How does it factorize the SLAM posterior? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Posterior Factorization (2 Marks):**

FastSLAM factorizes the SLAM posterior using the **Rao-Blackwellization** technique:

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} p(m_i \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})
$$

**Explanation:** Instead of estimating the joint state, FastSLAM first samples robot trajectories $x_{1:t}$ using a particle filter, then conditions the map estimate on each trajectory. Given the trajectory, the landmarks become **conditionally independent**, allowing each to be estimated by a **separate 2×2 EKF**.

**Step 2 — Particle Filter for Trajectory (2 Marks):**
- Each particle $k$ holds:
  - A robot trajectory hypothesis $x_{1:t}^{[k]}$
  - $n$ independent 2D EKF landmark estimators (means $\mu_{i}^{[k]}$, covariances $\Sigma_{i}^{[k]}$)
- Particles are sampled from the proposal distribution $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[k]}, u_t)$ and re-weighted by the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[k]}, m^{[k]})$.

**Step 3 — Landmark EKF Updates (1 Mark):**
For each observed landmark $i$ in particle $k$:

$$
K_{i,t}^{[k]} = \Sigma_{i,t-1}^{[k]} H_i^T (H_i \Sigma_{i,t-1}^{[k]} H_i^T + R)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_{i,t}^{[k]} = \mu_{i,t-1}^{[k]} + K_{i,t}^{[k]} (z_t - \hat{z}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_{i,t}^{[k]} = (I - K_{i,t}^{[k]} H_i) \Sigma_{i,t-1}^{[k]}
$$

**Step 4 — Complexity Reduction (1 Mark):**
- Total complexity: $O(M \cdot n)$ instead of $O(n^2)$ in EKF-SLAM.
- The 2D EKF per landmark is constant-time.

**Step 5 — Resampling & Degeneracy (1 Mark):**
- Particles are resampled based on weights to combat degeneracy.
- **Particle depletion** (loss of diversity) is mitigated by:
  - Increasing particle count $M$.
  - Using FastSLAM 2.0 with improved proposal distribution incorporating measurements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (2024 Pattern)**
> - **Confusing Online vs Full SLAM:** Many students write $p(m \mid z_{1:t})$ instead of $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ and lose **1 mark instantly**.
> - **Forgetting Jacobians:** In EKF-SLAM derivations, omitting $F_t$ and $H_t$ Jacobians costs **1.5 marks** — they are explicitly asked.
> - **Mixing up Motion and Observation Models:** Motion model uses $u_t$; observation model uses $z_t$. Confusing them in equations is a **1-mark penalty**.
> - **Skipping Normalization Constant $\eta$:** In Bayes' rule derivations, $\eta$ must be written — examiners allocate a separate sub-mark.
> - **Complexity Analysis:** Simply writing "$O(n^2)$" without explaining *why* (due to covariance matrix size) loses partial credit.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **SLAM = Joint Estimation of Pose + Map**, formalized as a posterior probability distribution.
- [x] **Online SLAM posterior:** $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — **most asked KTU formulation**.
- [x] **Full SLAM posterior:** $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — entire trajectory.
- [x] **Two key models:** Motion model $f(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and Observation model $h(x_t, m)$.
- [x] **Markov Assumption:** Current observation depends only on current state — simplifies recursion.
- [x] **EKF-SLAM:** Single Gaussian, $O(n^2)$ complexity, Jacobian $F_t$ and $H_t$ required.
- [x] **FastSLAM:** Factorized posterior via Rao-Blackwellization, particle filter + per-landmark EKFs.
- [x] **Graph-SLAM:** Sparse graph optimization, handles loop closures natively.
- [x] **Visual SLAM (vSLAM):** Monocular/Stereo/RGB-D cameras, e.g., ORB-SLAM3.
- [x] **Sensor types:** Lidar, Camera, Sonar, Radar, IMU, multi-sensor fusion.
- [x] **Map types:** Metric (grid/feature), Topological, Hybrid.
- [x] **Loop closure detection** is critical for global consistency in modern SLAM.
- [x] **SLAM is the "chicken-and-egg" problem:** mapping needs pose, localization needs map.
- [x] **Modern applications:** Autonomous cars, AR/VR, warehouse robots, space rovers, drones, rescue robots.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** Always write the **posterior**, the **two models**, the **Jacobians** (if EKF), and the **complexity** — these four items appear in nearly every KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning - Graph Search, Breadth First Search, Depth First Search, Dijkstra's Algorithm, A*, D*
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Path Planning - Graph Search, Breadth First Search, Depth First Search, Dijkstra's Algorithm, A*, D* # 🧭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Path Planning? **Path Planning** in mobile robotics is the computational problem of finding a continuous, collision-free trajectory from a **start configuration** to a **goal configuration** through a workspace containing obstacles. In graph-based path planning, the continuous workspace is discretized into a finite graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ represents discrete states (nodes) and $E$ represents feasible transitions (edges) between them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Path Planning is the process of determining a geometric path from an initial state to a final state such that the path is **collision-free**, **feasible**, and (ideally) **optimal** with respect to a chosen cost metric (distance, time, energy, or risk). ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kerala Road Trip Planner" 🗺️ Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram** to **Gurugram** using Google Maps. - The **map** of India is your **workspace**. - The **cities and junctions** are the **nodes (vertices)** of the graph. - The **roads** connecting them are the **edges**. - **Traffic, tolls, and distance** represent the **edge weights (costs)**. - **Flooded bridges and road closures** are the **dynamic obstacles** that require replanning. A *graph search algorithm* is exactly what runs inside that "route engine" — it explores cities one by one (or smartly, using heuristics) to find you the fastest route. That is path planning in essence. ## 1.2 Why Do Robots Need Path Planning? | Application | Why Path Planning? | |---|---| | Autonomous Cars (e.g., Waymo) | Avoid collisions with vehicles, pedestrians | | Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva) | Navigate narrow aisles efficiently | | Drones (UAVs) | Fly through 3D urban canyons without hitting buildings | | Surgical Robots | Move precisely inside a human body | | Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance) | Avoid rocks, craters, sand traps | ## 1.3 The Family of Path Planning Algorithms The algorithms covered in this module sit on a **spectrum of intelligence**: > [!NOTE] > **Uninformed (Blind) Search** uses only the graph structure. > **Informed (Heuristic) Search** uses a heuristic function $h(n)$ to *guess* how close a node is to the goal. $$ \text{Intelligence Axis: } \underbrace{\text{BFS} \rightarrow \text{DFS}}_{\text{Uninformed}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Dijkstra}}_{\text{Weighted}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{A*}}_{\text{Heuristic}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{D*}}_{\text{Dynamic}} $$ ## 1.4 The Configuration Space (C-space) Concept The **C-space** $\mathcal{C}$ is the space of all possible robot configurations. For a 2D point robot, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$. For a car, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (x, y, orientation). $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \text{robot at } q \text{ does not collide with any obstacle} \} $$ A valid path is a continuous map $\tau : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$. # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Core Components of a Graph Search Every algorithm discussed in this module shares a common skeleton: 1. **Open List (Frontier):** Nodes discovered but not yet explored. 2. **Closed List (Explored):** Nodes already fully processed. 3. **Cost Function $g(n)$:** Actual cost from start to node $n$. 4. **Heuristic Function $h(n)$:** Estimated cost from $n$ to goal. 5. **Evaluation Function $f(n)$:** Priority score used to pick the next node. $$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$ This single equation is the **backbone of A\*** and is the most-tested formula in KTU exams. ## 2.2 Algorithm 1: Breadth First Search (BFS) ### Core Idea Explore the graph in **layers** outward from the start node, like ripples on water. ### Working Principle - Uses a **FIFO Queue** (First-In-First-Out). - All nodes at depth $d$ are expanded **before** any node at depth $d+1$. - Guarantees the **shortest path in terms of number of edges** for unweighted graphs. ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. Enqueue the start node $s$; mark it visited. 2. While the queue is not empty: - Dequeue node $n$. - If $n$ is the goal, **reconstruct and return** the path. - Enqueue all unvisited neighbors of $n$ and mark them visited. 3. If the queue empties without finding the goal, return **failure**. > [!NOTE] > **BFS is complete** (finds a path if one exists) and **optimal for unweighted graphs**, but uses memory $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ which is prohibitive for large robot workspaces. ## 2.3 Algorithm 2: Depth First Search (DFS) ### Core Idea Go as **deep as possible** down one branch before backtracking. ### Working Principle - Uses a **LIFO Stack** (Last-In-First-Out) or recursion. - Does **NOT** guarantee the shortest path. - Very **memory-efficient** for deep, narrow graphs. ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. Push start node $s$ onto the stack. 2. While the stack is not empty: - Pop node $n$. - If $n$ is the goal, **return** the path. - Push all unvisited neighbors of $n$ onto the stack. 3. If stack empties, return **failure**. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU often asks: *"Why is DFS unsuitable for shortest path planning in robotics?"* Answer: Because DFS commits to one branch and may return a very long, winding path even when a short one exists. ## 2.4 Algorithm 3: Dijkstra's Algorithm ### Core Idea The **weighted version of BFS** — explores in order of cumulative path cost $g(n)$. ### Working Principle - Uses a **Priority Queue (Min-Heap)** keyed on $g(n)$. - Guarantees the **shortest path in weighted graphs with non-negative weights**. - Invented by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1956. ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. Set $g(s) = 0$ and $g(n) = \infty$ for all other nodes. 2. Insert all nodes into the priority queue. 3. While the queue is not empty: - Extract node $n$ with the **minimum $g(n)$**. - If $n$ is the goal, **reconstruct and return** the path. - For each neighbor $m$ of $n$: - Compute $g_{\text{new}} = g(n) + w(n, m)$. - If $g_{\text{new}} < g(m)$: update $g(m) = g_{\text{new}}$ and set parent. > [!NOTE] > Dijkstra is **uniform-cost search**: $f(n) = g(n)$, i.e., it has **no heuristic guidance**, so it expands in all directions equally — wasteful when the goal is far in one direction. ## 2.5 Algorithm 4: A* Search ### Core Idea **Dijkstra + Heuristic = A\*.** It uses $h(n)$ to bias the search toward the goal. ### The Master Evaluation Function $$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$ Where: - $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$ (known) - $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to goal (heuristic) - $f(n)$ = total estimated cost of the cheapest path through $n$ ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. Initialize $g(s) = 0$, $f(s) = h(s)$, and an OPEN list containing $s$. 2. While OPEN is not empty: - Pick node $n$ with the **lowest $f(n)$** from OPEN. - Move $n$ to CLOSED. - If $n$ is the goal, **reconstruct the path** and return. - For each neighbor $m$ of $n$: - Compute $g_{\text{tentative}} = g(n) + w(n, m)$. - If $m$ is in CLOSED and $g_{\text{tentative}} \geq g(m)$: **skip**. - If $m$ is not in OPEN or $g_{\text{tentative}} < g(m)$: - Update $g(m) = g_{\text{tentative}}$, $f(m) = g(m) + h(m)$. - Set parent of $m$ to $n$. - Insert/update $m$ in OPEN. ### Admissibility and Consistency | Property | Definition | Consequence | |---|---|---| | **Admissible Heuristic** | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ where $h^*$ is the true cost to goal | A* is **optimal** | | **Consistent Heuristic** | $h(n) \leq w(n,m) + h(m)$ for all neighbors $m$ | A* never re-expands nodes | | **Dominance** | $h_1(n) \geq h_2(n)$ for all $n$ and both admissible | $h_1$ expands **fewer nodes** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For 2D grids with 4-connectivity**, the **Manhattan distance** is admissible: > $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{\text{goal}} \vert$ > **For 8-connectivity**, use **Diagonal (Chebyshev) distance**: > $h(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_{\text{goal}} \vert, \vert y_n - y_{\text{goal}} \vert)$ > **For Euclidean graphs**, use the **straight-line distance**: > $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{\text{goal}})^2 + (y_n - y_{\text{goal}})^2}$ ## 2.6 Algorithm 5: D* (Dynamic A*) — Focused D* ### Why D* Exists A* assumes a **static environment**. But real robots encounter **moving obstacles** and **map changes**. Replanning A* from scratch every time is computationally expensive. ### Core Idea D* plans an initial path using A*, then **incrementally repairs** the path when the environment changes, working **backward from the goal**. ### Key Differences from A* | Feature | A* | D* | |---|---|---| | Search direction | Forward (start → goal) | Backward (goal → start) | | Cost updates | None (static) | **Incremental** after map change | | Replanning | Re-run from scratch | Reuse previous search tree | | Heuristic | $h(n)$ from $n$ to goal | $h(n)$ from $n$ to start | ### D* Main Loop 1. Run a backward A* from goal to start to obtain an initial path. 2. Robot follows the path step-by-step. 3. If an edge cost changes (e.g., new obstacle detected): - Propagate the cost change through the affected subgraph. - Mark affected nodes as **RAISE** or **LOWER** states. - Re-run the modified search **only locally** around the change. 4. Resume motion along the new optimal path. > [!NOTE] > D* is the foundation of the **NASA Mars Rover** path planning system and many autonomous vehicle stacks. Its variants — **D\* Lite** and **Field D\*** — are used in modern robotics. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table for the 14-mark derivation questions. | Algorithm | Evaluation Function $f(n)$ | Data Structure | Optimality | Completeness | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | BFS | $f(n) = \text{depth}(n)$ | FIFO Queue | Yes (unweighted) | Yes | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | | DFS | $f(n) = -\text{depth}(n)$ | LIFO Stack | No | Yes (finite) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | | Dijkstra | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Min-Priority Queue | Yes (non-negative) | Yes | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | | A* | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Min-Priority Queue | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | Yes | Depends on $h$ quality | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | | D* | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ (backward) | Min-Priority Queue | Yes (dynamic) | Yes | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ amortized | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | ## 2.8 Heuristic Formulas Reference $$ h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert $$ $$ h_{\text{Euclidean}}(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2} $$ $$ h_{\text{Chebyshev}}(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_g \vert, \vert y_n - y_g \vert) $$ $$ h_{\text{Diagonal}}(n) = \sqrt{2} \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + (\max(\Delta x, \Delta y) - \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)) $$ Where $\Delta x = \vert x_n - x_g \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert y_n - y_g \vert$. ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **A\*** powers the path planners in **ROS (Robot Operating System)** via the `navfn` and `global_planner` packages. - **D\* Lite** is used in **Carnegie Mellon's Tartan Racing** autonomous vehicle (Boss, the DARPA Urban Challenge winner). - **DFS/BFS** form the basis of **SLAM graph optimization** in the back-end. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: BFS on a Robot Workspace Graph Consider the graph below where $S$ is the start and $G$ is the goal. All edges have weight 1 (unweighted). $$ S \to A, \quad S \to B, \quad A \to C, \quad A \to D, \quad B \to D, \quad C \to G, \quad D \to G $$ ### BFS Trace Table | Step | Action | Queue (front → back) | Visited | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | Initialize | $[S]$ | $\{S\}$ | | 1 | Dequeue $S$, enqueue $A, B$ | $[A, B]$ | $\{S, A, B\}$ | | 2 | Dequeue $A$, enqueue $C, D$ | $[B, C, D]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D\}$ | | 3 | Dequeue $B$ — neighbors already visited | $[C, D]$ | unchanged | | 4 | Dequeue $C$, enqueue $G$ | $[D, G]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$ | | 5 | Dequeue $D$ — $G$ already in queue | $[G]$ | unchanged | | 6 | Dequeue $G$ — **GOAL FOUND** | $[]$ | — | **Reconstructed Path:** $S \to A \to C \to G$ (length 3, optimal) ## 3.2 Worked Example: Dijkstra on a Weighted Graph Graph edges and weights (assume symmetric): $$ w(S, A) = 1, \quad w(S, B) = 4, \quad w(A, C) = 2, \quad w(A, D) = 5, \quad w(B, D) = 1, \quad w(C, G) = 3, \quad w(D, G) = 2 $$ ### Dijkstra Trace | Step | Pop (min $g$) | Updates Made | $g(S)$ | $g(A)$ | $g(B)$ | $g(C)$ | $g(D)$ | $g(G)$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | Initialize | $0$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 1 | $S$ (0) | $g(A)=1$, $g(B)=4$ | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | 2 | $A$ (1) | $g(C)=1+2=3$, $g(D)=1+5=6$ | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ | $3$ | $6$ | $\infty$ | | 3 | $C$ (3) | $g(G)=3+3=6$ | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ | $3$ | $6$ | $6$ | | 4 | $B$ (4) | $g(D)=\min(6, 4+1)=5$ | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ | $3$ | $5$ | $6$ | | 5 | $D$ (5) | $g(G)=\min(6, 5+2)=6$ (no change) | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ | $3$ | $5$ | $6$ | | 6 | $G$ (6) | **GOAL — RECONSTRUCT** | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ | $3$ | $5$ | $6$ | **Optimal Path:** $S \to A \to C \to G$ with total cost $g(G) = 6$. > [!NOTE] > Notice Dijkstra expanded **every node** including $B$ and $D$, even though the goal was reachable via $A \to C$. This is its main weakness — wasted exploration. ## 3.3 Worked Example: A* on the Same Graph Heuristic: $h(n)$ = Manhattan distance to $G$ (using a unit grid layout, treating each node as $(x, y)$). Assume the node coordinates: - $S = (0, 0)$, $A = (1, 0)$, $B = (0, 1)$, $C = (2, 0)$, $D = (1, 1)$, $G = (3, 0)$. Manhattan heuristic values: - $h(S) = 3$, $h(A) = 2$, $h(B) = 3$, $h(C) = 1$, $h(D) = 2$, $h(G) = 0$. ### A* Trace | Step | OPEN (sorted by $f$) | Pop $n$ | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | Updates | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | $\{S\}$ | $S$ | $0$ | $3$ | $3$ | Add $A$($f$=3), $B$($f$=7) | | 1 | $\{A, B\}$ | $A$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | Add $C$($f$=4), $D$($f$=8) | | 2 | $\{C, B, D\}$ | $C$ | $3$ | $1$ | $4$ | Add $G$($f$=6) | | 3 | $\{G, B, D\}$ | $G$ | $6$ | $0$ | $6$ | **GOAL — RETURN PATH** | **Optimal Path:** $S \to A \to C \to G$, cost = 6. > [!IMPORTANT] > A* **stopped as soon as the goal was popped from OPEN**. It did **not** need to process $B$ or $D$. This is the **power of the heuristic** — it prunes unpromising branches. ## 3.4 Worked Example: DFS on the Same Graph (for contrast) DFS using a stack, exploring in alphabetical order: | Step | Action | Stack | Path | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | Push $S$ | $[S]$ | $S$ | | 1 | Pop $S$, push $A, B$ | $[A, B]$ | $S$ | | 2 | Pop $B$, push $D$ | $[A, D]$ | $S \to B$ | | 3 | Pop $D$, push $G$ | $[A, G]$ | $S \to B \to D$ | | 4 | Pop $G$ — **GOAL** | $[A]$ | $S \to B \to D \to G$ | **Path found:** $S \to B \to D \to G$, cost $= 4 + 1 + 2 = 7$. **DFS returned a sub-optimal path** (cost 7 vs. Dijkstra's 6), demonstrating its unsuitability for optimal planning. ## 3.5 Admissibility Proof Sketch (A* Optimality) > This is a high-yield KTU question. **Claim:** If $h(n)$ is admissible, A* returns an optimal path. **Proof Sketch:** Let $C^*$ be the cost of the optimal path. Let $n$ be the **first suboptimal goal** popped from OPEN. Then: $$ f(n) = g(n) > C^* $$ Since $h$ is admissible, $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Consider any node $m$ on the optimal path not yet expanded: $$ f(m) = g(m) + h(m) \leq C^* + 0 = C^* $$ The last inequality holds because for the goal $G^*$, $g(G^*) = C^*$ and $h(G^*) = 0$. Since OPEN is a min-priority queue, $m$ would be popped **before** $n$ (because $f(m) \leq C^* < f(n)$). This contradicts the assumption that $n$ was the first suboptimal goal popped. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.6 D* Cost Propagation: The RAISE/LOWER Concept When the robot moves and detects a change, affected edges enter one of two states: $$ \text{RAISE}(n): \quad g(n) > \text{old\_g}(n) \quad \text{(edge became more expensive)} $$ $$ \text{LOWER}(n): \quad g(n) < \text{old\_g}(n) \quad \text{(edge became cheaper)} $$ For a RAISE state, D* checks if any **under-consistent** neighbor can lower the cost: $$ \min_{m \in \text{neighbors}(n)} \left( g(m) + w(n, m) \right) < g(n) $$ If yes, $g(n)$ is updated to that minimum, and the inconsistency propagates. > [!NOTE] > KTU often tests this with a small example: "The robot is at $S$, the path is $S \to A \to G$, but suddenly the edge $A \to G$ weight increases from 2 to 10. Recompute the D* path." ## 3.7 Master Comparison Matrix (Analytical) | Criterion | BFS | DFS | Dijkstra | A* | D* | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Uses heuristic? | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Handles edge weights? | ❌ (assumes 1) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Optimal? | ✅ (unweighted) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ (admissible $h$) | ✅ (dynamic) | | Complete? | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Handles dynamic env? | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Memory efficient? | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (initially) | | Best for robotics? | Small grids | Maze puzzles | Weighted grids | Static nav | Live robots | # 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Graph Search Algorithm Family Tree ```mermaid graph TD A[Graph Search Algorithms] --> B[Uninformed Blind Search] A --> C[Informed Heuristic Search] B --> D[BFS - Breadth First Search] B --> E[DFS - Depth First Search] C --> F[Dijkstra - Weighted Uniform Cost] C --> G[A Star - Heuristic Guided] C --> H[D Star - Dynamic Replanning] G --> I[D Star Lite - Modern Variant] H --> I D --> J[Uses FIFO Queue] E --> K[Uses LIFO Stack] F --> L[Uses Min Priority Queue] G --> L H --> L I --> L ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: A* Algorithm Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start]) --> init[Initialize OPEN with start node s
g s equals 0
f s equals h s] init --> check{Is OPEN empty?} check -- Yes --> fail[Return FAILURE No path exists] check -- No --> pop[Pop node n with minimum f n from OPEN] pop --> goal{Is n the goal node G?} goal -- Yes --> success[Reconstruct path via parent pointers
Return SUCCESS] goal -- No --> expand[For each neighbor m of n] expand --> cost[Compute g tentative equals g n plus w n m] cost --> skip{m in CLOSED and g tentative not less than g m?} skip -- Yes --> next[Skip to next neighbor] skip -- No --> update[Set g m equals g tentative
Set f m equals g m plus h m
Set parent m equals n
Add or update m in OPEN] next --> more{More neighbors?} update --> more more -- Yes --> expand more -- No --> check fail --> stop1([End]) success --> stop2([End]) ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: D* Incremental Replanning Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Robot at Start S] --> B[Run backward A star from Goal G to S] B --> C[Execute first action of path] C --> D{Edge cost changed by sensor update?} D -- No --> E[Move to next node on path] E --> C D -- Yes --> F[Mark affected nodes as RAISE or LOWER] F --> G[Propagate cost changes in priority queue] G --> H{Is path still consistent?} H -- No --> I[Locally re-plan affected segment] I --> C H -- Yes --> C C --> J{Reached Goal G?} J -- No --> C J -- Yes --> K[Mission Complete] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Sample Graph for Worked Example ```mermaid graph LR S((S start)) -- 1 --> A((A)) S -- 4 --> B((B)) A -- 2 --> C((C)) A -- 5 --> D((D)) B -- 1 --> D C -- 3 --> G((G goal)) D -- 2 --> G style S fill:#90EE90 style G fill:#FFB6C1 style A fill:#FFFFE0 style B fill:#FFFFE0 style C fill:#FFFFE0 style D fill:#FFFFE0 ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Algorithm Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the environment dynamic?} Q1 -- No --> Q2{Are edge weights uniform?} Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Use D Star or D Star Lite] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Need shortest in number of steps?} Q2 -- No --> Q4{Need optimal weighted path?} Q3 -- Yes --> R2[Use BFS] Q3 -- No --> R3[Use DFS - for exploration only] Q4 -- No --> R4[Use Dijkstra] Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Is a good heuristic available?} Q5 -- Yes --> R5[Use A Star with admissible h] Q5 -- No --> R4 ``` ## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of an A*-Based Robot Navigator ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Perception P1[LiDAR Scanner] --> P2[Occupancy Grid Map Builder] P3[IMU and Odometry] --> P4[Localization Module] end subgraph Planning P2 --> G[Global Planner - A Star] P4 --> G G --> PATH[Optimal Path Waypoints] PATH --> LOC[Local Planner - DWA or MPC] end subgraph Control LOC --> CMD[Velocity Commands v and omega] CMD --> M[Motor Drivers] M --> W[Robot Wheels] end W --> P1 W --> P3 ``` # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Differentiate between informed and uninformed search. Give one example of each in the context of robot path planning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Uninformed (Blind) Search** explores the workspace graph using only the topology and edge costs, with no knowledge of how close a node is to the goal. The search proceeds uniformly outward. **Examples:** Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS). **Informed (Heuristic) Search** uses a heuristic function $h(n)$ that estimates the distance from any node $n$ to the goal, biasing the search toward promising regions. **Examples:** A* Search and D* Search. In robotics, BFS works on uniform grid maps with no terrain cost variation, while A* is the de-facto choice for weighted navigation with known goal location. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example + context)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **State the A* evaluation function. What is an admissible heuristic? Why does admissibility guarantee optimality of A\*?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The A* evaluation function is: $$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$ where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from start to node $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to the goal. A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if it **never overestimates** the true cost to the goal, i.e., $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Admissibility guarantees optimality because the algorithm will never pop a suboptimal goal from OPEN before exploring every node on the optimal path. Since $h(G) = 0$ for the true goal, $f(G) = g(G) = C^*$, and any suboptimal node would have $f(n) > C^*$, so it cannot be popped first. *(3 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark admissibility, 1 mark optimality reasoning)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Question A and Question B are completely independent alternatives. Solve any one. ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following graph with start $S$ and goal $G$. Show the priority queue at each step and write the final shortest path with its total cost.** Graph (edges and weights, undirected): - $S-A = 2$, $S-B = 5$, $A-C = 1$, $A-D = 4$, $B-D = 2$, $C-G = 3$, $D-G = 6$ **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** | Step | Extract Min | $g(S)$ | $g(A)$ | $g(B)$ | $g(C)$ | $g(D)$ | $g(G)$ | Comments | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Initialize | | 1 | $S$ (0) | 0 | 2 | 5 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Update $A$, $B$ | | 2 | $A$ (2) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 6 | $\infty$ | Update $C$, $D$ | | 3 | $C$ (3) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 6 | 6 | Update $G$ via $C$ | | 4 | $B$ (5) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 6 | $g(D)=\min(6,5+2)=4$ | | 5 | $D$ (4) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 6 | $g(G)$ stays at 6 (since $4+6=10 > 6$) | | 6 | $G$ (6) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 6 | **GOAL REACHED** | **Shortest Path:** $S \to A \to C \to G$ **Total Cost:** $g(G) = 2 + 1 + 3 = 6$ units. *(7 marks: 1 mark setup, 4 marks trace table, 1 mark path reconstruction, 1 mark total cost)* #### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **Explain A* algorithm with its evaluation function. Apply A* on the same graph (above) using Manhattan distance as heuristic, with goal at $G$ and grid coordinates: $S(0,0), A(1,0), B(0,1), C(2,0), D(1,1), G(3,0)$. Show the OPEN list trace and final path.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Heuristic Computation (Manhattan):** $$ h(S) = \vert 0-3 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 3 $$ $$ h(A) = \vert 1-3 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 2 $$ $$ h(B) = \vert 0-3 \vert + \vert 1-0 \vert = 3 $$ $$ h(C) = \vert 2-3 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 1 $$ $$ h(D) = \vert 1-3 \vert + \vert 1-0 \vert = 2 $$ $$ h(G) = \vert 3-3 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 0 $$ **A* OPEN List Trace:** | Step | OPEN (sorted by $f$) | Pop | $g$ | $h$ | $f$ | Updates | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | $\{S(3)\}$ | $S$ | 0 | 3 | 3 | Add $A(f=4)$, $B(f=8)$ | | 1 | $\{A(4), B(8)\}$ | $A$ | 2 | 2 | 4 | Add $C(f=4)$, $D(f=10)$ | | 2 | $\{C(4), B(8), D(10)\}$ | $C$ | 3 | 1 | 4 | Add $G(f=6)$ | | 3 | $\{G(6), B(8), D(10)\}$ | $G$ | 6 | 0 | 6 | **GOAL FOUND** | **Final Path:** $S \to A \to C \to G$, total cost = 6. **Discussion:** A* only expanded $S$, $A$, $C$ — **3 nodes** — whereas Dijkstra expanded 6 nodes. This is the **node-pruning power** of a good heuristic. *(7 marks: 2 marks algorithm explanation, 3 marks heuristic computation, 2 marks trace and conclusion)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Explain D\* algorithm. How does it differ from A\* in handling dynamic environments? Illustrate with a scenario.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **D\* (Dynamic A*) Algorithm:** D* is an extension of A* designed for environments where edge costs change after the initial plan is made. Unlike A*, it: 1. **Searches backward** from the goal to the start. 2. **Reuses the previous search tree** after map updates. 3. **Propagates cost changes locally** rather than re-planning from scratch. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Compute initial path using backward A* (from goal $G$ to start $S$). 2. Robot begins executing the path. 3. On sensing a map change (e.g., new obstacle): - Update the affected edge cost in the graph. - If cost increased: mark node as **RAISE**, push to OPEN. - If cost decreased: mark node as **LOWER**, push to OPEN. 4. Process OPEN in priority order of $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ (with $h$ from $n$ to $S$). 5. For each node, check **under-consistency**: - $g(n) > \min_{m \in \text{neighbors}} [g(m) + w(n, m)]$ - If true, lower $g(n)$ to this minimum (LOWER state). - Else, check over-consistency and RAISE accordingly. 6. Continue until OPEN is empty or goal is consistent. 7. Resume robot motion along the updated path. **Scenario Illustration:** Consider a robot navigating from $S$ to $G$ in a warehouse. The initial A* plan is $S \to A \to B \to G$ with total cost 10. Suddenly, a forklift blocks edge $A \to B$, increasing its weight from 3 to 100. - **A* approach:** Re-run A* from $S$ to $G$ — explores the entire workspace again — slow. - **D* approach:** Only the affected nodes ($A$, $B$, $G$) are re-evaluated. A new path $S \to A \to C \to G$ with cost 8 is computed in $O(k \log k)$ time where $k$ is the number of affected nodes (small). **Key Differences Table:** | Aspect | A* | D* | |---|---|---| | Search Direction | Forward (start → goal) | Backward (goal → start) | | Environment | Static | Dynamic | | Replanning | From scratch | Incremental | | Replanning Cost | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(k \log k)$, $k \ll \vert V \vert$ | | Sensor Integration | Pre-planning only | During execution | *(7 marks: 3 marks algorithm steps, 2 marks differences table, 2 marks scenario)* #### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Compare BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, A*, and D\* in terms of (i) use of heuristic, (ii) optimality, (iii) handling of weighted edges, (iv) handling of dynamic environments, and (v) typical robotics application.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** | Algorithm | (i) Heuristic | (ii) Optimality | (iii) Weighted Edges | (iv) Dynamic Env | (v) Robotics Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **BFS** | No | Yes (unweighted only) | No (assumes uniform) | No | Discrete grid mazes | | **DFS** | No | No | No | No | Maze exploration, topological sort | | **Dijkstra** | No | Yes (non-negative weights) | Yes | No | Shortest route on road networks | | **A*** | Yes ($h(n)$) | Yes (admissible $h$) | Yes | No | Static warehouse navigation, ROS global planner | | **D*** | Yes (backward $h$) | Yes (dynamic) | Yes | Yes (incremental) | Autonomous cars, Mars rovers, mobile robots | **Conclusion:** For **static navigation with known costs**, A* is the gold standard. For **dynamic, real-world robot navigation** with sensor updates, D* (or D* Lite) is preferred because it reuses prior computations. BFS and DFS are mostly used in **discrete puzzle-type problems**, while Dijkstra dominates **static route planning on road networks** where $h(n)$ is not easily available. *(7 marks: 1 mark per row of comparison)* --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the heuristic definition:** Many students write $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ but forget to explicitly state that $g$ is the **actual** cost and $h$ is the **estimated** cost. This loses 1 mark. > 2. **Wrong heuristic for wrong grid type:** Using Manhattan distance on an 8-connected grid is technically still admissible but **not consistent** — and inconsistent heuristics require extra care. Examiners will deduct marks for not matching heuristic to grid connectivity. > 3. **Skipping the priority queue column:** In Dijkstra/A* trace tables, omitting the OPEN list as a sorted priority queue costs 1–2 marks. Always show what is currently in OPEN and in what order. > 4. **Confusing admissibility with consistency:** Admissibility guarantees optimality. Consistency (monotonicity) guarantees no re-expansions. Mixing these up costs full marks in 14-mark questions. > 5. **Not explaining D* direction reversal:** D* searches **backward from goal to start**, not forward like A*. This is a frequently tested point worth 2–3 marks. > 6. **Missing path reconstruction:** Final answers must show the **actual sequence of nodes** from $S$ to $G$, not just the total cost. Examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for the path. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **BFS** = queue, level-order, shortest in unweighted graphs. - ✅ **DFS** = stack, deep-first, NOT optimal, used for exploration. - ✅ **Dijkstra** = min-priority queue, $f(n) = g(n)$, optimal for non-negative weights. - ✅ **A*** = adds heuristic: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, optimal if $h$ is admissible. - ✅ **D*** = backward dynamic A*, replans incrementally when edge costs change. - ✅ **Manhattan heuristic** for 4-connected grids, **Euclidean** for 2D, **Chebyshev** for 8-connected. - ✅ **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates. - ✅ **Consistent heuristic:** $h(n) \leq w(n,m) + h(m)$ — triangle inequality. - ✅ **A\* time complexity** is bounded by the number of nodes where $f(n) < C^*$, depending on heuristic quality. - ✅ **D\* efficiency** comes from incremental updates — only affected nodes are reprocessed. - ✅ In **KTU 2024 exams**, expect 3-mark theory questions and 14-mark numerical traces. - ✅ Always show **priority queue / OPEN list state** in numerical solutions. - ✅ **D* is the basis of D* Lite**, the most commonly implemented variant in modern robotics (used in ROS `dmcore` and many autonomous vehicle stacks).

topics.title: Potential Field Based Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential Field Based Path Planning**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Potential Field Based Path Planning — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Khatib, 1986 — Foundational KTU Reference):**
> *Potential Field Based Path Planning* is a **reactive, local path planning strategy** in which the robot's configuration space $C$ is mapped with a **scalar artificial potential function** $U(q)$. The goal configuration $q_{goal}$ generates an **attractive potential** that pulls the robot toward the target, while obstacles generate **repulsive potentials** that push the robot away. The robot navigates by descending the **negative gradient** of the total potential field, i.e., by following the resultant **artificial force** $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$.

Formally, the total potential is the linear superposition of two fields:

$$U_{total}(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

The robot's control law is given by the gradient descent rule:

$$\dot{q} = -\nabla U_{total}(q) = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q)$$

The planner is considered complete when the robot reaches a configuration $q^*$ such that $U_{total}(q^*) \leq \epsilon$ within a neighborhood of $q_{goal}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rolling Marble on a Trampoline" Analogy**
> Imagine a large stretched rubber sheet (the **configuration space**). At the target location, you push a heavy ball **downward** creating a deep funnel — this is the **attractive potential** (gravity-like pull). At every obstacle, you push a rigid **cone upward**, creating a sharp hill — this is the **repulsive potential** (push away). If you now place a small marble (the **robot**) anywhere on the sheet, it will naturally roll downhill, steered by gravity, avoiding the obstacle hills and ultimately falling into the goal funnel.
> 
> - **The slope of the sheet = artificial force on the robot.**
> - **The valleys = safe free-space paths.**
> - **The peaks = forbidden obstacle regions.**
> - **Stuck points = local minima** (where the marble settles in a small valley that is *not* the goal — a critical failure mode).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Symbol | Standard KTU Nomenclature | Typical Range (Engineering Practice) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\xi$ (xi) | **Attractive potential gain** | $0.5$ to $5.0$ (dimensionless or N/m) |
| $\eta$ (eta) | **Repulsive potential gain** | $0.5$ to $10.0$ (dimensionless or N·m²) |
| $\rho_0$ | **Distance of influence (threshold)** | $0.5\,\text{m}$ to $5.0\,\text{m}$ |
| $q$ | **Robot configuration vector** | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $q_{goal}$ | **Goal configuration** | $\in C_{free}$ |
| $q_{obs}$ | **Closest point on obstacle** | $\in C_{obs}$ |
| $d_{min}$ | **Minimum allowed clearance** | $> 0$ (safety margin) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** The two critical engineering design parameters are $\xi$ (controls goal-seeking aggressiveness) and $\eta$ (controls obstacle-avoidance aggressiveness). The ratio $\eta/\xi$ determines whether the robot "fears obstacles more than it desires the goal" — a frequent exam and design question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Structured Logic Breakdown

The Potential Field Method (PFM) operates in a four-stage analytical loop:

- **Step 1 — Configuration Space Mapping:** The 2D/3D workspace $\mathcal{W}$ is sampled into discrete configurations. Each configuration $q \in C$ is assigned a scalar potential value.
- **Step 2 — Superposition of Fields:** The attractive field from the goal and repulsive fields from all obstacles are linearly summed to construct $U_{total}(q)$. The principle of linear superposition is valid because potentials are scalar energy-like quantities.
- **Step 3 — Force Computation via Gradient:** The artificial force at $q$ is $F(q) = -\nabla U_{total}(q)$. In 2D, $\nabla = \left[\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x},\; \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\right]^T$.
- **Step 4 — Motion Update (Integration):** The robot updates its configuration by integrating the force over a small time-step $\Delta t$: $q_{k+1} = q_k + F(q_k)\cdot \Delta t$. The loop continues until $q$ converges to $q_{goal}$.

### 2.1.1 Why the Negative Gradient?
- The gradient $\nabla U$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of potential energy.
- A physical system naturally seeks the **minimum energy state**.
- Therefore, the **force of descent** is $-\nabla U$, which physically corresponds to a generalized force derived from a conservative potential.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Mathematical Form

- **Attractive Field ($U_{att}$)** is a function of distance $\rho_{g} = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$.
- **Repulsive Field ($U_{rep}$)** is a function of distance to the closest obstacle $\rho_{o} = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert$.
- The "closest point on obstacle" computation is performed using geometric methods (e.g., Minkowski subtraction for polygonal obstacles).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Quick-Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation a KTU student is expected to know** for ESE and module tests. Note: vertical bars are written using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Conditions / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Conic Attractive Potential | $U_{att}(q) = \xi \cdot \rho_{g}(q, q_{goal})$ | $\rho_{g} \leq d^*_{goal}$ (far-field, linear force) |
| 2 | Parabolic Attractive Potential | $U_{att}(q) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\, \rho_{g}^{2}(q, q_{goal})$ | $\rho_{g} > d^*_{goal}$ (near-field, constant force magnitude $\xi$) |
| 3 | Composite Attractive Potential | $U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\, \rho_{g}^{2}, & \rho_{g} \leq d^*_{goal} \\ \xi\, d^*_{goal}\,\rho_{g} - \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\, d^{*2}_{goal}, & \rho_{g} > d^*_{goal} \end{cases}$ | Smooth $C^1$ field for entire workspace |
| 4 | FIRAS Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_o} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho_o \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho_o > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | $\rho_0$ = distance of influence; KTU standard form |
| 5 | Attractive Force | $F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$ | Parabolic case, constant magnitude |
| 6 | Repulsive Force | $F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_o} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{1}{\rho_o^{2}}\,\hat{e}_{ro}$ | $\hat{e}_{ro}$ = unit vector from obstacle to robot |
| 7 | Total Artificial Force | $F_{total}(q) = F_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{rep,i}(q)$ | Sum over all $N$ obstacles in influence range |
| 8 | Robot Motion Update (Discrete) | $q_{k+1} = q_k + F_{total}(q_k)\cdot \Delta t$ | Euler integration; $\Delta t$ = simulation step |
| 9 | Goal Convergence Condition | $\Vert q_k - q_{goal} \Vert \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = small tolerance (e.g., $0.05\,\text{m}$) |
| 10 | Local Minima Condition | $\nabla U_{total}(q_{lm}) = 0,\; q_{lm} \neq q_{goal}$ | Triggers planning failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Formula #4 (FIRAS repulsive potential) is named after Faverjon, *Tournassoud*, and the standard reference **Khatib (1986)**. The notation $\dfrac{1}{\rho_o^{2}}$ in formula #6 is correct — the gradient of $\rho_o^{-1}$ produces the $\rho_o^{-2}$ factor. Many students mistakenly write $\rho_o^{-1}$ here and lose marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Manipulators:** Used in real-time obstacle avoidance for robotic arms in cluttered cells (e.g., bin-picking, welding cells).
- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva, for example) use potential-field variants as part of layered motion planners.
- **Drone Swarms:** Local collision avoidance in multi-UAV systems — each drone acts as a moving obstacle repelling neighbors.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Real-time path adjustment in confined anatomical spaces where global replanning is computationally infeasible.
- **Service Robotics:** Mobile robots navigating in human-populated environments (museums, hospitals).

## 2.4 Advantages and Limitations (Frequently Asked)

| Aspect | Strength | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Computation | $O(N)$ per step, very fast | Local minima traps |
| Implementation | Mathematically elegant, easy to code | No global optimality guarantee |
| Sensors | Reactive — works with partial sensor data | Oscillations in narrow passages |
| Dynamics | Smooth, continuous control signals | Cannot handle non-holonomic constraints directly |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Attractive Force from Parabolic Potential

We start from the **parabolic attractive potential** (formula #2 in the cheat sheet):

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\, \rho_{g}^{2}(q, q_{goal})$$

**Step A — Express the distance function.** The squared Euclidean distance from the current configuration to the goal is:

$$\rho_{g}^{2}(q, q_{goal}) = (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step B — Substitute back into the potential expression.**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\,(q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step C — Compute the gradient with respect to $q$.** Applying the standard vector calculus identity $\nabla_q (x^T x) = 2x$:

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi \cdot 2(q - q_{goal}) = \xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step D — Apply the negative gradient to obtain the attractive force.**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step E — Magnitude of the attractive force.** Take the Euclidean norm:

$$\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \xi \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert = \xi \cdot \rho_{g}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the reason the **parabolic form gives a force whose magnitude grows linearly with distance to the goal** — far from the goal, the robot is pulled strongly; near the goal, the pull weakens. This is desirable because it allows smooth deceleration.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Conic Attractive Potential and Its Linear Force

The **conic (conical) attractive potential** is:

$$U_{att}(q) = \xi \cdot \rho_{g}(q, q_{goal})$$

**Step A — Substitute the distance.**

$$U_{att}(q) = \xi \cdot \sqrt{(q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})}$$

**Step B — Differentiate using the chain rule.** Recall $\nabla \sqrt{f(q)} = \dfrac{\nabla f(q)}{2\sqrt{f(q)}}$:

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \xi \cdot \frac{2(q - q_{goal})}{2\sqrt{(q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})}} = \xi \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{g}}$$

**Step C — Compute the force (negative gradient).**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\xi \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{g}} = -\xi \cdot \hat{e}_{qg}$$

where $\hat{e}_{qg}$ is the **unit vector pointing from the goal toward the robot** (i.e., direction of attractive pull).

**Step D — Force magnitude.**

$$\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \xi \quad \text{(constant)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The conic form gives a **constant force magnitude everywhere** — useful for distant goal-pulling but causes the robot to "slam" into the goal. This is why the **composite (Khatib-blended) form** in formula #3 of the cheat sheet is preferred: it is conic far from the goal (strong, uniform pull) and parabolic near the goal (smooth, decelerating pull).

## 3.3 Derivation III — Repulsive Force (FIRAS Form)

Start with the FIRAS repulsive potential:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho_o} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, \quad \rho_o \leq \rho_0$$

Let $u = \dfrac{1}{\rho_o} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\, u^{2}$.

**Step A — Chain rule expansion.**

$$\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\, u \cdot \nabla u$$

**Step B — Compute $\nabla u$.**

$$u = \rho_o^{-1} - \rho_0^{-1} \;\Rightarrow\; \nabla u = \nabla(\rho_o^{-1}) = -\rho_o^{-2} \cdot \nabla \rho_o$$

(Recall: $\dfrac{d}{d\rho_o}\rho_o^{-1} = -\rho_o^{-2}$.)

**Step C — Compute $\nabla \rho_o$.** Since $\rho_o = \Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert$:

$$\nabla \rho_o = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\Vert q - q_{obs} \Vert} = \hat{e}_{ro}$$

where $\hat{e}_{ro}$ is the **unit vector from obstacle to robot**.

**Step D — Assemble the gradient.**

$$\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho_o} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\rho_o^{2}}\right)\hat{e}_{ro}$$

**Step E — Apply the negative gradient to get the repulsive force.**

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho_o} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho_o^{2}}\,\hat{e}_{ro}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** When the robot is very close to the obstacle ($\rho_o \to 0$), the term $\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_o} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \approx \dfrac{1}{\rho_o}$ becomes very large, and combined with the $\dfrac{1}{\rho_o^{2}}$ factor, the force magnitude behaves as $\sim \dfrac{\eta}{\rho_o^{3}}$ — an **inverse-cubic blow-up** that guarantees safety. Far from the obstacle ($\rho_o > \rho_0$), the entire expression evaluates to zero, leaving only the attractive force to act.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 2-DOF point robot is at $q = (2, 1)^T$ m. The goal is at $q_{goal} = (8, 6)^T$ m. An obstacle has its closest point at $q_{obs} = (3, 2)^T$ m. Given $\xi = 1.0$, $\eta = 2.0$, $\rho_0 = 3.0$ m. Compute the total artificial force and the next configuration using $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.

**Step 1 — Distance to goal.**

$$\rho_{g} = \sqrt{(8-2)^{2} + (6-1)^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 25} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81\,\text{m}$$

Since $\rho_{g} > d^*_{goal}$ (assumed), use conic attractive form.

**Step 2 — Unit vector from goal to robot.**

$$\hat{e}_{qg} = \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{g}} = \frac{(-6,\,-5)}{7.81} \approx (-0.768,\,-0.640)$$

**Step 3 — Attractive force.**

$$F_{att} = -\xi\,\hat{e}_{qg} \cdot \rho_{g} = -1.0 \cdot (-0.768,\,-0.640)\cdot 7.81 = (6.0,\;5.0)\,\text{N}$$

> Verification: $F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal}) = -1.0 \cdot ((-6),(-5)) = (6, 5)$ ✓

**Step 4 — Distance to obstacle.**

$$\rho_o = \sqrt{(3-2)^{2} + (2-1)^{2}} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\,\text{m}$$

Since $\rho_o < \rho_0 = 3.0$, the repulsive potential is active.

**Step 5 — Unit vector from obstacle to robot.**

$$\hat{e}_{ro} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho_o} = \frac{(-1,-1)}{1.414} \approx (-0.707,\,-0.707)$$

**Step 6 — Repulsive force magnitude factor.**

$$k = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho_o} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho_o^{2}} = 2.0\left(\frac{1}{1.414} - \frac{1}{3.0}\right)\frac{1}{2.0}$$

$$k = 2.0 \cdot (0.7071 - 0.3333) \cdot 0.5 = 2.0 \cdot 0.3738 \cdot 0.5 = 0.3738$$

**Step 7 — Repulsive force vector.**

$$F_{rep} = k \cdot \hat{e}_{ro} = 0.3738 \cdot (-0.707,\,-0.707) \approx (-0.264,\,-0.264)\,\text{N}$$

**Step 8 — Total force.**

$$F_{total} = F_{att} + F_{rep} = (6.0 - 0.264,\; 5.0 - 0.264) = (5.736,\; 4.736)\,\text{N}$$

**Step 9 — Next configuration (Euler step).**

$$q_{k+1} = q_k + F_{total} \cdot \Delta t = (2,1) + (5.736,\;4.736)\cdot 0.1 = (2.574,\;1.474)\,\text{m}$$

> **Interpretation:** The robot moves approximately $0.74$ m in this step, primarily toward the goal but with a slight deflection away from the obstacle — the classic potential-field behavior.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Force Magnitude Behavior Summary

| Region | $\rho_o$ vs $\rho_0$ | $F_{rep}$ behavior | $F_{att}$ behavior | Net Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Far from obstacle, far from goal | $\rho_o > \rho_0$ | $0$ | Conic, constant $\xi$ | Strong goal pull |
| Near obstacle, far from goal | $\rho_o < \rho_0$ | Increasing, $\sim \eta/\rho_o^{3}$ | Strong, conic | Deflection begins |
| At obstacle boundary | $\rho_o = \rho_0$ | $0$ (continuous) | Strong | Smooth transition |
| Inside obstacle | $\rho_o \to 0$ | $\to \infty$ (blow-up) | — | Hard repulsion |
| Far from obstacle, near goal | $\rho_o > \rho_0$ | $0$ | Parabolic, $\to 0$ | Smooth docking |
| **Local minimum** | $\rho_o < \rho_0$ | $F_{att} = -F_{rep}$ | $F_{rep} = F_{att}$ | **Robot stuck** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Robot at q_init] --> B[Acquire Goal q_goal and Map]
    B --> C[Sample Configuration Space C]
    C --> D[Compute Attractive Potential U_att]
    D --> D1[Form: 0.5 xi rho_g^2]
    B --> E[Identify Obstacles and Closest Points]
    E --> F[Compute Repulsive Potential U_rep]
    F --> F1[Form: 0.5 eta times FIRAS term]
    D1 --> G[Superpose Fields]
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Compute Total Force F = minus gradient U_total]
    H --> I{Force Magnitude less than epsilon?}
    I -->|No| J[Update Configuration q_k+1 = q_k + F times dt]
    J --> K{Goal Reached? q within tolerance}
    K -->|No| D
    K -->|Yes| L[Stop: Path Complete]
    I -->|Yes, but q not at goal| M[Local Minimum Detected]
    M --> N[Apply Escape Strategy: Random Walk / Virtual Obstacle]
    N --> D
```

## 4.2 Potential Field Topology (Conceptual Force Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WS[Workspace 2D Plane]
        direction LR
        R[Robot q at position x y]:::robotNode
        G[Goal q_goal]:::goalNode
        O1[Obstacle 1]:::obsNode
        O2[Obstacle 2]:::obsNode
    end
    G -->|F_att pulls robot toward goal| R
    O1 -->|F_rep1 pushes robot away| R
    O2 -->|F_rep2 pushes robot away| R
    R -->|F_total vector| R
    classDef robotNode fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef goalNode fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef obsNode fill:#e57373,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Functional Pipeline — Force Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Current Configuration q]
        I2[Goal Configuration q_goal]
        I3[Obstacle Database]
    end
    subgraph COMP[Computation Layer]
        C1[Distance Module: rho_g and rho_o]
        C2[Attractive Potential Calculator]
        C3[Repulsive Potential Calculator]
        C4[Gradient Engine]
        C5[Force Vector Assembler]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Total Force F_total]
        O2[Next Configuration q_next]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> O1
    C5 --> O2
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Local Minima Detection and Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot at q_k] --> B[Compute F_total]
    B --> C{Norm of F_total less than threshold tau?}
    C -->|No| D[Normal Gradient Descent Step]
    D --> A
    C -->|Yes| E{Is q_k near q_goal within epsilon?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Success: Terminate Planning]
    E -->|No| G[Local Minimum Detected]
    G --> H[Recovery Strategy Selection]
    H --> H1[Strategy A: Random Walk Perturbation]
    H --> H2[Strategy B: Add Virtual Obstacle at q_k]
    H --> H3[Strategy C: Switch to Global Planner]
    H1 --> A
    H2 --> A
    H3 --> Z[Replan with A* or RRT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:**
> - **Yellow node** = robot (the moving agent).
> - **Green node** = goal (attractor).
> - **Red nodes** = obstacles (repellors).
> - **Block diagram 4.3** mirrors the actual code/implementation flow expected in KTU lab examinations.
> - **Diagram 4.4** captures the *most-asked-about* aspect of potential fields: **escape from local minima**.
