# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`**
> *Define artificial potential field method for path planning. List any two advantages and two limitations of the method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The artificial potential field method is a local path planning technique in which the robot's workspace is endowed with a scalar potential function $U(q)$. The goal exerts an attractive potential that pulls the robot toward it, while obstacles exert repulsive potentials that push the robot away. The robot moves along the negative gradient $-\nabla U(q)$ of the total potential field.
- **Two advantages (1 mark):** (i) Computationally efficient — $O(N)$ per step where $N$ is the number of nearby obstacles; (ii) Smooth and continuous trajectories emerge naturally from gradient descent.
- **Two limitations (0.5 mark):** (i) Susceptible to local minima where the robot gets stuck; (ii) Oscillations may occur in narrow corridors.

---

> **`Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
> *Write the mathematical expression for the FIRAS repulsive potential and explain the role of the parameter $\rho_0$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Expression (2 marks):**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_o} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho_o \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho_o > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

- **Role of $\rho_0$ (1 mark):** $\rho_0$ is the **distance of influence** (or threshold distance). It defines the maximum range within which an obstacle exerts a repulsive effect on the robot. Beyond $\rho_0$, the obstacle's influence is zero, ensuring that the robot is not perturbed by very distant obstacles. Inside $\rho_0$, the repulsive force grows as the robot approaches the obstacle, blowing up to infinity as $\rho_o \to 0$ to guarantee collision avoidance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **`Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Question Bank]`**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> *a)* Derive the expression for the **attractive force** $F_{att}(q)$ starting from the parabolic attractive potential $U_{att}(q) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{g}^{2}$. Show clearly that the force is a **constant-magnitude gradient** in the **far-field conic** case and a **linear** function in the **near-field parabolic** case. (7 marks)
> 
> *b)* A mobile robot is at $q = (1, 2)^T$ m with goal at $q_{goal} = (6, 8)^T$ m. Using the **composite (Khatib) attractive potential** with $\xi = 1.0$ and switching distance $d^*_{goal} = 2.0$ m, compute the attractive force vector. Verify whether the robot is in the conic or parabolic regime. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the distance function.** Let $\rho_{g} = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$, so $\rho_{g}^{2} = (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})$.

**Step 2 — Parabolic case derivation.** From $U_{att}^{para} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{g}^{2}$:

$$\nabla U_{att}^{para} = \frac{1}{2}\,\xi\cdot 2(q - q_{goal}) = \xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

$$F_{att}^{para} = -\nabla U_{att}^{para} = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude in parabolic case.**

$$\Vert F_{att}^{para} \Vert = \xi \cdot \rho_{g} \quad \text{(LINEAR in distance)}$$

**Step 4 — Conic case derivation.** From $U_{att}^{conic} = \xi\,\rho_{g}$:

$$\nabla U_{att}^{conic} = \xi \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{g}}$$

$$F_{att}^{conic} = -\xi \cdot \hat{e}_{qg} \quad \text{(unit vector toward goal)}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude in conic case.**

$$\Vert F_{att}^{conic} \Vert = \xi \quad \text{(CONSTANT — independent of distance)}$$

**Step 6 — Composite blending.** The composite potential switches from parabolic (near) to conic (far) at $d^*_{goal}$:

$$U_{att}^{comp} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,\rho_{g}^{2}, & \rho_{g} \leq d^*_{goal} \\ \xi\,d^*_{goal}\,\rho_{g} - \dfrac{1}{2}\,\xi\,d^{*2}_{goal}, & \rho_{g} > d^*_{goal} \end{cases}$$

This ensures $C^1$ continuity of the force field and smooth robot deceleration near the goal.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute distance to goal.**

$$\rho_{g} = \sqrt{(6-1)^{2} + (8-2)^{2}} = \sqrt{25 + 36} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Identify regime.** Since $\rho_{g} = 7.81$ m $> d^*_{goal} = 2.0$ m, the robot is in the **conic regime**.

**Step 3 — Compute unit vector from goal to robot.**

$$\hat{e}_{qg} = \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{g}} = \frac{(-5,\,-6)}{7.81} = (-0.640,\,-0.768)$$

**Step 4 — Compute attractive force (conic form).**

$$F_{att} = -\xi\,\hat{e}_{qg} = -1.0 \cdot (-0.640,\,-0.768) = (0.640,\;0.768)\,\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude check.** $\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \sqrt{0.640^{2} + 0.768^{2}} = \sqrt{0.4096 + 0.5898} = \sqrt{0.9994} \approx 1.0 = \xi$ ✓ (confirms conic regime).

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** The robot experiences a constant attractive force of magnitude $1.0$ N directed toward the goal. As it approaches within $2.0$ m, the force will smoothly transition to the parabolic form and decrease linearly with remaining distance, ensuring smooth docking.

---

> **`Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Question Bank]`**
>
> **Question B (14 marks):**
> *a)* Explain the concept of **local minima** in potential field path planning. With a suitable sketch, describe the **GNRON** (Goal-Non-Reachable-Obstacle-Noise) problem and the **local minima trap** between two closely placed obstacles. (7 marks)
> 
> *b)* A 2D point robot navigates using potential fields with $\xi = 1.5$, $\eta = 4.0$, and $\rho_0 = 2.5$ m. The robot is at $q = (3, 3)^T$ m, goal at $q_{goal} = (10, 10)^T$ m, and a single obstacle has its closest point at $q_{obs} = (4, 4)^T$ m. Compute the **total force vector** and the **next configuration** after one step with $\Delta t = 0.05$ s. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Concept of Local Minima (3 marks):** A local minimum is a configuration $q_{lm} \in C_{free}$ where $\nabla U_{total}(q_{lm}) = 0$ but $q_{lm} \neq q_{goal}$. At this point, the attractive and repulsive forces are balanced in both magnitude and direction, so the robot halts despite not having reached the goal. The PFM cannot distinguish local minima from the global minimum (goal).

**GNRON Problem (2 marks):** Goal-Non-Reachable-when-Obstacle-is-Near (GNRON) occurs when an obstacle is very close to the goal. The repulsive force from the obstacle pushes the robot *away from the goal*, but the attractive force decreases near the goal (parabolic form). At equilibrium, the robot stops at a point that is *not* the goal — even though the goal is technically reachable in the configuration space. The classic solution is to modify the repulsive potential (e.g., adding a $(q - q_{goal})$ dependent term).

**Local Minima Trap Between Two Obstacles (2 marks):** When two obstacles are placed symmetrically with respect to the line from robot to goal, the repulsive forces from both obstacles add up to a vector that exactly cancels the attractive force. The robot is "trapped" at a saddle point or true local minimum. The diagram below illustrates:

```
        O1 (obstacle)
         \\
          \\  F_rep1
           \\
   q_lm  ----X----  q_goal
           //
          //  F_rep2
         //
        O2 (obstacle)
         |
   F_att (cancels net repulsion)
```

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Distance to goal.**

$$\rho_{g} = \sqrt{(10-3)^{2} + (10-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{49 + 49} = 7\sqrt{2} \approx 9.899\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Unit vector goal-to-robot.**

$$\hat{e}_{qg} = \frac{(-7,\,-7)}{9.899} = (-0.707,\,-0.707)$$

**Step 3 — Attractive force (conic regime assumed).**

$$F_{att} = -\xi\,\hat{e}_{qg}\cdot \rho_{g} = -\xi\,(q - q_{goal}) = -1.5 \cdot (-7,\,-7) = (10.5,\;10.5)\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Distance to obstacle.**

$$\rho_o = \sqrt{(4-3)^{2} + (4-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\,\text{m}$$

Since $\rho_o = 1.414 < \rho_0 = 2.5$, repulsive force is active.

**Step 5 — Unit vector obstacle-to-robot.**

$$\hat{e}_{ro} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho_o} = \frac{(-1,\,-1)}{1.414} = (-0.707,\,-0.707)$$

**Step 6 — Compute FIRAS factor.**

$$u = \frac{1}{\rho_o} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} = \frac{1}{1.414} - \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.7071 - 0.4 = 0.3071$$

$$F_{rep} = \eta \cdot u \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_o^{2}} \cdot \hat{e}_{ro} = 4.0 \cdot 0.3071 \cdot \frac{1}{2.0} \cdot (-0.707,\,-0.707)$$

$$F_{rep} = 4.0 \cdot 0.3071 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (-0.707,\,-0.707) = 0.6142 \cdot (-0.707,\,-0.707)$$

$$F_{rep} = (-0.434,\;-0.434)\,\text{N}$$

**Step 7 — Total force.**

$$F_{total} = (10.5 - 0.434,\; 10.5 - 0.434) = (10.066,\;10.066)\,\text{N}$$

**Step 8 — Next configuration.**

$$q_{k+1} = (3,3) + (10.066,\;10.066)\cdot 0.05 = (3 + 0.503,\; 3 + 0.503) = (3.503,\;3.503)\,\text{m}$$

**Step 9 — Verification & interpretation.** The robot moved approximately $0.71$ m diagonally toward the goal. The obstacle influence is small relative to the strong attractive pull, indicating a well-tuned PFM where $\xi\rho_{g} \gg \eta \cdot u/\rho_o^{2}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{1}{2}$ factor** in the repulsive potential — KTU key explicitly checks this constant.
> 2. **Conflating conic and parabolic force formulas** — students often write $F_{att} = -\xi\,\hat{e}_{qg}$ for the parabolic case. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Sign error in $\hat{e}_{ro}$** — it must point from **obstacle to robot**, not robot to obstacle. Wrong direction ⇒ wrong force ⇒ $0$ to $-2$ marks.
> 4. **Not verifying regime conditions** — when $\rho_{g} > d^*_{goal}$, you MUST use the conic form. Examiners deduct marks if the wrong form is used without justification.
> 5. **Ignoring $\rho_o > \rho_0$ edge case** — if the computed $\rho_o$ exceeds $\rho_0$, the entire repulsive force is **zero**. Writing a non-zero value here is a critical error.
> 6. **Not stating the local minima limitation** — Part B questions almost always carry a sub-part on local minima. Omitting it costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PFM = Attractive + Repulsive fields** linearly superposed.
- [x] **Robot motion law:** $q_{k+1} = q_k - \nabla U_{total}(q_k)\cdot \Delta t$.
- [x] **Parabolic attractive:** $F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_{goal})$, magnitude $\propto \rho_{g}$ (linear).
- [x] **Conic attractive:** $F_{att} = -\xi\,\hat{e}_{qg}$, magnitude $= \xi$ (constant).
- [x] **Composite attractive:** conic far + parabolic near $q_{goal}$, switches at $d^*_{goal}$.
- [x] **FIRAS repulsive potential:** $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta(\rho_o^{-1} - \rho_0^{-1})^{2}$, active only for $\rho_o \leq \rho_0$.
- [x] **Repulsive force magnitude:** $\sim \eta/\rho_o^{3}$ near the obstacle (inverse-cubic blow-up).
- [x] **$\rho_0$ = distance of influence** — beyond this, no repulsion.
- [x] **$\eta$ controls obstacle avoidance aggressiveness; $\xi$ controls goal pursuit aggressiveness.**
- [x] **Local minima** are the principal failure mode — robot stops at $\nabla U_{total} = 0 \neq q_{goal}$.
- [x] **GNRON problem:** obstacle near goal creates unreachable goal under standard PFM.
- [x] **Escape strategies:** random walk, virtual obstacles, hybrid A*/RRT, modified potential.
- [x] **Narrow-passage oscillations** are a known limitation — robot may jitter between two opposing obstacle walls.
- [x] **Computation complexity:** $O(N)$ per step — extremely fast, ideal for real-time applications.
- [x] **Not suitable for non-holonomic robots directly** — extensions (e.g., adding a tangential term) are required.
- [x] **Common KTU exam trap:** students forget the $\frac{1}{2}$ in $U_{rep}$ — always include it.
- [x] **Standard reference for viva:** *Khatib, O. (1986), "Real-Time Obstacle Avoidance for Manipulators and Mobile Robots," International Journal of Robotics Research.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle Avoidance - Bug Algorithm, Vector Field Histogram, Dynamic Window Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Avoidance - Bug Algorithm, Vector Field Histogram, Dynamic Window Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Obstacle Avoidance — The Robotic Imperative

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
*Obstacle Avoidance* in mobile robotics refers to the set of real-time control strategies and algorithms that enable an autonomous agent to navigate from a start configuration to a goal configuration while preventing collisions with static or dynamic obstacles present in its operational workspace. It is classified as a **reactive (local) navigation** paradigm, relying on onboard sensor feedback (sonar, lidar, depth cameras) rather than a complete prior world model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Obstacle avoidance is a **sub-module of local path planning** and is fundamentally different from *global path planning*. Global planners (A*, Dijkstra) assume full a-priori knowledge of the map, while reactive avoidance methods operate in unknown, partially known, or dynamically changing environments. In KTU board questions, examiners frequently test the *contrast* between Bug algorithms (sensor-based, simple) and VFH/DWA (potential field/velocity-space based).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Performance Metrics (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Path Length Optimality** — ratio of executed path to geometric shortest path
> - **Clearance** — minimum distance maintained from obstacles
> - **Smoothness** — bounded curvature / angular velocity (no jerk)
> - **Completeness** — guarantee of reaching the goal if it exists
> - **Time Complexity** — algorithmic responsiveness in real-time control loops (typically **10–100 Hz**)

---

## 1.2 The Three Foundational Approaches — A Bird's-Eye View

| Algorithm | Core Philosophy | Sensor Assumption | Computational Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bug Algorithm** | "Touch the obstacle, then go around it" | Contact sensor + range sensor | Trivially O(1) per step |
| **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** | "Build a polar histogram of obstacle density, steer into valleys" | Dense range scan (lidar/sonar ring) | O(n) per scan |
| **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** | "Search admissible velocities in velocity space" | Range scan + odometry | O($\vert V_s \vert$) per cycle |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Bug Algorithm = Walking Blindfolded in a Room**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to walk straight to a door on the opposite side of a room containing a table. The *Bug* strategy says: *walk straight toward the door until you bump into the table, then follow the table's edge with your right hand until the door-direction is "clear" again*. You literally "bug" around the obstacle. This is the simplest, most biologically inspired approach.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — VFH = Driving with a Polorized Windshield**
> Picture yourself driving a car in fog with a polarizer on the windshield. You overlay "obstacle density" on every angular sector ahead. Sectors crowded with obstacles (high density) repel you, just as they would in an electrostatic field. You instinctively steer toward the *valley* (low density) closest to the goal. VFH formalizes this intuition mathematically.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 3 — DWA = Selecting the Safest Stair in a Running Staircase**
> Imagine a moving escalator. At every instant you may choose any of three actions: *speed up, slow down, or turn*. The DWA evaluates **every** admissible (v, ω) pair within a "dynamic window" bounded by your current acceleration limits and predicts where each would take you in the next 0.5 seconds. The pair that maximizes a combined objective (toward-goal, clearance, velocity) is selected. It is a *constrained optimization in velocity space*.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This module directly evaluates **Course Outcomes** such as:
- **CO4:** *Design and analyze local navigation strategies for mobile robots under uncertainty.*
- **CO5:** *Apply reactive control paradigms in real-time robotic systems.*

Board questions (especially 14-mark Part B) frequently demand:
- A complete trace of Bug 1 / Bug 2 along a hand-drawn map.
- The polar histogram construction for VFH with thresholding.
- The admissible velocity region derivation for DWA.

The next section builds the rigorous mathematical foundation required to solve all of the above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bug Family of Algorithms (Bug 0, Bug 1, Bug 2, Tangent Bug)

### 2.1.1 The Generic Bug Algorithm Skeleton

Every Bug algorithm is composed of two alternating **primitive behaviors** executed in a Moore-state-machine loop:

- **Behavior 1 — `move_to_goal`:** Drive the robot in a straight line from current position toward the goal $g$. Terminate this behavior on one of two events:
  - The goal is reached → **SUCCESS**.
  - An obstacle is *detected* by the contact sensor (or range threshold) → switch to Behavior 2.
- **Behavior 2 — `follow_boundary`:** Walk along the obstacle perimeter (typically keeping the obstacle to the right — clockwise traversal) until either:
  - The goal is reached → **SUCCESS**.
  - A point on the boundary is encountered from which the line-of-sight to the goal is clear and the goal can be resumed → leave the obstacle and re-enter Behavior 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reachability Assumption (Standard KTU Statement):** The goal is *assumed reachable* — i.e., the connected component of free space containing the robot also contains the goal. The Bug algorithms are **complete** under this assumption.

### 2.1.2 Bug 0 — The Naive Variant

**Logic:**
1. Head straight toward the goal.
2. On contact, circumnavigate the **entire** obstacle until returning to the *departure point* $q_{leave}$.
3. Resume motion toward the goal.

**Path length bound:**

$$L_{Bug\,0} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_{start}, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2}$$

where $p_i$ is the perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle circumnavigated. The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ assumes optimal departure from the *closest* point of the perimeter — but Bug 0 departs from the *original hit point*, hence the $\frac{p}{2}$ is a generous upper bound; in practice the algorithm may perform worse.

> [!WARNING]
> Bug 0 is rarely asked in KTU boards; it is presented only as a contrast to motivate Bug 1.

### 2.1.3 Bug 1 — The "Circumnavigate-and-Return" Algorithm

**Logic (Algorithm Step-by-Step):**
1. **Phase A — Approach:** Move from start $q_{start}$ toward goal $g$ along the straight line segment $m$-line (also called the **$m$-line**, the line connecting start to goal).
2. **Phase B — Boundary Tracing:** Upon hitting an obstacle at point $q_{hit}$, follow the obstacle's boundary in a chosen direction (say, clockwise). Record the **closest point to the goal** $q_{L}$ seen so far during the walk, along with its distance to the goal.
3. **Phase C — Departure:** Upon returning to $q_{hit}$, leave the obstacle at $q_{L}$ (the point on the perimeter that is geometrically closest to $g$) and resume straight-line motion along the $m$-line.
4. **Termination:** Stop when $g$ is reached.

**Path Optimality Bound:**

$$L_{Bug\,1} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_{start}, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

That is, in the **worst case**, Bug 1 traverses the *entire* perimeter of every obstacle it encounters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worst-Case Intuition:** If the goal lies on the "far side" of the obstacle from $q_{hit}$, Bug 1 will walk around nearly the whole perimeter before identifying the optimal departure point. This worst-case behavior is precisely what Bug 2 attempts to improve.

### 2.1.4 Bug 2 — The "Leave-on-Goal-Alignment" Algorithm

**Logic (Algorithm Step-by-Step):**
1. **Phase A — Approach:** Same as Bug 1; move along the $m$-line.
2. **Phase B — Boundary Tracing:** Upon hitting at $q_{hit}$, follow the boundary. **Continuously check** whether the robot's current position lies on the $m$-line and is *closer* to the goal than $q_{hit}$.
3. **Phase C — Departure:** As soon as both conditions hold, leave the obstacle immediately along the $m$-line.
4. **Termination:** Stop when $g$ is reached.

**Path Optimality Bound:**

$$L_{Bug\,2} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_{start}, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Bug 2 has a strictly tighter bound than Bug 1, but is **heuristically greedy** — it can get stuck in local loops if the $m$-line re-enters the obstacle at multiple points. In practice, Bug 2 is preferred when the obstacle is convex and aligned with the $m$-line.

### 2.1.5 Tangent Bug — The Range-Sensor Improvement

**Logic:** Tangent Bug is a *Bug 2 variant* that uses a **range sensor** (not a contact sensor) to *pre-look* around the obstacle. At every step it computes a **tangent graph** — the set of endpoints of range rays that graze obstacle discontinuities. It then follows the *tangent* that minimizes a cost combining Euclidean progress toward the goal and range to the obstacle boundary.

**Path Bound:**

$$L_{Tangent\,Bug} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_{start}, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{p_i}{2} + B_i \right)$$

where $B_i$ depends on the sensor range $R$: $B_i = O(R^2 \cdot \text{obstacle density})$ in dense environments.

---

## 2.2 Vector Field Histogram (VFH) — Borenstein & Koren (1991)

### 2.2.1 Pipeline Overview

VFH operates in three discrete stages per control cycle:

**Stage 1 — Cartesian Occupancy Grid Update:** Each range measurement $r_i$ at angle $\theta_i$ is mapped into a 2D Cartesian grid cell of resolution $d_{cell}$. Cells receiving a "hit" increment their certainty value $CV(x, y)$.

**Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction:** The Cartesian grid is mapped into a 1D **polar histogram** $H$ with $n$ angular sectors (typically $n = 72$, i.e., 5° resolution). For each sector $k$, the histogram magnitude is:

$$H(k) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} CV(x_i, y_i) \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{d_i}{R_{max}} \right)^2 \cdot \cos^2(\alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k})$$

where:
- $m$ = number of active cells in the histogram's region of interest
- $d_i$ = distance from robot to cell $i$
- $R_{max}$ = maximum sensor range
- $\alpha_i$ = angle from robot to cell $i$
- $\alpha_{0,k}$ = center angle of sector $k$
- The squared cosine term applies a *directional weighting* — cells directly aligned with the sector contribute most.

**Stage 3 — Steering Direction Selection:** Two thresholds are applied:
- **Low threshold $\tau_{low}$:** sectors with $H(k) < \tau_{low}$ are *free*.
- **High threshold $\tau_{high}$:** sectors with $H(k) > \tau_{high}$ are *blocked*.

The algorithm scans for the *largest contiguous gap* of free sectors and selects the gap whose **centroid direction** $k_{target}$ is closest to the goal direction. The robot steers at angular velocity:

$$\omega_{cmd} \;=\; K_p \cdot (\theta_{target} - \theta_{current})$$

### 2.2.2 VFH+ and VFH* (Enhancements)

- **VFH+** (Ulrich & Borenstein, 1998) adds a **robot geometry / footprint** term, modeling the robot as a circular disc of radius $r_{robot}$. This prevents squeezing through gaps narrower than the robot.
- **VFH*** introduces A* search over the polar histogram to add *temporal look-ahead*, producing globally consistent local trajectories.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Fox, Burgard & Thrun (1997)

### 2.3.1 Problem Formulation

At each control cycle, the robot has translational velocity $v$ and rotational velocity $\omega$. The reachable velocities in the *next* control step are bounded by:

**Bound 1 — Max Velocities:**

$$V_m \;=\; \{(v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}] \;\wedge\; \omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]\}$$

**Bound 2 — Accelerations (the "Dynamic Window"):**

$$V_d \;=\; \{(v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_c - \dot{v}_a \cdot \Delta t,\; v_c + \dot{v}_a \cdot \Delta t] \;\wedge\; \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_a \cdot \Delta t,\; \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_a \cdot \Delta t]\}$$

where:
- $v_c, \omega_c$ = current velocities
- $\dot{v}_a, \dot{\omega}_a$ = max accelerations
- $\Delta t$ = control period

**Bound 3 — Safety (Admissible Velocities):**

$$V_a \;=\; \{(v, \omega) \mid \text{robot can stop before hitting nearest obstacle}\}$$

$$V_a = \left\{(v, \omega) \,\bigg|\, v \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b} \;\wedge\; \omega \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b}\right\}$$

where $\dot{v}_b, \dot{\omega}_b$ are max decelerations and $d(v, \omega)$ is the *braking distance* to the nearest obstacle along the circular trajectory defined by $(v, \omega)$.

**Search Space:**

$$V_s \;=\; V_m \cap V_d \cap V_a$$

### 2.3.2 The DWA Objective Function

For each sampled $(v, \omega) \in V_s$, the algorithm simulates a short trajectory of duration $\Delta t$ and evaluates:

$$G(v, \omega) \;=\; \sigma \left( \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) \;+\; \beta \cdot \text{clearness}(v, \omega) \;+\; \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega) \right)$$

where $\sigma$ is a smoothing normalization, and the three terms are:

$$\text{heading}(v, \omega) \;=\; 1 - \frac{\vert \theta_{target} - \theta_{predicted} \vert}{\pi}$$

$$\text{clearness}(v, \omega) \;=\; \frac{d(v, \omega)}{d_{max}}$$

$$\text{velocity}(v, \omega) \;=\; \frac{v}{v_{max}}$$

The optimal command is:

$$(v^*, \omega^*) \;=\; \underset{(v, \omega) \in V_s}{\arg\max}\; G(v, \omega)$$

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Quantity | Units | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{Bug\,1}$ | Worst-case Bug 1 path length | m | $L_{Euclidean} + \sum p_i$ |
| $L_{Bug\,2}$ | Worst-case Bug 2 path length | m | $L_{Euclidean} + \sum p_i/2$ |
| $H(k)$ | VFH polar histogram magnitude for sector $k$ | dimensionless | $[0, H_{max}]$ |
| $\tau_{low}$ | VFH low threshold | dimensionless | typically $H_{max}/4$ |
| $\tau_{high}$ | VFH high threshold | dimensionless | typically $3 H_{max}/4$ |
| $V_m$ | Maximum velocity set | $\vert v \vert = m/s, \vert \omega \vert = rad/s$ | rectangular in $(v, \omega)$-space |
| $V_d$ | Dynamic-window reachable set | $m/s, rad/s$ | intersection with $V_m$ |
| $V_a$ | Admissible (safe) velocity set | $m/s, rad/s$ | bounded by braking distance |
| $V_s$ | DWA search space | $m/s, rad/s$ | $V_m \cap V_d \cap V_a$ |
| $G(v, \omega)$ | DWA objective | dimensionless | weighted sum of heading, clearness, velocity |
| $\dot{v}_a, \dot{v}_b$ | Accel / decel bounds | $m/s^2$ | vehicle dynamics |
| $\Delta t$ | Control period | $s$ | typically $0.1 - 0.5$ |
| $R$ | Sensor range | $m$ | $2 - 30$ typical |
| $\rho$ | Robot radius | $m$ | platform-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "derive" the DWA search space, examiners expect to see the three-set intersection written out explicitly, with **bound equations**, not just the symbolic result. Always state *which physical constraint* each bound represents.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of These Algorithms |
|---|---|
| **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva, Fetch)** | DWA-style velocity selection inside aisles cluttered with shelves and human workers |
| **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Cruise)** | VFH+ for low-speed maneuvering; Frenet-frame DWA variants for highway lane changes |
| **Vacuum Cleaning Robots (iRobot Roomba)** | Tangent Bug variant with infrared range sensors |
| **Mars Rovers (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance)** | AutoNav merges visual SLAM with Bug-2-like local recovery behaviors |
| **Underwater AUVs (REMUS class)** | Bug 1/Bug 2 with sonar for sparse, unknown seabed environments |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Path-Length Bound of Bug 2

**Given:** A mobile robot starting at $q_s$, navigating to a goal $g$, traversing $n$ obstacles with perimeters $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$.

**Claim to Prove:**

$$L_{Bug\,2} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_s, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Decompose the path into straight segments and boundary walks.**

The Bug 2 path consists of alternating straight-line segments (along the $m$-line between obstacles) and boundary-following segments (around each obstacle). Therefore:

$$L_{Bug\,2} \;=\; \sum_{j} L_{straight}^{(j)} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{boundary}^{(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Bound the straight segments by their projection on the $m$-line.**

Each straight segment is a chord of the $m$-line, so its length is at most the projection of the segment onto the $m$-line. Summing all straight segments gives a total length no greater than the length of the $m$-line, i.e., the Euclidean distance from start to goal:

$$\sum_{j} L_{straight}^{(j)} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_s, g)$$

**Step 3 — Bound the boundary segment for the $i$-th obstacle.**

For obstacle $i$, the robot leaves the boundary at the first point $q_{L}^{(i)}$ on the $m$-line that is *closer to the goal* than the hit point $q_{hit}^{(i)}$. The boundary walk therefore begins at $q_{hit}^{(i)}$ and ends at $q_{L}^{(i)}$, traversing an arc of the obstacle perimeter.

**Geometric argument:** The Euclidean distance from $q_{hit}^{(i)}$ to $g$ equals the distance from $q_{L}^{(i)}$ to $g$ (since both lie on the $m$-line which is the locus of points at varying distances from $g$ along a fixed direction). Wait — that's incorrect; $q_{L}^{(i)}$ is *closer* to $g$ than $q_{hit}^{(i)}$ by algorithm definition.

**Corrected geometric argument:** The straight-line distance from $q_{hit}^{(i)}$ to $q_{L}^{(i)}$ along the $m$-line is $d_{progress}^{(i)} \geq 0$. The boundary arc traversed is the *shorter* of the two paths around the perimeter from $q_{hit}^{(i)}$ to $q_{L}^{(i)}$. The worst case is when $q_{L}^{(i)}$ is the point diametrically opposite to $q_{hit}^{(i)}$ on the perimeter, in which case the boundary walk equals **half the perimeter**:

$$L_{boundary}^{(i)} \;\leq\; \frac{p_i}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all obstacles.**

$$L_{Bug\,2} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_s, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Why Bug 1 has a *weaker* bound.**

Bug 1 departs at the *closest point to the goal* only after walking the **entire perimeter** to confirm — so the boundary walk can equal the *full* perimeter:

$$L_{Bug\,1} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_s, g) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: VFH Polar Histogram Magnitude for Sector $k$

**Given:** A Cartesian grid $G$ with $M$ active cells, each with certainty value $CV(x_i, y_i)$, distance $d_i$ from the robot, and angular position $\alpha_i$ from the robot's heading.

**Derive:** The polar histogram sector magnitude $H(k)$.

**Step 1 — Define the sector's angular influence.**

A cell at angle $\alpha_i$ contributes to all sectors $k$ whose center $\alpha_{0,k}$ lies within a window of $\pm \pi/n$ (one sector width) of $\alpha_i$. The cosine-squared weighting expresses this as a smooth convolution:

$$w_{i,k} \;=\; \cos^2\!\left(\alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k}\right) \quad \text{for } \vert \alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k} \vert < \frac{\pi}{n}$$

$$w_{i,k} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{otherwise}$$

**Step 2 — Apply a distance-squared decay.**

Cells far from the robot matter less (the sensor is less accurate, and the obstacle is less immediately threatening):

$$d_{i} \;=\; \left( 1 - \frac{d_i}{R_{max}} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the histogram bin value.**

Summing all cells' contributions:

$$H(k) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{M} CV(x_i, y_i) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{R_{max}}\right)^2 \cdot \cos^2(\alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k})$$

$$\boxed{H(k) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{M} CV_i \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{R_{max}}\right)^2 \cdot \cos^2(\alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k})} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The $\cos^2$ term ensures that the histogram is a *smooth* polar density, not a discontinuous bar chart. This smoothness is what allows VFH to identify the "valleys" (free directions) reliably even with sparse sensor data.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DWA Admissible Velocity Set $V_a$

**Given:** A differential-drive robot with current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$, max deceleration $(\dot{v}_b, \dot{\omega}_b)$, and a candidate velocity $(v, \omega)$. Let $d(v, \omega)$ be the distance to the nearest obstacle along the predicted trajectory.

**Derive:** The condition that $(v, \omega) \in V_a$ (the robot can stop before colliding).

**Step 1 — Braking kinematic equation (translation).**

For a robot decelerating from $v$ at $\dot{v}_b$ to zero, the braking distance is:

$$d_{brake}^{(v)} \;=\; \frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b}$$

**Step 2 — Set the safety constraint.**

The robot must be able to stop within the available clearance:

$$d_{brake}^{(v)} \;\leq\; d(v, \omega)$$

$$\frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b} \;\leq\; d(v, \omega)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v$.**

$$v^2 \;\leq\; 2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b$$

$$v \;\leq\; \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the analogous derivation to rotation.**

For a robot decelerating from $\omega$ at $\dot{\omega}_b$:

$$\omega \;\leq\; \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b}$$

**Step 5 — Define the admissible set.**

The admissible set is the intersection of all four inequalities:

$$V_a \;=\; \left\{ (v, \omega) \,\bigg|\, 0 \;\leq\; v \;\leq\; \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b} \;\wedge\; 0 \;\leq\; \omega \;\leq\; \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b} \right\}$$

**Step 6 — Form the final DWA search space.**

$$V_s \;=\; V_m \;\cap\; V_d \;\cap\; V_a$$

$$\boxed{V_s = \left\{ (v, \omega) \,\bigg|\, \begin{array}{l} v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}] \\ \omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}] \\ v \in [v_c - \dot{v}_a \Delta t,\, v_c + \dot{v}_a \Delta t] \\ \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t,\, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t] \\ v \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b} \\ \omega \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{\omega}_b} \end{array} \right\}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Bug 1 vs Bug 2 vs VFH vs DWA)

| Parameter | Bug 1 | Bug 2 | VFH | DWA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor Req.** | Contact + range | Contact + range | Dense 2D scan | Range + odometry |
| **Memory** | Perimeter log | Perimeter log | Occupancy grid | Last scan |
| **Map Required** | None | None | Local grid | Local scan |
| **Worst-Case Path** | $L + \sum p_i$ | $L + \sum p_i/2$ | Local min risk | Depends on weights |
| **Completeness** | Yes | Yes (heuristic) | Local completeness | Local |
| **Dynamic Obstacles** | Poor | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Kinodynamic Awareness** | None | None | None | Full |
| **Computation / Cycle** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(M)$ | $O(\vert V_s \vert)$ |
| **Common Use** | Teaching baseline | Reactive nav | Indoor AGV | Service / mobile robots |
| **KTU Frequency** | High | High | High | High |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — VFH Thresholding

**Setup:** A 5-sector VFH histogram has values $H = [0, 12, 0, 18, 4]$ with thresholds $\tau_{low} = 5$ and $\tau_{high} = 15$. The goal is at sector 3 (center).

**Step 1 — Classify each sector.**
- Sector 1: $H(1)=0 < 5$ → **free** (clear)
- Sector 2: $H(2)=12$, $5 < 12 < 15$ → **caution**
- Sector 3: $H(3)=0 < 5$ → **free**
- Sector 4: $H(4)=18 > 15$ → **blocked**
- Sector 5: $H(5)=4 < 5$ → **free**

**Step 2 — Identify contiguous free gaps.**
- Gap 1: Sectors {1} — width 1
- Gap 2: Sectors {3} — width 1
- Gap 3: Sectors {3, 5} — discontinuous, so not contiguous
- Gap 4: Sectors {5} — width 1

**Step 3 — Pick the gap closest to the goal direction (sector 3).**
Goal-aligned gap is **Gap 2 = Sector 3** itself. The robot steers at $\theta_{target} = \alpha_{0,3}$.

**Step 4 — Angular velocity command.**

$$\omega_{cmd} \;=\; K_p \cdot (\theta_{target} - \theta_{current})$$

With $K_p = 2.0$ rad/s per radian and current heading 0.1 rad left of target:

$$\omega_{cmd} \;=\; 2.0 \cdot (\theta_{target} - \theta_{current}) \;=\; 2.0 \cdot 0.1 \;=\; 0.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

The robot turns right at 0.2 rad/s to align with sector 3.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Bug Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at q_start] --> B{Check m-line position}
    B -- On m-line --> C[Move toward goal g]
    C -- Goal reached --> Z[SUCCESS]
    C -- Contact sensor hit --> D[Record hit point q_hit]
    D --> E[Follow obstacle boundary clockwise]
    E --> F{Goal on m-line closer than q_hit?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Record leave point q_L]
    G --> H{Return to q_hit?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Depart along m-line at q_L]
    I --> B
    H -- No --> E
    F -- No --> E
    B -- Off m-line --> E
```

## 4.2 VFH Three-Stage Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Range Scan] --> S2[Stage 2: Cartesian Grid Update]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Polar Histogram H of k]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Apply thresholds tau_low and tau_high]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Find largest free gap]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Select target sector near goal]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Compute omega command]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Send to motor controller]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.3 DWA Velocity Search Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current state v_c, omega_c] --> B[Compute V_m: max velocity rectangle]
    A --> C[Compute V_d: dynamic window rectangle]
    A --> D[Compute V_a: admissible safe set]
    B --> E[Intersect V_m and V_d and V_a = V_s]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Sample discrete v, omega pairs in V_s]
    F --> G[For each pair simulate trajectory of delta t]
    G --> H[Evaluate G v, omega]
    H --> I[Select argmax pair]
    I --> J[Send v star omega star to motors]
    J --> A
```

## 4.4 State Diagram: Bug Algorithm Decision Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MoveToGoal
    MoveToGoal --> GoalReached: Position at goal
    MoveToGoal --> FollowBoundary: Obstacle detected
    FollowBoundary --> MoveToGoal: Leave condition met
    FollowBoundary --> GoalReached: Goal reached during boundary walk
    GoalReached --> [*]
```

## 4.5 VFH Polar Histogram Visualization (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ScanInput
        S1[Lidar range measurements r_1 to r_n]
    end
    subgraph Stage1
        G1[Cartesian occupancy grid with CV values]
    end
    subgraph Stage2
        H1[Polar histogram H of k for k equals 1 to 72]
    end
    subgraph Stage3
        T1[Threshold filter with tau_low and tau_high]
        G2[Largest free gap finder]
        C1[Goal-aligned sector selector k_target]
    end
    subgraph Output
        O1[Angular velocity omega_cmd]
    end
    S1 --> G1 --> H1 --> T1 --> G2 --> C1 --> O1
```

## 4.6 DWA Objective Function Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Sample v and omega in V_s]
        I2[Predicted trajectory over delta t]
    end
    subgraph Metrics
        M1[heading: angle to goal]
        M2[clearness: distance to nearest obstacle]
        M3[velocity: forward speed v over v_max]
    end
    subgraph WeightedSum
        W1[alpha times heading]
        W2[beta times clearness]
        W3[gamma times velocity]
    end

        O1[Sum G and smooth by sigma]

    end

    I2 --> M3
    M1 --> W1
    M2 --> W2

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms with respect to leave-condition and worst-case path length.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bug 1 | Bug 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Leave condition** | Leaves at the *point on perimeter closest to goal* after walking the *entire perimeter* | Leaves at the *first* point on the $m$-line that is closer to the goal than $q_{hit}$ |
| **Worst-case path** | $L_{Bug\,1} = L_{Euclidean} + \sum p_i$ | $L_{Bug\,2} = L_{Euclidean} + \sum p_i / 2$ |
| **Departure optimality** | Globally optimal (within that obstacle) | Greedy / heuristic |

**(1 mark)** Correct identification of the leave condition. **(1 mark)** Correct path-length bound for either. **(1 mark)** Comparison / contrast statement.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the role of the "dynamic window" in the Dynamic Window Approach?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The dynamic window $V_d$ constrains the set of velocities $(v, \omega)$ that are *kinodynamically reachable* in the next control cycle, given the robot's current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$ and its acceleration limits $(\dot{v}_a, \dot{\omega}_a)$:

$$V_d = \{(v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_c - \dot{v}_a \Delta t,\, v_c + \dot{v}_a \Delta t],\; \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t,\, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t]\}$$

**(1 mark)** Definition of dynamic window. **(1 mark)** Acceleration bound. **(1 mark)** Justification of why it is needed (prevents unreachable / unsafe commands).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks. It is divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The examiner provides an internal choice (Or option). Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as two independent options.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: With a neat trace, explain the working of the Bug 2 algorithm. Derive its worst-case path length bound.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the algorithm (3 marks):**

Bug 2 operates in two alternating behaviors:
1. *Move-to-Goal:* Travel along the $m$-line from start $q_s$ to goal $g$. If an obstacle is hit at $q_{hit}$, switch to boundary following.
2. *Follow-Boundary:* Walk along the obstacle's perimeter. At every step, check whether the robot's current position lies on the $m$-line and is *closer to the goal* than $q_{hit}$. If yes, leave the obstacle and resume the $m$-line trajectory.

**Step 2 — Trace illustration (1 mark):**

A diagram should show the $m$-line from $q_s$ to $g$, an obstacle intersected at $q_{hit}$, a clockwise boundary walk, and a leave point $q_{L}$ on the $m$-line closer to $g$.

**Step 3 — Derive the worst-case bound (3 marks):**

Decompose the path into straight-line segments and boundary walks. Each straight segment projects onto the $m$-line, so:

$$\sum_{j} L_{straight}^{(j)} \;\leq\; L_{Euclidean}(q_s, g)$$

Each boundary walk for obstacle $i$ traverses at most half the perimeter (worst case when the leave point is diametrically opposite the hit point):

$$L_{boundary}^{(i)} \;\leq\; \frac{p_i}{2}$$

Summing over all $n$ obstacles:

$$\boxed{L_{Bug\,2} \leq L_{Euclidean}(q_s, g) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the three-stage pipeline of the Vector Field Histogram (VFH) method with the polar histogram equation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Cartesian Occupancy Grid (2 marks):** Each range measurement $r_i$ at angle $\theta_i$ is mapped to a Cartesian grid cell. The cell's certainty value $CV(x, y)$ is incremented, modeling a probabilistic occupancy estimate.

**Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction (3 marks):** The Cartesian grid is reduced to a 1D polar histogram $H$ of $n$ angular sectors (typically $n = 72$). For each sector $k$:

$$H(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} CV_i \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{R_{max}}\right)^2 \cdot \cos^2(\alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k})$$

where $M$ is the number of active cells, $d_i$ is the distance to cell $i$, $R_{max}$ is the sensor range, $\alpha_i$ is the cell's angle from the robot, and $\alpha_{0,k}$ is the center angle of sector $k$.

**Stage 3 — Steering Selection (2 marks):** Apply thresholds $\tau_{low}$ and $\tau_{high}$. Identify the largest contiguous *free* gap. Among that gap, choose the sector $k_{target}$ closest to the goal direction. The robot's angular velocity is $\omega_{cmd} = K_p (\theta_{target} - \theta_{current})$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice (Or Option)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Derive the admissible velocity set $V_a$ for the Dynamic Window Approach, and state the final DWA search space $V_s$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the braking kinematic constraint (3 marks):**

A robot decelerating from velocity $v$ at maximum deceleration $\dot{v}_b$ travels a distance:

$$d_{brake} = \frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b}$$

For safety, the robot must be able to stop within the distance $d(v, \omega)$ to the nearest obstacle:

$$\frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b} \leq d(v, \omega) \;\Rightarrow\; v \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b}$$

The rotational analog:

$$\omega \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v, \omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b}$$

**Step 2 — Define $V_a$ explicitly (2 marks):**

$$V_a = \left\{(v, \omega) \,\bigg|\, v \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b} \;\wedge\; \omega \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{\omega}_b}\right\}$$

**Step 3 — Form the final search space (2 marks):**

$$V_s = V_m \cap V_d \cap V_a$$

where $V_m$ is the max-velocity rectangle, $V_d$ is the dynamic-window rectangle, and $V_a$ is the admissible safe set. Hence:

$$V_s = \left\{(v, \omega) \,\bigg|\, \begin{array}{l} v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}] \cap [v_c - \dot{v}_a \Delta t,\, v_c + \dot{v}_a \Delta t] \\ \omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}] \cap [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t,\, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t] \\ v \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b},\; \omega \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{\omega}_b} \end{array}\right\}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Write the DWA objective function and explain each of its three terms. Justify why a smoothing normalization is applied.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the objective (2 marks):**

$$G(v, \omega) = \sigma\left(\alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{clearness}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega)\right)$$

**Step 2 — Explain the three terms (3 marks):**

- **Heading:** $\text{heading}(v, \omega) = 1 - \frac{\vert \theta_{target} - \theta_{predicted} \vert}{\pi}$ — measures how aligned the predicted trajectory is with the goal direction. Maximizing this term drives the robot toward the goal.
- **Clearness:** $\text{clearness}(v, \omega) = \frac{d(v, \omega)}{d_{max}}$ — ratio of the clearance to the nearest obstacle to a maximum reference. Maximizing this term keeps the robot away from obstacles.
- **Velocity:** $\text{velocity}(v, \omega) = \frac{v}{v_{max}}$ — normalized forward speed. Maximizing this encourages fast progress.

**Step 3 — Justify smoothing (2 marks):**

The smoothing $\sigma(\cdot)$ normalizes the weighted sum so that $G$ is bounded in a consistent range (typically $[0, 1]$) regardless of the magnitudes of $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$. This prevents the term with the largest natural scale from dominating the optimization. Common choices for $\sigma$ are $\sigma(x) = \tanh(x)$ or a simple min-max normalization across the sampled $(v, \omega)$ pairs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine-squared weighting in $H(k)$:** Many students write $H(k) = \sum CV_i$ without the angular weighting. **This loses 2 marks** because the smoothing convolution is the *distinguishing* feature of VFH over a naive angular histogram.
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_a$ with $V_d$:** $V_d$ is about *acceleration* limits; $V_a$ is about *safety*. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Stating the path bound without the worst-case reasoning:** Writing $L_{Bug\,2} = L_{Euclidean} + \sum p_i/2$ *without* the geometric argument (why half the perimeter is the upper bound) earns only partial credit. Always include the "diametrically opposite point" justification.
>
> 4. **Omitting the control period $\Delta t$ in the dynamic window:** Many students write the $V_d$ expression with "$\pm \dot{v}_a$" instead of "$\pm \dot{v}_a \Delta t$". The product of acceleration and time is the velocity increment, not acceleration alone.
>
> 5. **DWA objective missing the smoothing $\sigma$:** Writing $G = \alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{clearness} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}$ without the normalization $\sigma(\cdot)$ is a 1-mark deduction. The smoothing is non-trivial and is a frequent board-exam probe.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Bug 1 and Bug 2 in the trace:** Bug 1 walks the *full* perimeter; Bug 2 leaves at the *first* valid alignment. Examiners give 1 mark specifically for the *correct trace behavior*, so getting this wrong is doubly penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

**Bug Algorithms:**
- Bug 1: Walk full perimeter, leave at closest-to-goal point. Bound: $L + \sum p_i$.
- Bug 2: Walk perimeter, leave at first m-line point closer to goal. Bound: $L + \sum p_i/2$.
- Tangent Bug: Bug 2 + range sensor tangent graph. Better in sparse environments.
- All Bug algorithms are *complete* under the reachable-goal assumption.
- Bug algorithms use *only* the contact sensor (Bug 1, Bug 2) or range sensor (Tangent Bug) — no global map.

**Vector Field Histogram (VFH):**
- Three-stage pipeline: scan → grid → polar histogram.
- Polar histogram equation: $H(k) = \sum CV_i (1 - d_i/R_{max})^2 \cos^2(\alpha_i - \alpha_{0,k})$.
- Two thresholds: $\tau_{low}$ (free) and $\tau_{high}$ (blocked).
- Steering: pick largest free gap, choose goal-aligned sector, $P$-control on $\omega$.
- Enhancements: VFH+ (footprint), VFH* (A* look-ahead).

**Dynamic Window Approach (DWA):**
- Search space $V_s = V_m \cap V_d \cap V_a$.
- $V_m$ = max velocity rectangle.
- $V_d$ = dynamic window bounded by acceleration $\dot{v}_a, \dot{\omega}_a$ over $\Delta t$.
- $V_a$ = admissible safe set, bounded by braking distance: $v \leq \sqrt{2 d \dot{v}_b}$.
- Objective: $G = \sigma(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{clearness} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity})$.
- Always *kinodynamically* feasible by construction.

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Don't confuse dynamic window (kinematics) with admissible set (safety).
- Don't forget the $\cos^2$ term in VFH.
- Don't omit the geometric justification for the half-perimeter bound.
- Don't apply Bug algorithms to dynamic environments without modification.
- Don't use DWA with sparse sensors — its clearness metric is unreliable.

**Engineering Mapping:**
- Bug family → search & rescue, planetary rovers, unknown cluttered environments.
- VFH → indoor AGVs, warehouse robots, low-speed mobility aids.
- DWA → autonomous cars, dynamic environments, service robots.

**Cross-Connections to Other Modules:**
- Module 2 (Sensors): VFH and DWA both depend on dense range data.
- Module 3 (Localization): All three algorithms assume the robot knows its own pose.
- Module 5 (Path Planning): Bug is a primitive local planner; VFH/DWA are reactive local planners feeding into global A*/Dijkstra frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Architectures - Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing, Control Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT502 - ROBOTICS AND INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation Architectures - Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing, Control Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Navigation Architecture — Formal KTU Definition

A **Navigation Architecture** in mobile robotics is the structural and algorithmic framework that organizes perception, localization, cognition (path planning), and motion control into a coherent software/hardware stack so that a robot can determine *where it is*, *where it wants to go*, and *how to get there* reliably. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCADT502, Module 4), navigation architectures are studied under two design pillars:

1. **Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing** — a software-engineering discipline for building the architecture.
2. **Control Localization** — the algorithmic core that estimates the robot's pose (position + orientation) over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCADT502 – Module 4):** Navigation architectures, modularity for code reuse and sharing, and control localization are the three prescribed sub-topics. Expect at least one 7-mark sub-part in the End Semester Exam (ESE) on architecture diagrams, and one full 14-mark question on localization (Bayes / Kalman / Particle filter).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot is Like a Delivery Boy with a Smartphone"

Imagine you order food via a delivery app. Three things happen simultaneously:

- **The App (Software Module):** The app is split into clean tabs — *Menu*, *Cart*, *Map*, *Rider Tracking*. Each tab is a *module* that can be updated or replaced without breaking others. If Zomato wants to add a new payment method, they only edit the *Cart* module. This is **modularity for code reuse**.
- **The GPS Dot (Localization):** Your phone constantly asks satellites *"Where am I?"* and combines that with the last known position, speed, and direction. That is **control localization** — recursively estimating your position from noisy sensors.
- **The Route Planner (Navigation Layer):** The app decides the best route. The architecture is the *whole pipeline*: sensors → localization → planner → wheel motors.

A robot is essentially a delivery boy who never gets tired, but needs the same three boxes working together.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Plain Meaning | KTU-Standard Notation |
|------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Pose** | Where the robot is (x, y, θ) | $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ |
| **Belief** | The robot's internal "guess" of its pose | $bel(x_t)$ |
| **Odometry** | Distance traveled measured by wheel encoders | $u_t$ (control input) |
| **Measurement** | Sensor reading (lidar, camera, GPS) | $z_t$ |
| **Map** | Known environment representation | $m$ |
| **CO - Course Outcome** | Mapped learning outcome | CO3, CO4 (typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloom's Level Targeted (KTU 2024):** *Apply* and *Analyze*. Students must be able to draw an architecture diagram, derive the Bayes recursion, and apply it to a 1-D localization example.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing

### 2.1.1 What is "Modularity" in a Robotics Context?

A **module** is a self-contained software unit that exposes a well-defined interface (inputs/outputs) and hides its internal implementation. In robotics, a *good* architecture decomposes the system into modules such as:

- **Perception module** (sensor drivers, feature extraction)
- **Localization module** (filter, map matching)
- **Planning module** (global path planner)
- **Control module** (PID, trajectory follower)
- **Behaviors module** (obstacle avoidance, follow-wall)

The goal is to satisfy two engineering virtues simultaneously:
- **Reusability:** The same localization module should work on a TurtleBot and on a Husky without rewriting.
- **Shareability:** Research labs can publish a planner module and others can drop it into their robot.

### 2.1.2 The Three Canonical Navigation Architectures (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 module explicitly studies three patterns. The following table is the **single most important comparison** to memorize:

| Property | Deliberative (Horizon) | Reactive (Subsumption) | Hybrid (Three-Layer) |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Decision model** | *Sense → Plan → Act* (serial) | *Sense → Act* (reflexive) | *Deliberator + Sequencer + Reactive* |
| **World model** | Full geometric map | None / local | Hybrid (global + local) |
| **Strength** | Optimal global paths | Fast response, robust | Combines strengths |
| **Weakness** | Slow, brittle to sensor noise | No memory, gets stuck in loops | Complex to design |
| **Example** | Stanford Cart (1970s) | Brooks' Subsumption (1986) | RHINO, BART, ROS Navigation Stack |
| **Best analogy** | A human planning a road trip with a paper map | A cockroach dodging your foot | A driver using GPS *and* reflexes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subsumption Architecture (Rodney Brooks, 1986)** is a guaranteed KTU question. Layers of competence (0 = avoid obstacles, 1 = wander, 2 = map, 3 = plan motion, 4 = reason about objects). Higher layers *subsume* (override) lower layers.

### 2.1.3 The Three Software-Modularity Principles (Code Reuse)

When implementing these architectures, KTU expects you to know the following three Object-Oriented / software-engineering principles:

1. **Encapsulation** — A localization class exposes only `getPose()` and `update(measurement)`. Internal matrices of the Kalman filter are hidden.
2. **Inheritance / Polymorphism** — Define an abstract `Sensor` class; `Lidar` and `Camera` inherit and override `read()`.
3. **Standardized Interfaces (Middlewares)** — Use frameworks like **ROS (Robot Operating System)** where modules communicate via *topics* (`/scan`, `/odom`, `/cmd_vel`). This is the modern realization of "code reuse and sharing."

### 2.1.4 Why Modularity? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

- **Parallel development** — Three teams can work on perception, planning, and control simultaneously.
- **Testing** — You can unit-test the localization module with synthetic data without driving the real robot.
- **Portability** — Swap a 2-D lidar for a 3-D lidar by writing one new driver; the planner doesn't change.

---

## 2.2 Control Localization — Theoretical Core

**Localization** is the problem of estimating the robot's pose $x_t$ from:
- A stream of controls $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$ (odometry)
- A stream of measurements $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ (range, vision, GPS)
- A map $m$ (known a priori)

### 2.2.1 The Two Recursive Probabilities You Must Memorize

The entire theoretical edifice of localization rests on two probability densities:

**A. Motion Model (Prediction):** Probability of arriving at $x_t$ after executing control $u_t$ from $x_{t-1}$:

$$p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$$

**B. Measurement Model (Correction):** Probability of observing $z_t$ given the robot is at $x_t$ in map $m$:

$$p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$$

Combining them gives the **Bayes filter recursion**, the master equation of localization.

### 2.2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table of Localization Equations — Memorize Before ESE**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Bayes Theorem | $p(A \vert B) = \dfrac{p(B \vert A) \, p(A)}{p(B)}$ | Inverts observation likelihood to estimate pose |
| 2 | Total Probability | $p(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}) p(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Marginalizes over previous pose |
| 3 | Markov Assumption | $p(x_t \vert x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | The future is independent of the past given the present |
| 4 | Bayes Filter Recursion | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | **Master equation of localization** |
| 5 | Kalman Prediction (Mean) | $\bar{\mu}_t = A \mu_{t-1} + B u_t$ | Propagates Gaussian belief through linear motion |
| 6 | Kalman Prediction (Cov) | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A \Sigma_{t-1} A^T + R$ | Adds process noise $R$ to uncertainty |
| 7 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T (C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q)^{-1}$ | Weights prediction vs. measurement noise $Q$ |
| 8 | Kalman Update (Mean) | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C \bar{\mu}_t)$ | Corrects mean using innovation $z_t - C\bar{\mu}_t$ |
| 9 | Kalman Update (Cov) | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Shrinks covariance (more certainty) |
| 10 | Particle Filter Resampling | $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Reweights particles by sensor likelihood |
| 11 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Triggers resampling when $N_{eff} < N/2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule Reminder:** In all formulas above, conditional bars (such as $\vert$) are rendered using LaTeX `\vert` inside `$$` math blocks, **never** as raw `|` pipe characters, to prevent breaking the markdown table.

### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in Amazon warehouses use Kalman filter localization with wheel odometry + lidar reflectors.
- **Self-driving cars** (Waymo, Tesla) use **Particle Filters / Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** because GPS and lidar posteriors are multi-modal (the car can be in multiple lanes).
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)** use **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** with visual odometry + IMU because the terrain is non-linear.

---

## 2.3 Three Flavors of Localization (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Map Known? | Initial Pose | Algorithm | Use Case |
|------|------------|--------------|-----------|----------|
| **Position Tracking** | Yes | Known | Kalman / EKF | Wheel slip correction |
| **Global Localization** | Yes | Unknown | MCL / Markov | "Kidnapped robot" problem |
| **SLAM** | No (built simultaneously) | Unknown | EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM | Mapping + localization together |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Recursion (The Most Important Derivation in Module 4)

We will derive the master equation of localization in **eight fully explicit steps**, with no skipped algebra.

**Step 1 — Define the belief.** The robot's belief about being at $x_t$ is the posterior density of $x_t$ given all past controls and measurements:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of conditional probability.**

$$bel(x_t) = \dfrac{p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the direct application of $p(A,B) = p(B \vert A) p(A)$ rearranged for the posterior.

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov Assumption (no memory beyond $x_t$).** The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose:

$$p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \vert x_t)$$

*Conversion logic:* A lidar reading at time $t$ tells you nothing about past lidar readings once you know the current pose. This is the **sensor Markov property**.

**Step 4 — Apply the Markov Assumption to the denominator as well.**

$$p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

(We will absorb this as a normalizer $\eta$.)

**Step 5 — Introduce $x_{t-1}$ using the law of total probability in the second factor.**

$$p(x_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

*Conversion logic:* The robot's current pose is reached *via some* previous pose $x_{t-1}$. Sum (integrate) over all possible previous poses.

**Step 6 — Apply Markov again — pose depends only on previous pose and current control.**

$$p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

*Conversion logic:* Given $x_{t-1}$ and the control $u_t$ you just executed, the current pose doesn't depend on history.

**Step 7 — Recognize $p(x_{t-1} \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = bel(x_{t-1})$ (the previous belief).**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \int p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 8 — Replace the normalizer.** Since the denominator $p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ doesn't depend on $x_t$, it is a scalar normalizer:

$$\eta = \dfrac{1}{p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Final Master Equation (write this in your exam verbatim):**

$$\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two factors inside the integral are exactly the **measurement model** (outer multiplicative term) and the **motion model** (inside the integral). Memorize the shape: **predict with motion, then correct with measurement.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Deriving Kalman Filter from Bayes (Linear-Gaussian Specialization)

### Step 1 — Assume all densities are Gaussian and all models are linear.

$$bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$$

$$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

$$z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

### Step 2 — Predict the mean (linearity of expectation).

$$\bar{\mu}_t = E[A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t] = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$$

### Step 3 — Predict the covariance (linearity of variance).

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$$

### Step 4 — Form the innovation (measurement residual).

$$\nu_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$$

### Step 5 — Compute innovation covariance.

$$S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t$$

### Step 6 — Compute Kalman gain (minimizes posterior covariance).

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T S_t^{-1}$$

### Step 7 — Update the mean.

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$$

### Step 8 — Update the covariance.

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If asked to show that Kalman minimizes mean squared error, expand $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$, set $\partial \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)/\partial K_t = 0$, and recover $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T (C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q)^{-1}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization) Algorithm

| Step | Equation / Operation | Description |
|------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{X}_t = \bar{\mathcal{X}}_t = \emptyset$ | Initialize empty sets |
| 2 | For $m = 1$ to $M$ (particles): | |
| 3 | $x_t^{[m]} \sim p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})$ | Sample from motion model |
| 4 | $w_t^{[m]} = p(z_t \vert x_t^{[m]}, m)$ | Importance weight = sensor likelihood |
| 5 | $\bar{\mathcal{X}}_t = \bar{\mathcal{X}}_t \cup \langle x_t^{[m]}, w_t^{[m]} \rangle$ | Store weighted particle |
| 6 | End For | |
| 7 | Compute $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{m=1}^M (w_t^{[m]})^2}$ | Sample-adequacy metric |
| 8 | If $N_{eff} < N/2$: resample with replacement proportional to $w_t^{[m]}$ | Discard low-weight particles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors in the measurement model:** For a 2-D lidar with range $z_t^k$ hitting a wall at expected distance $z_t^{k*}$, the typical likelihood is:
>
> $$p(z_t \vert x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \left[ z_{hit} \, p_{hit}(z_t^k \vert x_t, m) + z_{short} \, p_{short}(z_t^k \vert x_t, m) + z_{max} \, p_{max}(z_t^k \vert x_t, m) + z_{rand} \, p_{rand}(z_t^k) \right]$$
>
> with $z_{hit} + z_{short} + z_{max} + z_{rand} = 1$. **Memorize the four mixture components** — this is a KTU 14-marker favorite.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Linear Gaussian, 1-D)

A robot moves along the x-axis. Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 1$. Control $u_1 = 1$ with $A = 1, B = 1, R = 0.5$. Measurement $z_1 = 2$ with $C = 1, Q = 1$.

**Prediction:**

$$\bar{\mu}_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1) = 1$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1)(1)(1) + 0.5 = 1.5$$

**Update:**

$$S_1 = (1)(1.5)(1) + 1 = 2.5$$

$$K_1 = (1.5)(1) / 2.5 = 0.6$$

$$\mu_1 = 1 + 0.6(2 - 1) = 1.6$$

$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - 0.6)(1.5) = 0.6$$

**Interpretation:** The estimate moved from 1 (predicted) to 1.6 (corrected by the measurement), and uncertainty shrank from 1.5 to 0.6. ✔ Correct Kalman behavior.

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Layer Hybrid Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["DELIBERATIVE LAYER  Global Path Planner"]
        A1[World Model and Map Database]
        A2[A Star Planner Module]
        A3[Dijkstra Planner Module]
    end

    subgraph L2["EXECUTION LAYER  Sequencer and Task Manager"]
        B1[Task Scheduler Module]
        B2[Cost Map Builder]
        B3[Local Goal Assigner]
    end

    subgraph L3["REACTIVE LAYER  Behaviors and Control"]
        C1[Obstacle Avoidance Module]
        C2[Follow Wall Module]
        C3[PID Motion Controller]
    end

    S1[Lidar Sensor Driver] --> B2
    S2[Wheel Odometry] --> C3
    S3[IMU Sensor] --> C3

    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> M[Wheel Motor Actuators]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Top-down data flow. The **deliberative layer** consumes a global map and emits a coarse path. The **execution layer** converts that path into local waypoints. The **reactive layer** turns waypoints into real-time wheel commands. Each box is an *independent software module* — this is the practical realization of **modularity for code reuse and sharing**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Bayes Filter Recursion as a Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Previous Belief bel x minus 1] --> B[Prediction Step]
    U[Control Input u t] --> B
    B --> C[Predicted Belief bar x t]
    Z[Sensor Measurement z t] --> D[Correction Step]
    M[Map m] --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E[Normalize by eta]
    E --> F[Current Belief bel x t]
    F --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU exam diagram** for any localization question. If you redraw this from memory in the exam, you are guaranteed partial credit even before writing any equation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Modularity via ROS-style Topics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerMod["PERCEPTION MODULE"]
        P1[Camera Driver]
        P2[Lidar Driver]
    end

    subgraph LocMod["LOCALIZATION MODULE"]
        L1[Particle Filter Node]
        L2[Kalman Filter Node]
    end

    subgraph PlanMod["PLANNING MODULE"]
        Q1[Global Planner Node]
        Q2[Local Planner Node]
    end

    subgraph CtrlMod["CONTROL MODULE"]
        R1[Velocity Smoother]
        R2[PID Controller]
    end

    P1 -- /camera/image --> L1
    P2 -- /scan --> L1
    L1 -- /pose --> Q1
    L2 -- /odom --> Q2
    Q1 -- /global_path --> Q2
    Q2 -- /local_path --> R1
    R1 -- /cmd_vel --> R2
    R2 --> A[Robot Actuators]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each box is a **separate ROS node** that can be developed, tested, and deployed by a different team. The *topic names* (e.g., `/scan`, `/cmd_vel`) are the standardized interface — exactly the role of abstract base classes in C++/Python OOP. This satisfies all three modularity principles (encapsulation, polymorphism, standardized interfaces).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of EKF Localization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Start EKF Cycle]) --> P1[Predict Pose using Motion Model]
    P1 --> P2[Linearize Motion Model as Jacobian A]
    P2 --> P3[Propagate Covariance as bar Sigma equals A Sigma A transpose plus R]
    P3 --> U1[Acquire Sensor Measurement z t]
    U1 --> U2[Linearize Measurement Model as Jacobian C]
    U2 --> U3[Compute Kalman Gain K equals bar Sigma C transpose S inverse]
    U3 --> U4[Update Mean mu equals bar mu plus K innovation]
    U4 --> U5[Update Covariance Sigma equals I minus K C bar Sigma]
    U5 --> Out([Publish Pose Estimate])
    Out --> Loop[Return to Predict at next time step]
```
