# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Aligned with KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part A (Answer any 5 out of 7, each 3 marks) and Part B (Choice-based, each 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). All questions below are mapped to Module 4 outcomes.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question Paper, PCADT502)]**
*"With a neat block diagram, explain the three-layer hybrid navigation architecture used in mobile robots."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A three-layer (hybrid) architecture combines the global reasoning of a **deliberative layer** (top), the task scheduling of an **execution/sequencer layer** (middle), and the fast reflexes of a **reactive layer** (bottom). The deliberative layer holds the world model and runs global planners (A*, Dijkstra). The sequencer converts the global path into local waypoints and tracks progress. The reactive layer hosts low-level behaviors — obstacle avoidance, follow-wall — and a PID motion controller that issues wheel commands. *(1 mark for each correct layer explanation, 1 mark for the diagram.)* 

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the three-layer architecture with the *three levels of competence* in Brooks' subsumption. Three-layer = (deliberative, sequencer, reactive). Subsumption = (avoid, wander, map, plan motion, reason). Mixing them up costs full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Expected Pattern, PCADT502)]**
*"Define the Bayes filter recursion for mobile robot localization. State the Markov assumption used in its derivation."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bayes filter estimates the posterior belief over the robot's pose:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Markov assumption:** Given the current state $x_t$, the future is conditionally independent of the past. Thus $p(x_t \vert x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and $p(z_t \vert x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \vert x_t)$. *(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for $bel$ definition, 1 mark for Markov.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Choice-Based Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper, Module 4, Set A)]**
**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **Subsumption Architecture** proposed by Rodney Brooks. With a neat diagram, describe its layered competence and the principle of *inhibition* (subsumption). Compare it with the **deliberative architecture** on the parameters of world model, response time, and robustness.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the three software-engineering principles — **encapsulation, polymorphism, and standardized interfaces** — that enable *modularity for code reuse and sharing* in robotics. Give a concrete example of how the **ROS (Robot Operating System)** realizes these principles in a mobile robot navigation stack.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Subsumption Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Origin (1 mark):** Proposed by Rodney Brooks (MIT, 1986) in *"A Robust Layered Control System for a Mobile Robot."*
2. **Layered Competence (3 marks):** Five layers, lowest to highest:
   - Layer 0: *Avoid* — obstacle avoidance (highest priority reflex).
   - Layer 1: *Wander* — random exploration.
   - Layer 2: *Map* — build an incremental map.
   - Layer 3: *Plan motion* — short-horizon path planning.
   - Layer 4: *Reason about objects* — high-level task reasoning.
3. **Inhibition Principle (1 mark):** Higher layers can *subsume* (inhibit / override) the output of lower layers via suppressor nodes. This avoids a central planner.
4. **Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Deliberative | Subsumption |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| World model | Full a-priori map | None (reactive) |
| Response time | Slow (global replanning) | Fast (reflexive) |
| Robustness | Brittle to model errors | High (no model dependency) |
| Best for | Static, structured envs | Dynamic, unknown envs |

---

**(b) Modularity Principles (7 marks):**

1. **Encapsulation (1.5 marks):** Each module exposes only a *public interface*. Example: `LidarDriver::getRangeScan()` hides the underlying USB protocol. Students lose marks if they confuse encapsulation with data hiding *only* — it is also about clean API design.
2. **Polymorphism / Inheritance (1.5 marks):** Define an abstract `Sensor` class with a virtual `read()` method. `Lidar`, `Camera`, and `IMU` derive from `Sensor` and override `read()`. The planner can call `sensor->read()` regardless of the actual type.
3. **Standardized Interfaces (1.5 marks):** ROS topics and services. E.g., `/scan` (sensor_msgs/LaserScan) is the *standard* message type; any node subscribing to it works with any publisher. This is the modern equivalent of a published software interface contract.
4. **ROS Navigation Stack Example (2.5 marks):** ROS 2 Navigation2 stack contains `nav2_planner` (global), `nav2_controller` (local), `nav2_bt_navigator` (behavior tree sequencer), and `amcl` (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization). Each is a separate package maintained by different developers; the *standardized topic interface* (`/cmd_vel`, `/map`, `/amcl_pose`) lets them be swapped independently — perfect code reuse.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Question A):** Many students write only the *names* of ROS nodes without explaining *how* encapsulation/polymorphism/standardization is realized. Marks are awarded for the **mapping** from principle → mechanism → example. A bullet list of ROS nodes alone gets at most 2 of the 7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Localization Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Actual-style, Module 4, Set B)]**
**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the **Bayes filter recursion** for robot localization from first principles. Clearly state the **Markov assumption** at each step of the derivation.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For a linear-Gaussian system with state $x_t$, control $u_t$, and measurement $z_t$ given by:
$$x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.5)$$
$$z_t = x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1.0)$$
Starting with $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 1$, and receiving $u_1 = 1$, $z_1 = 2$, compute the **Kalman filter** prediction and update steps. Show explicitly the reduction in covariance after the measurement update.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Bayes Filter Derivation (7 marks):**

Present the eight-step derivation from **Section 3.1** of these notes. Allocation:

- 1 mark for writing the posterior $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- 1 mark for conditional-probability expansion.
- 1 mark for first Markov application (sensor).
- 1 mark for law of total probability and second Markov application (motion).
- 1 mark for recognizing $bel(x_{t-1})$.
- 1 mark for the final boxed equation.
- 1 mark for explicitly naming the *normalizer* $\eta = 1/p(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$.

---

**(b) Kalman Filter Numerical (7 marks):**

From the worked example in **Section 3.4**, the solution is reproduced:

**Prediction step (2 marks):**
- Motion model coefficients: $A = 1, B = 1, R = 0.5$.
- $\bar{\mu}_1 = A \mu_0 + B u_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1) = 1$.
- $\bar{\Sigma}_1 = A \Sigma_0 A^T + R = (1)(1)(1) + 0.5 = 1.5$.

**Innovation & Gain (2 marks):**
- $C = 1$, $Q = 1.0$.
- $S_1 = C \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^T + Q = (1)(1.5)(1) + 1.0 = 2.5$.
- $K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 C^T S_1^{-1} = 1.5 \times 1 \div 2.5 = 0.6$.

**Update (2 marks):**
- $\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 (z_1 - C \bar{\mu}_1) = 1 + 0.6 (2 - 1) = 1.6$.
- $\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 C) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - 0.6)(1.5) = 0.6$.

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The covariance reduced from 1.5 → 0.6 (a 60% reduction in uncertainty). The mean shifted from the prior prediction of 1.0 toward the measurement 2.0, ending at 1.6, weighted by the Kalman gain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Sign error** in innovation $z_t - C\bar{\mu}_t$ (students often invert it and write $\bar{\mu}_t - z_t$). This flips the entire correction.
> - **Forgetting to add process noise $R$** to the predicted covariance. Without it, $\Sigma$ would shrink monotonically to zero — a mathematically impossible "perfectly known" robot.
> - **Confusing $R$ and $Q$:** $R$ is *process* (motion) noise; $Q$ is *measurement* (sensor) noise. Reversing them gives a robot that trusts its wheels more than its lidar — dangerous in practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it 30 minutes before walking into the ESE hall.**

### ✅ Architecture & Modularity
- Three canonical navigation architectures: **Deliberative, Reactive (Subsumption), Hybrid (Three-Layer)**.
- Brooks' **five layers of competence**: avoid → wander → map → plan motion → reason.
- Three modularity principles: **encapsulation, polymorphism, standardized interfaces**.
- Modern realization = **ROS / ROS 2 nodes** communicating over **topics** and **services**.

### ✅ Localization Theory
- Master equation: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- **Two models** required: motion model $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ and measurement model $p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$.
- **Markov assumption** = present state summarizes all relevant history.

### ✅ Kalman Filter (Linear-Gaussian)
- Prediction: $\bar{\mu}_t = A \mu_{t-1} + B u_t$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A \Sigma_{t-1} A^T + R$.
- Update: $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T (C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q)^{-1}$, $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C\bar{\mu}_t)$, $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C)\bar{\Sigma}_t$.
- $R$ = process noise, $Q$ = measurement noise. **Do not mix them up.**

### ✅ Particle Filter (MCL)
- Sample from motion model, weight by measurement model, resample when $N_{eff} < N/2$.
- Handles **multi-modal beliefs** (kidnapped robot problem, global localization).
- Lidar measurement model = mixture of **hit, short, max, random** components.

### ✅ Localization Flavors
- **Position tracking:** known initial pose, Kalman / EKF.
- **Global localization:** unknown initial pose, MCL.
- **SLAM:** map unknown, build it while localizing (EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM).

### ✅ Common Examiner Traps
- Mixing **process noise $R$** and **measurement noise $Q$**.
- Forgetting the **normalizer $\eta$** in Bayes filter.
- Confusing **subsumption layers** with **three-layer architecture**.
- Writing the **innovation** as $\bar{\mu}_t - z_t$ instead of $z_t - C\bar{\mu}_t$.

---
